Vertical spacing between elements inside the panel is modest (8–16px) to keep it functional yet not cramped. A 24–32px gap below the panel separates it from the seller-info-panel underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level module at the top of the right sidebar; scrolls with the page and maintains its position relative to the main content column without sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bid status indicator is nested visually inside the bidding panel, positioned immediately below the panel’s title row (where the heading and watchlist toggle sit). It is left-aligned with the bidding heading and spans most of the panel’s width but occupies only a single line or two of text. It may show the user’s current max bid, leading bid, or whether they’ve been outbid. There is a small vertical gap of about 8px between this status text and the form fields below, clearly separating read-only information from input controls while keeping them perceived as a single functional block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the bidding-panel container; it does not alter the sidebar grid and simply extends the panel’s height as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The seller info panel appears in the right sidebar directly below the bidding-panel, separated by a vertical gap of 24–32px. It stretches the full width of the sidebar and uses 16–20px internal padding, with an optional light gray top border or subtle heading to distinguish it. Key seller details (name, location, contact preference, rating if applicable) are arranged in a short, vertically stacked list—either simple label–value lines or a few concise paragraphs. The panel’s height remains relatively compact so that seller information is visible without excessive scrolling once the user reaches the top of the sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element stacking in the sidebar flow; it grows vertically with content but maintains its position between the bidding panel and the seller-message-form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The seller message form sits immediately beneath the seller-info-panel in the right sidebar, separated by a vertical gap of about 24px. It occupies the sidebar’s full width and has 16–24px internal padding, similar to the bidding panel for consistency. A short, left-aligned heading (e.g., “Contact Seller”) appears at the top, followed by a vertically stacked set of fields: a single-line subject (optional), a multi-line message textarea, and a submit button. Each field spans nearly the full width of the sidebar with 8–12px spacing between them. The form’s overall height is moderate, allowing the textarea enough depth for comfortable typing without dominating the sidebar. A 24–32px gap below this form separates it from any owner-only actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element that scrolls with the sidebar content, positioned between seller-info-panel and own-listing-actions where applicable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The owner listing actions panel is placed at the bottom of the right sidebar, appearing only when the current user is the seller of the lot. It is stacked below the seller-message-form with a 24–32px vertical gap, or directly below the seller-info-panel if the messaging form is not shown. The panel spans the full sidebar width, with 16–20px of internal padding and a minimal heading such as “Manage Listing.” Within the panel, actions like edit listing, close auction, or relist are presented as a short vertical list of links or compact buttons, each on its own line with 8–12px spacing. Its visual weight is lighter than the bidding panel (fewer borders, simple text buttons) so it reads as a utility area reserved for the seller rather than a primary CTA for buyers.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level module at the bottom of the sidebar’s content stack; appears conditionally and simply increases the sidebar’s scrollable height when present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lot description section is positioned in the left column below the lot-attributes-panel, separated by a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It spans the full width of the left column, flush with its left and right boundaries. A subtle section heading (e.g., “Description”) may appear at the top, left-aligned, with the main body text flowing beneath as one or more paragraphs. Paragraphs are set in a readable line length with comfortable line-height and about 12–16px spacing between each paragraph. The section can extend significantly downward if the description is long, forming the bulk of the scrollable content on the left side. There is no heavy container; the section ends either with a slight increase in bottom padding or a light gray divider before any further content or the page footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block in the left column that grows vertically with content and forms the lower portion of the main reading flow; scrolls naturally with the page.

name: Create Listing
title: Create Listing
description: Page for sellers to create a new livestock auction listing by entering lot details, pricing, auction timing, and location, then publishing it as an active or scheduled auction lot.

core_features: Initialize the Create Listing form using getCreateListingFormOptions, loading categories, state options, price unit options, default auction format, and default closing time.
Optionally pre-select the listing category based on the incoming URL parameter categoryId from auctions.html.

Provide a structured form to capture required AuctionLot fields such as title, category, head count, price unit, starting bid amount, auction format, location state, location city, and closing date/time, plus optional details like breed and description.
Configure auction timing by letting the user set closing date and time, combining them into a closing_datetime value for createAuctionListing.
Configure pricing by allowing selection of price unit and capturing starting bid amount and optional reserve price amount, mapped to createAuctionListing.

Allow entry of descriptive animal details (including management notes and age/breeding status text) and selection of optional media files, with media events exposed for integration with an upload system outside this page’s interfaces.
Validate required fields and basic business rules on the client before invoking createAuctionListing, surfacing clear inline error messages.
Create and publish the new auction listing by calling createAuctionListing with the form data, then redirecting to lot_detail.html with the new lotId on success.
Provide navigation back to my_listings.html so users can abandon creation or review existing listings.
Populate category-based navigation for the page header using getAuctionCategories so users can navigate to other category views if needed.
user_workflows: Accessing the Create Listing page: 1) User clicks the 'Sell Livestock' link in the global header or another 'Create Listing' call-to-action. 2) Browser navigates to listing_create.html, possibly with a categoryId URL parameter. 3) On load, the page calls getCreateListingFormOptions and getAuctionCategories. 4) When form options are loaded, the page initializes the form fields, applying defaultAuctionFormat and defaultClosingTimeLocal, and pre-selecting category if a valid categoryId is present in the URL.

Pre-selecting a category from auctions.html: 1) User is browsing auctions.html filtered by a particular category and clicks a 'Create Listing' control. 2) Browser opens listing_create.html with ?categoryId=<category-id>. 3) The Create Listing page parses categoryId from the URL. 4) After getCreateListingFormOptions (or getAuctionCategories) returns, the page checks whether categoryId matches a returned category. 5) If it matches, the category field in the form is pre-set to that category; if not, the form leaves the category unselected and does not error.

Completing the listing form and publishing: 1) User fills in the listing title. 2) User selects a category (or leaves the pre-selected category) from the options returned by getCreateListingFormOptions. 3) User optionally selects or enters breed information. 4) User enters the head count as a numeric value. 5) User selects location state and enters location city from the options and inputs in the form. 6) User selects auction format (using defaultAuctionFormat as the initial selection). 7) User selects a price unit from the priceUnits provided by getCreateListingFormOptions. 8) User enters a starting bid amount in the chosen price unit. 9) User optionally enters a reserve price amount in the same price unit. 10) User selects a closing date and closing time (pre-filled with defaultClosingTimeLocal if applicable). 11) User enters descriptive text for the lot in the description field. 12) User optionally selects media files via the media control (files are stored in in-page state and/or emitted for later upload handling). 13) User clicks the 'Publish Listing' button. 14) The page runs client-side validation; if errors are found, messages are shown and submission is blocked. 15) If validation passes, the page calls createAuctionListing with the mapped form values. 16) While waiting, the submit button is disabled and a submitting state is shown. 17) If createAuctionListing returns success=true, the page reads lotId from the response and redirects to lot_detail.html?lotId=<lotId>. 18) If success=false or the call fails, the page displays the returned message and keeps the user on the form.

Handling validation errors before submission: 1) User clicks 'Publish Listing' with missing or invalid required fields (e.g., empty title, missing category, or non-positive head count). 2) Form validation runs on the client. 3) For each invalid field, the corresponding input is marked with an error state and an error message is shown in the validation error display component. 4) The page does not call createAuctionListing. 5) User updates the fields and retries submission until validation passes.

Navigating back to My Listings: 1) User decides not to create a new listing or wants to review existing lots. 2) User clicks the 'Back to My Listings' link/button on the page. 3) Browser navigates to my_listings.html with no additional parameters. 4) Any unsaved changes in the form are abandoned unless handled by higher-level navigation guards outside this specification.

Using header category navigation while on Create Listing: 1) Header component calls getAuctionCategories on page load and renders navigational links based on returned categories. 2) User clicks a category link in the header (e.g., to go view listings in that category instead of creating one). 3) Browser navigates away from listing_create.html to the relevant category view (implementation of that page is outside this spec).
interactions: On page load, a data-loader interaction triggers getCreateListingFormOptions and getAuctionCategories and enters a loading state until both responses are received or an error occurs.

When getCreateListingFormOptions resolves, the form component binds its category dropdown, state dropdown, price unit options, default auction format, and default closing time from the returned data. The form enters an enabled state.
When URL parameter categoryId is present, a state-manager interaction attempts to match it to one of the loaded category IDs; if matched, it sets the form’s selected category to that value.
When the user changes the category field, the form’s internal state for categoryId is updated, and any previous validation error for category is cleared.
When the user types in the title, breed, head count, city, starting bid amount, reserve price amount, or description, the form’s corresponding state values are updated on each change or blur event.

When the user selects the auction format, the form updates auctionFormat in its state; this may adjust helper text or validation rules if needed (for example, still requiring a closing date/time for online timed formats).
When the user selects a state from the dropdown, the form updates locationState and clears any validation error for locationState.

When the user selects a price unit, the form updates priceUnit and clears any related validation errors. The selected price unit is used to interpret startingBidAmount and reservePriceAmount for the createAuctionListing request.

When the user selects a closing date and closing time, the form updates closingDate and closingTime fields in state. These are later combined into a closing_datetime string when calling createAuctionListing.

When the user selects media files, the media component stores the selected files in its internal state and emits an event to the parent form component indicating the current file list; this page does not directly call any media upload interface.
When the user clicks 'Publish Listing', the form prevents default submission, runs client-side validation, and either shows validation errors or, if valid, triggers the createAuctionListing call.

While createAuctionListing is in progress, the form sets an isSubmitting flag, disables the submit button and possibly other inputs to prevent duplicate submissions, and may show a generic loading indicator.
When createAuctionListing returns success=true, a submit-handler interaction reads lotId from the response and programmatically navigates to lot_detail.html?lotId=<lotId>.

When createAuctionListing returns success=false or the call errors, the form clears isSubmitting, surfaces the returned message in an error banner or inline message, and leaves all user-entered values intact.
When the user clicks the 'Back to My Listings' link or button, a navigation interaction changes the browser location to my_listings.html without making any API calls.
When header category navigation links are clicked, navigation interactions change the browser location to the respective category browsing pages (not defined here), leaving the Create Listing page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is the URL parameter categoryId and the internal form state derived from API responses and user input. On initial load, the page parses the current URL search parameters. If a categoryId parameter is present, it is stored as initialCategoryId. The page then calls getCreateListingFormOptions to retrieve categories, state options, price units, defaultAuctionFormat, and defaultClosingTimeLocal, and also calls getAuctionCategories to populate header navigation. Once categories are available, the page compares initialCategoryId to the list of known category IDs; if a match is found, the form's categoryId field is initialized to that value. If no match is found or categoryId is absent, the categoryId field remains unset and the user must choose one. The page maintains internal state variables for all form fields (title, categoryId, breed, headCount, locationState, locationCity, auctionFormat, priceUnit, startingBidAmount, reservePriceAmount, closingDate, closingTime, description, and selected media), as well as status flags such as isLoadingOptions, optionsLoadError, isSubmitting, and validationErrors. No state is persisted in localStorage; all state exists in memory while the page is loaded. When the user submits the form, the state_logic combines closingDate and closingTime into a single closing datetime representation suitable for passing as closingDate and closingTime parameters to createAuctionListing (with the backend responsible for building the final closing_datetime). After a successful createAuctionListing response, the page reads lotId from the response and updates browser location to lot_detail.html?lotId=<lotId>, effectively transitioning to the lot_detail page state. If the user navigates away via header navigation or the 'Back to My Listings' link, the current in-memory form state is discarded, and any next visit to listing_create.html will re-run initialization and re-read URL parameters.

components.functionality: On page load, fetches all options required to render the Create Listing form and header navigation by calling getCreateListingFormOptions and getAuctionCategories, manages loading and error states, and passes the loaded data to child components.
Reads the categoryId URL parameter from listing_create.html and, after categories are loaded, applies it as the default selected category in the Create Listing form if it matches a known category.

Main form component for capturing auction lot details including title, category, breed, head count, location, auction format, price unit, starting bid, reserve price, closing date and time, description, and media selection. Maintains internal form state, performs client-side validation, and triggers the API submission handler.

Implements client-side validation rules for the Create Listing form such as required field checks (title, category, head count, locationState, locationCity, auctionFormat, priceUnit, startingBidAmount, closingDate, closingTime), numeric and format constraints, and cross-field checks, updating validationErrors state and preventing submission when invalid.

Encapsulates pricing-related inputs including price unit selection (bound to priceUnits from getCreateListingFormOptions), starting bid amount, and optional reserve price amount, and ensures these values are ready for submission to createAuctionListing.

Handles auction timing fields, using defaultClosingTimeLocal as the initial value for the closing time and capturing user-selected closing date and time, which are later combined into a closing datetime for createAuctionListing.
Provides inputs for location_state and location_city using state options from getCreateListingFormOptions and user text input, updating form state for use in createAuctionListing.

Provides the main description text area for the lot, where the seller can enter details such as age, breeding status, and management notes, and binds the text to the AuctionLot.description field for submission.

Allows users to select optional media files (e.g., images) associated with the lot, stores selected files in component state, and emits events to the form component. Actual upload handling is delegated to higher-level logic outside the assigned interfaces.

Orchestrates the listing creation process when the user submits the form: receives validated form data, maps it to the parameters required by createAuctionListing (including separating closingDate and closingTime from the combined closing datetime concept), calls createAuctionListing, and handles success and error responses including navigation to lot_detail.html on success.
Displays field-level and form-level validation errors produced by the form-validation-handler, linking messages to specific inputs and updating in real time as the user corrects issues.
Shows non-validation errors returned from createAuctionListing (e.g., failure messages) or from getCreateListingFormOptions, allowing the user to see why an operation failed and retry if appropriate.

Renders global header navigation including category-based links using categories loaded from getAuctionCategories and a link to the Create Listing page, enabling users to navigate to other parts of the site from this page.
Provides a simple navigation control that routes the user from listing_create.html to my_listings.html when clicked, without calling any APIs.

Interactive control within the Create Listing form that triggers form validation and, if valid, the publish-listing-submit-handler; it reflects isSubmitting state by disabling itself while a createAuctionListing call is in progress.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column grid likely based on a 12-column system, with main content occupying approximately 8–9 columns and the right sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same classic blog-style layout as the mockup: a full-width, slim header at the very top, and below it a two-column content area. The left column holds the primary Create Listing form content in a single, top-to-bottom flow with clearly separated sections (description, media, pricing, timing, location, submit). The right column acts as a slim sidebar hosting secondary navigation (the Back to My Listings link) and remains mostly open/white to preserve the minimalist, utility feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same classic blog-style layout as the mockup: a full-width, slim header at the very top, and below it a two-column content area. The left column holds the primary Create Listing form content in a single, top-to-bottom flow with clearly separated sections (description, media, pricing, timing, location, submit). The right column acts as a slim sidebar hosting secondary navigation (the Back to My Listings link) and remains mostly open/white to preserve the minimalist, utility feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header-navigation occupies a narrow horizontal band (about 56–72px tall) across the full viewport width. Below, the main content area is centered with maximum width around 1100–1200px. Within this, the left main column takes roughly 65–70% of the width (about 8–9 grid columns) and the right sidebar takes 30–35% (about 3–4 columns). Vertical spacing between major form sections is 32–40px, and within each section, controls use 16–24px padding. The sidebar link sits near the top of the sidebar with generous top and bottom space so it does not compete with the main form.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately low density: clear line spacing, 16–24px padding inside form blocks, and 24–32px vertical gaps between groups of fields. Labels and inputs are stacked vertically, not crammed side-by-side. Error banners and validation messages have enough padding and margin (16–24px) to be clearly distinguishable without feeling heavy. The sidebar has sparse content, reinforcing that the form is the primary focus.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the top-left with the site logo and primary navigation in the header, then moves across to any right-aligned header elements. The user’s eye then drops to the main form title and top of the Create Listing form in the left column. From there, the eye moves down sequentially through each form section. The subtle vertical separation between the left content column and the right sidebar, plus whitespace, then pulls attention to the right where the Back to My Listings link appears near the top of the sidebar. On errors, the API error banner near the top of the main column briefly becomes the first stop in the flow, followed by scanning down to inline validation errors around individual inputs.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, minimalist form-focused screen. A slim, full-width header bar runs across the very top with navigation aligned left-to-right, echoing a modern WordPress-style theme. Below, the content is centered in a two-column layout: a wider left column containing the Create Listing form and a narrower right sidebar offering a single Back to My Listings link as a secondary navigation element. The left column begins close under the header with the page title and a stacked sequence of form sections—lot description, media, pricing, auction timing, location, and finally the submit controls—each separated by consistent vertical space and subtle horizontal rules or whitespace rather than heavy boxes. Error feedback appears in a light horizontal banner just above the form, and more granular validation messages live near the corresponding inputs, maintaining a clear reading path. The right column is visually quieter, with the back link placed near the top and ample white space below, maintaining the sense of a straightforward, content-first layout where the main form dominates attention while the sidebar provides light, supportive navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init-loader is invisible by default and only appears as a semi-transparent overlay centered over the main content area (both left form column and right sidebar) once the page starts loading initial data. When active, it covers the entire content region below the header-navigation, stretching from the left edge of the main content container to the right edge, and from just under the header down to at least the first viewport height. A subtle centered spinner or text indicator is placed in the vertical and horizontal center of this overlay. The header-navigation remains visible above it, preserving the overall layout structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-content overlay positioned over the main two-column area, fixed relative to the content container (not the whole window), blocking interaction with underlying form elements while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-context-applier has no visual representation and therefore takes up no explicit space in the layout. Functionally it operates behind the scenes, affecting field defaults and available options within the left-column Create Listing form, but it does not alter the visual arrangement or spacing of components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it does not participate in layout and exerts no spatial behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The create-listing-form sits in the main left column directly below any page title or form heading (which would align with the top-left of the content area). It begins approximately 32px–40px below the bottom edge of the header-navigation, aligned flush with the left margin of the main content container. The form spans about 65–70% of the content width, leaving a clear gutter (24–32px) between its right edge and the vertical boundary that separates it visually from the right sidebar. Inside, the form content is arranged in a vertical stack of distinct sections—lot-description-section, media-selector, pricing-section, auction-timing-section, location-section, and finally the submit controls—each separated by 32px–40px vertical space. Each section uses a light content card feeling: no heavy borders, just 16–24px internal padding and possibly subtle horizontal dividers or headings to distinguish sections. The create-listing-form dictates the main vertical rhythm of the page, extending downward and driving scroll behavior.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element occupying the main left column; scrolls naturally with the page as content grows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form-validation-handler is purely logical and has no direct visual footprint. Its effect is visible through the placement and state of the validation-error-display components around the inputs inside the create-listing-form, but as a unit it does not occupy any dedicated area or alter the spacing of sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no spatial behavior, only influences the state of visible feedback elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the create-listing-form, the pricing-section appears in the left column as one of the main stacked blocks, typically placed after the lot-description-section and media-selector. It spans the same width as the form (85–90% of the left column, respecting inner padding) and begins approximately 32px below the section above it. A section heading such as “Pricing” sits at the top-left of this block, with 8–16px of space below it before the first input row. Inputs such as base price, price type, and any increments are arranged in simple vertical or two-column rows, but always within a clear vertical grid, with 16–24px between each input group. The section’s right edge aligns visually with the right edge of other form sections, maintaining a consistent column line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section inside the form; it expands vertically as more pricing fields are added but maintains fixed horizontal alignment with other form sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The auction-timing-section is another major block within the create-listing-form, placed directly below the pricing-section. It starts around 32px–40px beneath the bottom-most element of the pricing-section, maintaining the consistent vertical spacing used throughout the form. It spans the same horizontal width as the pricing-section, aligned to the left column. At its top-left, a section heading like “Auction Timing” appears, followed by date and time fields stacked vertically. Each row (e.g., start date/time, end date/time) uses 16–24px vertical spacing between labels and inputs, preserving the low-density, readable feel. The entire block reads as a single content unit, subtly separated from adjacent sections via whitespace rather than heavy borders.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block nested in the main form column; it scrolls with the rest of the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location-section sits toward the lower part of the create-listing-form in the left column, typically appearing after the auction-timing-section. It begins approximately 32px–40px below the preceding section and spans the same width, aligning its left and right edges with the rest of the form’s sections. A section heading at the top-left is followed by inputs for ranch name, city, state, and any pickup or delivery details, arranged vertically with 16–24px between fields. If any fields are shown side-by-side (e.g., city and state), they remain within the same overall section width and respect the base grid, ensuring a clean, straight right edge and comfortable inner padding of 16–24px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the form’s left column, growing vertically as needed and scrolling with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lot-description-section is the first substantive block inside the create-listing-form in the left column, immediately under the form’s title or heading. It starts roughly 16–24px below the form heading, spanning the full working width of the form. The section includes inputs like lot title, category, head count, weights, and a multi-line description, organized in a top-to-bottom sequence. Labels are placed above their corresponding inputs with about 4–8px spacing, and 16–24px vertical separation between different fields. This section aligns to the same left and right boundaries as all other form sections, and it sets the visual rhythm and typographic scale for the rest of the form content. Any inline help text appears in smaller, lighter text directly below the relevant input but within the same section, without breaking the clean vertical alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, top-most form section in the main left column; it anchors the start of the form’s scrolling content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The media-selector appears in the left column inside the create-listing-form as its own block, typically positioned directly below the lot-description-section and above the pricing-section. It starts about 32px beneath the last description field and maintains the same left and right boundaries as the rest of the form. A simple heading or label such as “Photos / Media” sits at the top-left, followed by either a drag-and-drop area or upload button aligned to the left. The media area has 16–24px internal padding and may have a light gray border or background to make it identifiable without feeling heavy. Any thumbnail previews line up beneath or beside the upload control, keeping to one or two columns of thumbnails to avoid clutter. Vertical spacing between media controls and neighboring sections remains consistent with the 32px–40px section spacing used in the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the form; as more media items are added, the section extends downward but maintains its width and aligns with other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The publish-listing-submit-handler has no direct visual element. Its influence is reflected in the behavior of the submit-button and subsequent feedback elements such as the api-error-banner and any success states. It does not occupy space in the layout and does not introduce any new alignment or spacing requirements on its own.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it does not affect spatial arrangement, only controls the flow after submit.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation-error-display appears in two related ways within the left column’s create-listing-form. At the field level, small error messages are placed directly beneath the corresponding input, aligned to the left edge of the input field. Each inline error line uses a compact height (approximately one line of text with 4–8px top margin from the input and 8–12px bottom margin before the next element), preserving the vertical rhythm while clearly separating the error text from labels and other content. At the form level, if a summary error is used, it appears as a narrow horizontal block at the top of the form content, just below the page title or form heading and above the lot-description-section. This summary spans the full width of the form column, with 12–16px padding and 16–24px margin below it to keep it distinct yet integrated with the main form content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with form contents; the summary error behaves like a small banner within the left column, while field-level errors expand the vertical height of individual fields but do not change horizontal layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The api-error-banner is positioned in the main left column, just beneath the header-navigation and above the top of the create-listing-form’s main fields. It spans the full width of the content column (aligned with the left and right edges of the form), appearing about 16–24px below the header. The banner has a clean rectangular appearance with 12–16px internal padding and a light background or border color to distinguish it while remaining consistent with the minimalist aesthetic. There is 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the banner and the top of the form’s first section so that it does not visually merge with form fields. When not active, it is completely absent, allowing the form to move upward into its space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the left column; it pushes the form content down while visible and collapses entirely when hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation occupies a slim, full-width bar anchored at the very top of the page. It stretches from the left to the right edge of the viewport and has a height of roughly 56–72px, with 16–24px of vertical padding inside. The logo or site name aligns flush to the left within the header’s content container, primary navigation links are centered horizontally or slightly left-of-center in a single row, and a search icon or secondary action sits on the far right. The header’s background is white or very light gray, matching the page background, while a subtle bottom border or shadow (1px light gray) distinguishes it from the content area below. All main page content, including the two-column form layout, begins immediately below this header with about 24–32px of vertical gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document by default; it does not scroll with the page content (no sticky behavior assumed), so as the user scrolls down, the header moves off-screen.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-my-listings-link is placed at the top of the right-hand sidebar, echoing the mockup’s sidebar list but reduced to a single prominent link. The sidebar itself begins at the same vertical level as the create-listing-form’s title in the left column. The link sits flush with the left margin of the sidebar, approximately 32–40px below the header-navigation, and aligned horizontally with the top of the form’s first section. It is presented as a simple text link (optionally with a small left-pointing arrow icon) that spans only the intrinsic width of its text, not the full sidebar width, keeping the visual weight light. Below this link, at least 32–48px of vertical white space is left before any potential future items, preserving the minimalist, low-density feel and making it clear that this is a secondary navigation control.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the right sidebar; remains aligned with the main content column and scrolls with the page when content exceeds the viewport height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit-button is located at the bottom of the create-listing-form in the left column, forming the visual endpoint of the form’s vertical flow. It is placed approximately 24–32px below the last input field in the location-section or any final form element. The button aligns to the right edge of the form’s inner content area, creating a clear action focus on the bottom-right while leaving some whitespace to its left. If secondary actions (e.g., save draft) exist, they would appear to the left of this primary button, but the primary submit-button remains the rightmost element. Vertically, there is about 32–40px of whitespace below the button before the start of the footer or the end of the content, so it does not feel cramped against the bottom edge of the viewport when scrolled into view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control anchored at the end of the form content; its vertical position shifts only as the form’s preceding sections expand or contract.

name: Edit Listing
title: Edit Listing
description: Form page for editing an existing draft or active livestock auction listing, using dynamic edit permissions and saving changes back to the listing.

core_features: Load an existing auction listing for editing based on the lotId URL parameter by calling getListingForEdit.
Display all returned listing details (e.g., title, quantity, pricing, closing date/time, description) with fields enabled or disabled according to editPermissions.
Allow sellers to modify permitted fields such as head count, starting bid, reserve price, closing date/time, and description, with inline validation before submission.
Support editing draft listings in full where allowed and saving changes without changing the listing's current status.
Support limited updates to active listings where business rules allow (e.g., description only), clearly indicating which fields cannot be changed.
Submit updates via updateAuctionListing with a dynamically constructed updates payload containing only changed and permitted fields.
Handle success and error responses from updateAuctionListing, showing feedback to the user and navigating back to the lot detail page on successful save.
Provide navigation back to My Listings or to the updated lot detail view using URL-based navigation with the original lotId.
user_workflows: Accessing the Edit Listing page: The user clicks an 'Edit' action on my_listings.html or lot_detail.html; the browser navigates to listing_edit.html with ?lotId=<selected-lot-id>; the page reads lotId from the URL and calls getListingForEdit(lotId); on success, the page shows the editable form with fields enabled or disabled according to editPermissions.

Editing a draft listing and saving changes: With a draft listing loaded, the user updates allowed fields such as head_count, starting_bid_amount, reserve_price_amount, closing_datetime, and description; the form validates required fields and numeric/date formats; the user clicks 'Save Changes'; the page builds an updates object containing only the modified fields that are permitted; updateAuctionListing(lotId, updates) is called; if success is true, the user is redirected to lot_detail.html?lotId=<lot-id>; if success is false, the error message is displayed and the user remains on the edit page.

Editing an active listing with limited permissions: An active listing is loaded and editPermissions indicates restrictions (for example, pricing or head_count cannot be edited); restricted fields are presented as read-only; the user may change allowed fields such as description or closing_datetime if permitted; on 'Save Changes', only changed and allowed fields are included in updates; updateAuctionListing is called; if successful, the user is redirected to lot_detail.html with the same lotId; if the API rejects the update, the error message is shown and existing field values remain on-screen.

Handling loading and access errors: If lotId is missing from the URL, the page shows an error state and a link back to my_listings.html; if getListingForEdit returns an error (such as not found or not owned by the user), the page displays an appropriate error message and disables the form; the Back to My Listings navigation remains available.

Navigating back to My Listings without saving: At any time, the user can click 'Back to My Listings'; the browser navigates to my_listings.html without submitting the form or calling updateAuctionListing; any unsaved changes are discarded.

Staying on the page after a validation error: The user modifies fields and clicks 'Save Changes'; client-side validation detects problems (for example, missing required fields or invalid numeric values); the page displays inline validation messages and does not call updateAuctionListing until all validation errors are resolved and the user resubmits.
interactions: On page load, the page reads the lotId query parameter from the URL; if present, it triggers getListingForEdit(lotId); while loading, the main form is hidden or disabled.

If getListingForEdit succeeds, the form fields are populated with values from the returned lot object (e.g., title, head_count, starting_bid_amount, reserve_price_amount, closing_datetime, description); each field's editable state is determined by the corresponding value in editPermissions.
If getListingForEdit fails or returns an error state, an error message is displayed and the edit form is disabled; a 'Back to My Listings' control remains active.

When the user changes a form field (for example, updating head_count or starting_bid_amount), the new value is stored in the component state and can be compared against the original lot values to determine which fields have changed.

When the user clicks 'Save Changes', the form runs client-side validation on all fields that are editable and required; if validation passes, the component constructs an updates object containing only those fields that have changed and are permitted by editPermissions; it then calls updateAuctionListing(lotId, updates).

While updateAuctionListing is in progress, the save button is disabled and a saving state indicator can be shown; when updateAuctionListing returns, success and message are examined to determine the next state.
If updateAuctionListing returns success: true, the page navigates to lot_detail.html?lotId=<returned-lot-id> using the lot.id from the response (or the existing lotId if unchanged).

If updateAuctionListing returns success: false, the error message is displayed above the form; field values remain as the user last edited them so that corrections can be made; the user can then attempt to save again.
When the user clicks 'Back to My Listings', the page triggers navigation to my_listings.html without making any API calls, regardless of the current form state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by the lotId URL parameter and the responses from getListingForEdit and updateAuctionListing. On initial load, the page reads lotId from location.search; if lotId is missing, the page enters a 'missingId' error state where the edit form is not rendered and only an error message and navigation back to my_listings.html is available. If lotId is present, the page enters a 'loading' state and calls getListingForEdit(lotId). When getListingForEdit resolves successfully, the page transitions to a 'ready' state with two primary data objects: lot (the listing data) and editPermissions (field-level permissions). The form fields bind to lot and use editPermissions to determine which controls are editable or read-only. As the user edits fields, the page maintains a working copy of lot values and tracks which fields differ from the original values to construct the updates object. When the user submits the form, the state changes to 'saving' and updateAuctionListing(lotId, updates) is called. Based on the response, the state either transitions to 'redirecting' (navigating to lot_detail.html?lotId=<lot.id> on success) or back to 'ready' with an error message displayed (on success: false or network/API errors). URL parameters are also used for outgoing navigation: after a successful save, navigation to lot_detail.html includes ?lotId=<lot.id>, and the link back to My Listings navigates to my_listings.html with no additional parameters. No local storage is used; the URL and in-memory state within the page control all behavior.

components.functionality: Controls the overall behavior of listing_edit.html, including reading the lotId from the URL, loading the listing via getListingForEdit, managing loading/error/saving states, and coordinating child components.
Displays the page title and high-level listing context, and provides a 'Back to My Listings' navigation control.
Shows key immutable listing information such as current status, auction format, location, and price unit, and indicates which parts of the listing are not editable based on editPermissions.

Main form that displays and allows editing of the listing fields. It binds to the lot data, respects editPermissions to enable or disable fields, tracks changes, performs client-side validation, and triggers save operations.

Handles basic listing attributes within the form, such as title, breed, head_count, and location fields. Editable status of each field is determined by the corresponding value in editPermissions (for example, head_count depends on can_edit_head_count).

Manages pricing-related fields including price_unit (read-only if not editable), starting_bid_amount, and reserve_price_amount. Fields are enabled only if editPermissions.can_edit_pricing is true, and numeric validation is applied before allowing submission.

Displays and optionally allows editing of closing_datetime, depending on editPermissions.can_edit_closing_datetime. It provides controls for date and time selection and enforces basic validation (such as required format) before submission.

Provides an editable text area for the listing description when editPermissions.can_edit_description is true; otherwise shows the description as read-only. Tracks content changes for inclusion in the updates object.
Displays inline and global validation errors for the form, including both client-side validation failures and messages returned from updateAuctionListing when success is false.

Renders the 'Save Changes' button and any secondary actions (such as cancel/back) and manages disabled states during loading and saving. When activated, it triggers validation and then calls updateAuctionListing via the page controller.
Handles and displays loading, missing-lotId, and load-error states for the page. When an error is present, it hides or disables the form and shows navigation options back to My Listings.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column grid likely based on a 12-column system, with main content occupying approximately 8–9 columns and the right sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a classic, linear top-to-bottom arrangement on a two-column canvas. A slim global header bar spans the full width at the very top. Below it, the page-controller defines a central content region with a left main column and a right sidebar column, matching the mockup’s 65–70% (left) / 30–35% (right) split. All edit-listing components live in the left column in a single vertical stack: status summary at the top, then validation messages, then the edit form with its field groups, and finally the save controls. Load and error states occupy the same left-column region, temporarily replacing or overlaying the form content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a classic, linear top-to-bottom arrangement on a two-column canvas. A slim global header bar spans the full width at the very top. Below it, the page-controller defines a central content region with a left main column and a right sidebar column, matching the mockup’s 65–70% (left) / 30–35% (right) split. All edit-listing components live in the left column in a single vertical stack: status summary at the top, then validation messages, then the edit form with its field groups, and finally the save controls. Load and error states occupy the same left-column region, temporarily replacing or overlaying the form content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header consumes a shallow horizontal band (~56–64px tall) across the full viewport. Below, the main content is centered in a max-width container (around 1120–1200px), divided into a left main column occupying roughly 8–9 of 12 grid columns and a right sidebar occupying 3–4 columns, with a 24–32px gutter. Within the main column, each major block (status summary, form, save controls) is separated vertically by 32–48px. Inside each block, padding is 16–24px, with 12–16px vertical spacing between related controls, following the base 8px spacing system. The right sidebar (not defined by these components) visually mirrors the mockup’s auction lots list but remains secondary to the form.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderately low content density: single-column form sections with labels and inputs stacked vertically, clear 12–16px spacing between fields, and 24–32px between subsections inside a field group. Typography is black/gray on white, with generous line height and paragraph spacing similar to the mockup’s listing details. No heavy boxes or decorative treatments—just subtle dividers and whitespace to group sections. Validation and status areas are clearly separated but compact enough not to feel bulky.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a strong top-to-bottom path with a slight left-to-right emphasis at the very top. The eye first lands on the global header (logo left, nav center, search right), then drops into the left column where it immediately encounters the listing edit status summary, followed by any validation messages. From there, the eye moves sequentially down through the form sections in order: basic info, pricing, timing, description, and finally the save controls as the key call-to-action region. A subtle vertical divider and whitespace visually separate the left editing column from the right sidebar, echoing the mockup’s behavior, but the user’s attention remains anchored on the left column form flow.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a clean white canvas with a slim, full-width header bar pinned to the top, featuring the site logo on the far left, a row of navigation links centered, and a simple search icon aligned to the far right. Beneath this, a centered content container defines a two-column layout: a wider left column for the edit listing workflow and a narrower right column reserved for sidebar content, separated by a light gray vertical divider and a comfortable gutter. In the left column, the first element the user sees is a concise status summary block for the listing they are editing, sitting fairly close to the top of the content area. Directly below that, any validation messages appear as a full-width band, then the main edit form flows downward in a single, readable column. The form is divided into clearly labeled sections—basic details, pricing, timing, and description—each separated by ample vertical spacing rather than boxed cards, mirroring the mockup’s content-first style. At the very bottom of the left column, a horizontal strip of primary and secondary action buttons aligns left, clearly signaling where to save or discard changes. The right sidebar runs parallel but visually subordinate, echoing the mockup’s lot list structure without competing with the main editing workflow. Overall, the layout feels like a straightforward, modern blog/editor interface adapted to livestock listings: minimal, text-driven, and easy to scan from top to bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the global canvas and grid. It spans the full browser width with a white background and centers the main content in a max-width container of roughly 1120–1200px. Within this container, it establishes a two-column grid: a left main column occupying about 65–70% of the width and a right sidebar occupying 30–35%, separated by a 24–32px gutter and a subtle light gray vertical divider line. All edit-listing components (status summary, validation messages, form, sections, save controls, and load states) are placed in a single vertical stack inside the left column. The top of this left column sits approximately 32–40px below the bottom edge of the global header. On smaller screens, the page controller collapses this layout into a single-column flow by stacking the left and right columns vertically, with the edit form content appearing first and inheriting full width; spacing between stacked sections remains at 24–32px to preserve readability.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the structural layout container in normal document flow. It manages responsive behavior (two-column on desktop/tablet, single-column on narrow screens) but does not itself scroll or fix any elements; scrolling is handled by the browser viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The edit listing header appears as a slim bar fixed to the very top of the page content, spanning 100% of the viewport width. It has a white background and a 1px light gray bottom border, visually separating it from the main content below. Inside, content is horizontally aligned on a single line: the logo sits flush left with about 16–24px left padding, the primary navigation links are centered in the available space as a compact horizontal menu, and a simple search icon or minimal search trigger is aligned flush right with matching 16–24px right padding. Vertical padding is modest (8–12px top and bottom) to keep it slim, resulting in a total header height of roughly 56–64px. This bar extends across both the left main column and the right sidebar area, establishing the same visual hierarchy as the mockup’s global header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits at the top of the normal document flow. It can optionally be made sticky on scroll, but by default it behaves as a non-sticky header: it scrolls away with the page content while always remaining visually above the two-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The listing edit status summary is the first content block inside the left main column, positioned roughly 32–40px below the bottom edge of the global header. It spans the full width of the left column and aligns flush with the column’s left edge, respecting the content padding defined by the page controller (typically 16–24px inset from the viewport edge on smaller screens). This block resembles a simple, text-first header region: a line or two of prominent text (for example, the listing title or ‘Edit Listing – Lot 7’) aligned to the left, with status details (e.g., Draft/Active, last updated) either on the same line to the right or on a second line beneath, depending on available width. Vertical padding inside the block is 16–24px, but it does not need a heavy background; it can appear on the plain white canvas, optionally separated from the content below by a thin horizontal divider or extra 16px vertical gap. The block’s bottom edge sits about 24–32px above the validation messages area, clearly signaling the start of the editing context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column’s scroll flow. It remains at the top of the editable content area and scrolls offscreen as the user moves down the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The edit listing form is positioned directly beneath the status summary in the left main column, beginning about 24–32px below the status block’s bottom edge. It spans the full width of the left column from left to right, aligning with the status summary’s left edge. Within this form container, content flows vertically in a single column: first the validation messages (if present), then each field group section (basic, pricing, timing, description), and finally the save controls. The form itself does not need a distinct card background; instead, it uses section headings, 1px gray dividers, and 32–40px vertical spacing between field groups to create structure, closely mirroring the mockup’s simple label/value text stacks. Individual fields inside each section align left, with labels above inputs and 12–16px vertical spacing between field rows, creating a consistent, low-density feel. The form’s bottom edge ends just above the save controls block, with a 24–32px gap that clearly separates content entry from action buttons.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally within the left column. The form scrolls as a whole; it does not have its own internal scroll container. All child sections and controls are contained within this vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The basic information field group is the first form section inside the edit listing form, positioned immediately below the validation messages area (or directly under the form’s top edge if no messages are present). It spans the full width of the left column and begins with a left-aligned section title (e.g., ‘Basic Details’) in a slightly larger or bolder weight than field labels, with about 8–12px of space below the heading. The fields within this group—such as listing title, category, location—are arranged in a single vertical stack, each field occupying the full column width on narrow screens and up to one-half width on larger screens if side-by-side layouts are used, but always aligned to the left and respecting a simple grid. Vertical spacing between fields is 12–16px, and there is 24–32px of padding below the last field before the next section begins. A subtle 1px light gray horizontal divider can appear at the bottom of the group to visually separate it from the pricing section, in line with the mockup’s understated dividers.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form’s vertical flow, with no special behavior. It expands vertically as required by the number of fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing field group sits directly below the basic information group, separated by a vertical gap of about 32–40px or a combination of a 16px gap plus a thin gray divider, continuing the mockup’s clear yet minimal section breaks. It spans the full width of the left column and opens with a left-aligned section heading (e.g., ‘Pricing’), followed by a compact description line if needed. Pricing-related fields (opening bid, price per head, reserve price, etc.) are arranged in a single column of rows with 12–16px vertical spacing. On wider viewports, shorter numeric fields may appear in a two-column sub-grid within this section, but the overall section still reads as a coherent vertical block. There is 24–32px of padding beneath the last pricing field, before the timing section begins, maintaining clear visual separation between concepts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the full available width of the left column and expanding vertically with its content. It remains part of the normal scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timing field group appears below the pricing section, offset by another 32–40px vertical gap or a subtle divider plus gap, matching the rhythm of the previous transitions. It spans the left column’s width and begins with a left-aligned heading such as ‘Timing & Availability’. Date and time fields (auction start, end, preview windows, etc.) are arranged vertically with consistent 12–16px spacing, possibly using side-by-side placement for start/end fields when horizontal space allows, yet always maintaining a clean, predictable alignment. Relative to the sections above, this block feels similar in density and hierarchy—no extra framing, just whitespace and typographic hierarchy. The bottom of the timing group sits 24–32px above the description section, keeping the sequence clean and scannable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form, expanding vertically based on the number and complexity of timing fields. It scrolls along with the rest of the form content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The description field group is positioned directly under the timing section, separated by the same 32–40px vertical spacing or a light divider. It spans the full width of the left column and typically contains fewer but larger controls—most notably a multi-line text area for the listing description. A left-aligned heading (e.g., ‘Description & Details’) sits at the top with 8–12px of space before the first field. The description text area itself stretches nearly the full column width, with a comfortable height (e.g., 6–8 lines visible by default) and 12–16px spacing above and below, giving it visual weight similar to the narrative text blocks seen on the mockup’s listing page. Any secondary fields within this group (e.g., notes to auctioneer) are stacked below with the same vertical spacing. The bottom of this section sits 24–32px above the save controls, making the transition to the action area feel intentional and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the left column. As a potentially tall content area, it simply increases the form’s vertical length and scrolls naturally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation messages block appears inside the edit listing form, immediately beneath the status summary’s visual area and above the first field group. When present, it spans the full width of the left column and sits flush with the form’s left edge. It has slightly stronger visual emphasis than standard fields: a subtle tinted background (e.g., very light red or yellow), a 1px border in a matching tone, and 12–16px of padding on all sides, yet still very minimal and in keeping with the clean aesthetic. Multiple messages are listed in a vertical stack with 8–12px between lines, prioritized from most critical to least. The top of this block is about 16–24px below the status summary, and its bottom edge is 24–32px above the basic info field group, clearly separating guidance/errors from editable fields. When there are no validation issues, this component collapses entirely, allowing the basic info section to slide up into its place with no leftover whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered, occupying space only when there are messages to show. It is static within the form’s vertical flow and scrolls along with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save controls block forms the final horizontal band of the left column content. It is positioned directly beneath the description field group, separated by a 24–32px vertical gap to create a clear distinction between data entry and actions. This block spans the full width of the left column and is left-aligned with the form sections above. Inside, the primary action button (e.g., ‘Save Changes’ or ‘Update Listing’) appears on the far left, followed by secondary actions (e.g., ‘Cancel’, ‘Save as Draft’) to its right, aligned on the same baseline with 12–16px horizontal spacing between buttons. Vertical padding within this band is modest (12–16px top and bottom), and the entire block can be visually anchored by a thin top border or simply by the whitespace above. The bottom of this block leaves 48–64px of whitespace before the page bottom or footer area, similar to the mockup’s open lower margin, giving the actions room to breathe and making them easy to locate at the end of the scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the form’s content. On long forms it scrolls offscreen and is not sticky by default, but could optionally be enhanced later with a sticky variation if needed while maintaining this baseline layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading/error state component occupies the same footprint as the main left-column content and appears in place of, or layered above, the form content depending on configuration. When the listing is loading, it appears centered within the left column, roughly halfway down the visible viewport: a simple spinner or minimal icon with a short status line, both horizontally centered and surrounded by ample whitespace (at least 48–64px above and below) so it stands out against the otherwise empty column. When an error occurs, this component appears as a full-width message block at the top of the left column content area, sitting about 32–40px below the global header—roughly where the status summary would normally be. It uses a subtle error tint background with 16–24px padding and possibly a short explanatory paragraph and retry button, mimicking the visual treatment of validation messages but slightly more prominent. In error mode, it can either push the rest of the content down or, if the form cannot load, occupy the entire left-column area with nothing else visible below it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces or overlays the form content within the left column. It is centered vertically for loading states and positioned at the top of the content column for error states. It scrolls normally if its content exceeds the viewport but typically fits without requiring internal scrolling.

name: My Listings
title: My Listings
description: View and manage your own livestock auction listings, filter by status, sort, and navigate to lot details or create/edit listings.

core_features: Load the current user's auction listings via getMyListings, applying the statusFilter and sortOption URL parameters when present.
Display listings in a table/grid with key fields from getMyListings (title, category, status, head count, current price, price unit, closing date, bid count).
Show status-level counts (draft, scheduled, live, closed, canceled) using totalsByStatus to drive status filter tabs.
Provide a status filter control that updates the statusFilter URL parameter and reloads listings via getMyListings.
Provide a sort control that updates the sortOption URL parameter and reloads listings via getMyListings.
Provide an optional category filter using data from getAuctionCategories combined with category_id on listings for client-side narrowing of the displayed list.
Allow clicking any listing row/card to navigate to the lot detail page (lot_detail.html) with the appropriate lotId URL parameter.
Provide a 'Create New Listing' button that navigates to listing_create.html for creating a new auction lot.

Provide an 'Edit' action for each listing that navigates to listing_create.html with a lotId URL parameter for editing that specific listing (assuming listing_create.html handles both create and edit when given a lotId).
Handle loading, empty, and error states for getMyListings and getAuctionCategories without relying on hardcoded data.
user_workflows:
Review all listings:
1. User navigates to my_listings.html (from header or other pages) without URL parameters.
2. page-init-controller reads the URL, finds no statusFilter or sortOption, and calls getMyListings with undefined statusFilter and sortOption so the backend default behavior applies.
3. Listings, totalsByStatus, and categories (via getAuctionCategories) are loaded.
4. status-summary-tabs displays counts per status; listings-table shows all returned items.
5. User scans the table to review current statuses, bids, and closing dates, and clicks any listing row to open lot_detail.html?lotId={id}.

Work with draft listings only:
1. User selects the 'Draft' tab in status-summary-tabs.
2. status-summary-tabs updates the URL to my_listings.html?statusFilter=draft (preserving any existing sortOption) and triggers page reload or history update.
3. page-init-controller reads statusFilter=draft and calls getMyListings with statusFilter set to 'draft' and the current sortOption.
4. listings-table now displays only draft listings; totalsByStatus remains based on all listings so each tab still shows its count.
5. User uses the Edit action on any row to navigate to listing_create.html?lotId={id} for editing that draft.

Monitor live listings and sort them:
1. User opens my_listings.html with existing URL parameters, or selects the 'Live' tab in status-summary-tabs.
2. page-init-controller calls getMyListings with statusFilter='live' and the current sortOption.
3. User opens sort-control and chooses a different sort option.
4. sort-control updates the URL with sortOption={chosenOption} (preserving statusFilter) and triggers a refetch via getMyListings.
5. listings-table re-renders with live listings in the new sort order so the user can quickly see which listings are closing soonest or performing best (depending on the backend sort behavior).

Create a new listing:
1. User clicks the Create New Listing button in listings-toolbar.
2. create-listing-button navigates to listing_create.html (optionally preserving statusFilter and sortOption in the query string if desired by the implementation, without altering core logic on this page).
3. After creating a new listing, listing_create.html may navigate back to my_listings.html with statusFilter set (for example, to focus on draft or scheduled listings). On return, page-init-controller reads the updated URL and refreshes data via getMyListings accordingly.

Narrow listings by category (client-side):
1. After data loads, category-filter-dropdown is populated using getAuctionCategories and the category_id values from getMyListings.items.
2. User selects a category from category-filter-dropdown.
3. category-filter-dropdown updates internal UI state (not necessarily reflected in URL) and instructs listings-table to display only items whose category_id matches the selected category.
4. User clears the category filter to return to showing all items that matched the current statusFilter and sortOption.
interactions: On initial page load, page-init-controller reads statusFilter and sortOption from the URL and makes a single getMyListings call with those parameters, plus a getAuctionCategories call in parallel.
Clicking a status tab (e.g., Draft, Scheduled, Live, Closed, Canceled) in status-summary-tabs updates the statusFilter parameter in the URL and triggers a refresh of getMyListings.
Changing the sort option in sort-control updates the sortOption parameter in the URL and triggers a refresh of getMyListings.
Selecting or clearing a category in category-filter-dropdown updates the in-memory category filter and causes listings-table to filter its existing items client-side without another API call.
Clicking the Create New Listing button in listings-toolbar navigates the browser to listing_create.html using URL navigation.
Clicking a listing row or card in listings-table navigates the browser to lot_detail.html?lotId={id}, where {id} is the selected AuctionLot.id from getMyListings.items.
Clicking the Edit action in listings-table navigates the browser to listing_create.html?lotId={id} so the user can edit that listing (assuming listing_create.html supports this behavior).

If getMyListings or getAuctionCategories fails, page-init-controller surfaces an error state in feedback-banner and offers a retry action that re-invokes the relevant interface calls with the same URL parameters.
If getMyListings returns an empty items array for the current filters, empty-state-panel displays a functional message and a shortcut to clear filters or create a new listing.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is controlled primarily through the statusFilter and sortOption URL parameters.

On load:
- page-init-controller reads window.location.search to obtain statusFilter and sortOption.
- If statusFilter is present, that value is passed directly into getMyListings as statusFilter; if it is absent, getMyListings is called with statusFilter undefined so the backend applies its default behavior.
- If sortOption is present, that value is passed into getMyListings as sortOption; if it is absent, getMyListings is called with sortOption undefined and the backend determines the default sort order.
- getAuctionCategories is called without parameters to retrieve all available categories.

On status change:
- When the user selects a different status tab, status-summary-tabs updates the statusFilter query parameter using URL navigation (for example, by modifying window.location.href or using history APIs) while preserving sortOption if it exists.
- After the URL is updated, page-init-controller re-runs and calls getMyListings with the new statusFilter and the existing sortOption.

On sort change:
- When the user selects a different sort option, sort-control updates the sortOption query parameter in the URL, preserving the current statusFilter if present.
- page-init-controller then calls getMyListings with the current statusFilter and the new sortOption.

On navigation to lot details:
- listings-table constructs a URL lot_detail.html?lotId={selectedLotId} using the AuctionLot.id value from the selected item and triggers navigation.

On navigation to create/edit listing:
- create-listing-button navigates to listing_create.html for new listings.
- For editing, listings-table navigates to listing_create.html?lotId={selectedLotId}. Additional query parameters may be preserved as needed, but this page's logic only depends on statusFilter and sortOption.

Other filters such as the optional category filter are maintained in memory within relevant components (e.g., category-filter-dropdown). They influence which items are visible in listings-table but do not change URL parameters, ensuring that only the defined statusFilter and sortOption parameters control server-side data fetching via getMyListings.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial load and subsequent reloads by reading URL parameters, calling getMyListings and getAuctionCategories, and distributing the resulting data and loading/error states to child components.

Displays one tab per status using totalsByStatus from getMyListings and indicates which statusFilter is active; when the user selects a tab, it updates the URL and triggers data reload via page-init-controller.
Allows the user to choose a sort option for listings; writes the chosen sortOption into the URL, then signals page-init-controller to refresh getMyListings with the new sortOption.
Uses getAuctionCategories to populate a dropdown or similar control and allows the user to filter the currently loaded listings by category_id client-side, without calling additional interfaces.
Provides consolidated controls above the listings: status-summary-tabs, sort-control, category-filter-dropdown, and the Create New Listing button.
Triggers navigation to listing_create.html so the user can create a new auction listing.

Renders the collection of AuctionLot items returned by getMyListings (optionally filtered by category-filter-dropdown) in a tabular or grid format showing key fields and per-row actions for viewing details or editing.
Displays when getMyListings.items is empty for the current statusFilter and sortOption, providing guidance and shortcuts such as clearing filters or creating a new listing.
Shows loading indicators during getMyListings/getAuctionCategories calls and error messages if any call fails, providing a retry option that reuses the current URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column grid likely based on a 12-column system, with main content occupying approximately 8–9 columns and the right sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a classic blog-style content + sidebar structure adapted for a listings management view. Below the global slim header, all My Listings content is placed inside a centered page container. Within that, the main column on the left (about 65–70% width) holds the core interaction flow: status tabs, toolbar with primary actions, and the listings table or empty state. The right sidebar (about 30–35% width) is used for secondary controls (sorting and category filtering), stacked vertically like simple sidebar widgets. The data-loader and feedback banner sit above these, spanning the content width, but remain visually minimal.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a classic blog-style content + sidebar structure adapted for a listings management view. Below the global slim header, all My Listings content is placed inside a centered page container. Within that, the main column on the left (about 65–70% width) holds the core interaction flow: status tabs, toolbar with primary actions, and the listings table or empty state. The right sidebar (about 30–35% width) is used for secondary controls (sorting and category filtering), stacked vertically like simple sidebar widgets. The data-loader and feedback banner sit above these, spanning the content width, but remain visually minimal.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A shallow horizontal band directly under the global header is reserved for transient elements like the feedback banner and loading states, spanning the full content width. Below that, the primary vertical space is given to the main listings column on the left (roughly 8–9 of 12 grid columns), with the right sidebar occupying the remaining 3–4 columns. The listings table or empty state consumes most of the vertical height in the main column. In the sidebar, the sort and category controls are compact blocks at the top, with generous top margin separating them from the main column content, echoing the mockup’s sidebar spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains moderately low. Controls in the toolbar and sidebar have 12–16px internal padding and 16–24px gaps between adjacent elements. The status tabs are horizontally laid out with ample horizontal breathing room, and the table rows use comfortable line height with 16–24px vertical padding per row. The empty state panel, when shown, has more generous padding to avoid feeling cramped. Overall, there are clear vertical separations (24–32px) between major blocks: feedback banner, tabs, toolbar, table/empty state, and sidebar sections.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the mockup’s left-to-right, top-to-bottom pattern. After the global header, the eye first hits any feedback banner, then moves down to the status summary tabs (primary segmentation of content). From there, the flow continues down into the listings toolbar and primary call-to-action, and then into the listings table. Only after processing the main column does attention shift right, drawn by the vertical alignment and whitespace to the sidebar where sort and category filters are scanned from top to bottom. The data-loader overlays or appears near the top of the main column without breaking this overall flow.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the slim, full-width site header, the My Listings page content sits inside a centered, fixed-width container with a clean white background and light gray dividers. At the very top of this container, a narrow horizontal band is reserved for any feedback banner, spanning the same width as the content.
