Below, the area splits into a two-column layout: a wide main column on the left and a narrower sidebar on the right. The left column begins with a row of status summary tabs, then a minimalist toolbar featuring the primary My Listings actions, including a prominently placed Create Listing button aligned to the right. Directly under the toolbar, the main content region either shows a structured listings table or, when empty, a centered empty state panel with generous padding. The right sidebar starts a bit lower than the left column’s tabs, echoing the mockup’s offset, and contains vertically stacked blocks for category filtering and sort controls, each in its own lightly framed area with modest internal padding. The whole layout feels linear and utilitarian: the user reads and interacts from top to bottom in the main column, then glances to the right for refinement controls, all within a minimalist, content-first design with consistent 8px-based spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader is non-visible by default and does not occupy persistent layout space. When active during initial page load or major data refresh, it manifests as a subtle overlay localized to the main content area (the left column below the status tabs and toolbar). Visually, this appears as a semi-transparent white veil covering the listings table or empty-state region, centered within the main column’s width (about 65–70% of the total page width), with a small spinner or loading indicator centered both horizontally and vertically within that area. It never covers the global header, the status summary tabs, or the toolbar, allowing the user to keep context while understanding that the table data is loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the listings area in the main column when active, positioned absolutely within the main content container so it scrolls out of view with the content and disappears entirely once data has loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status summary tabs sit at the very top of the My Listings content area, just below any feedback banner and aligned with the left edge of the main content container. They form a horizontal strip spanning the full width of the left column (roughly 8–9 of 12 grid columns, or about 65–70% of the page width). Each tab is rendered as a simple text label (e.g., All, Active, Draft, Closed) with a small numeric count, separated by 16–24px horizontal gaps. The tab row has about 16px of vertical padding and a light gray bottom border, giving it a subtle ‘section header’ feel without heavy styling. There is approximately 24–32px of whitespace above the tabs (to separate them from the feedback banner or header) and about 16–24px below them before the listings toolbar begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block anchored at the top of the main column; it scrolls with the page and does not remain fixed once the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is placed in the upper portion of the right sidebar, visually mirroring the role of the search field in the original mockup’s right column. It sits aligned to the left within the sidebar column, occupying roughly 80–100% of the sidebar’s width (about 25–30% of the page width overall). The control appears as a simple label above a compact select or dropdown field, with 8–12px vertical spacing between label and input. The sort block itself is padded with 12–16px on all sides and may be separated from other sidebar elements by a light gray divider line or a 16–24px vertical gap. There is generous top margin (around 32px) from the top of the sidebar column down to the sort control, so it starts slightly lower than the status tabs in the main column, echoing the staggered top offset seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar element; it scrolls with the page and remains vertically stacked above the category filter, maintaining its relative position within the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category filter dropdown is positioned directly below the sort control in the right sidebar, forming the next block in the vertical stack of sidebar utilities. It is left-aligned and uses the same width as the sort control (about 80–100% of the sidebar width). A 16–24px vertical gap separates it from the sort control above, reinforcing the sense of independent sidebar widgets. Like the sort control, it includes a concise label on top and a dropdown/select field below, with 8–12px spacing between the two. The filter’s container has 12–16px internal padding and can share the same light gray border or background treatment as the sort control, creating a cohesive sidebar cluster. Below the category filter, additional vertical whitespace (24–32px) keeps the sidebar from feeling crowded even if no other elements follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar widget; it remains vertically stacked under the sort control and scrolls with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The listings toolbar sits immediately below the status summary tabs in the main left column, serving as the primary control row for managing listings. It is a horizontal bar spanning the full width of the left column (about 65–70% of page width), with 16–24px internal padding and a light gray bottom border that clearly separates it from the table or empty state below. Within this bar, secondary controls such as bulk actions, inline filters, or view toggles (if present) are left-aligned, while the Create Listing button is aligned to the far right, creating a clear left-to-right tension similar to a typical WordPress-style admin toolbar. There is approximately 8–16px of vertical spacing between the bottom edge of the tabs and the top edge of the toolbar, keeping the hierarchy clear but visually connected.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal control strip in the main column that scrolls with the content; it is not sticky but naturally appears near the top of the viewport when the page first loads.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The create listing button is placed inside the listings toolbar, right-aligned within the left main column. It is visually prominent but still consistent with the minimalist theme: a solid or outlined rectangular button with 12–16px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, aligned to the right edge of the toolbar’s content area. The button sits on the same horizontal baseline as any left-aligned toolbar controls, with about 8–16px of horizontal spacing from adjacent elements. On large screens, the button occupies a small fraction of the toolbar width (around 10–15% of the left column), leaving most of the toolbar space for other controls. There is ample whitespace above (from the tabs) and below (before the table) so the button doesn’t feel cramped, even though it shares a row with other utilities.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard inline element within the toolbar; it scrolls with the toolbar and does not stick to the viewport. On narrower widths, it may wrap to its own line within the toolbar while remaining right-aligned.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The listings table occupies the core of the main left column and is positioned directly below the listings toolbar with a 16–24px vertical gap. It spans the full width of the left column (approximately 65–70% of the page width), aligning its left edge with the tabs and toolbar above. The table uses a minimalist style: header row with bold or semi-bold text, light gray horizontal dividers between rows, and a white background. Each row has comfortable vertical padding (12–16px) and a line height that keeps the content legible and not overly dense. There is at least 24–32px of whitespace below the last row before reaching the end of the main content area. The right sidebar remains visible alongside the table on wider screens, with a clear vertical visual separation and consistent column gutter (around 24–32px) between the table’s right edge and the sidebar’s left edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that grows vertically with the number of listings; it scrolls normally within the main column. On smaller screens, it can collapse to full width with the sidebar stacking below, but the baseline design assumes a two-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state panel is shown in the same position and footprint as the listings table when there are no listings to display for the current filters. It appears directly beneath the listings toolbar, centered within the left column. The panel has generous padding (24–32px on all sides) within a subtle container that matches the table width (full width of the left column) and may have a faint border or light gray background to distinguish it from the page background. The main empty-state message is centered or left-aligned within the panel, with ample line spacing and 16–24px vertical spacing between the message, any supportive text, and any action button (e.g., a secondary Create Listing button). The vertical space around the panel mirrors what the table would occupy, ensuring the page doesn’t feel abruptly empty but still maintains the minimalist style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the listings table region within the main column. It scrolls with the rest of the content and does not change the layout of the toolbar or sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback banner is positioned just below the global site header and above the status summary tabs, spanning the full width of the main content container (covering both the left and right columns visually). It appears as a slim horizontal bar with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, centered within the page’s max-width container. The background is a subtle tinted color (e.g., light green for success, light red for errors), but the typography and styling remain minimal: single-line or two-line text aligned left, with any close/dismiss icon aligned to the right. There is 16–24px of vertical spacing below the banner before the status tabs start, ensuring it reads as a separate layer of transient information rather than blending into the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a static block that pushes the main content down when visible. It scrolls out of view as the user scrolls and does not remain fixed to the top of the viewport.

name: Watchlist
title: Watchlist
description: Page where the user can review, filter, sort, and manage all auction lots they are watching, with quick access to each lot’s detail page.

core_features: Load and display all watchlisted auction lots using getWatchlistItems, showing key data such as title, category, location, current price, auction format, status, and closing time.
Allow the user to sort watchlisted lots using options returned by getAuctionSortOptions, applied via the sortOption URL parameter.

Allow the user to filter watchlisted lots by category, maximum price, and closing date constraints using the categoryId, priceMax, closingDateFilterType, closingDateDaysFromNowMin, and closingDateDaysFromNowMax URL parameters.
Allow the user to remove individual lots from the watchlist via removeLotFromWatchlist, updating the list without stale entries.
Allow navigation from each watchlisted lot to its lot detail page (lot_detail.html) by passing the selected lot’s ID as a URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Access and review watchlist
1. User navigates to watchlist.html via the global header link.
2. The page reads URL parameters (sortOption, categoryId, priceMax, closingDateFilterType, closingDateDaysFromNowMin, closingDateDaysFromNowMax).
3. The page calls getAuctionSortOptions to populate the sort control.
4. The page calls getWatchlistItems with the current URL parameter values.
5. The resulting items are rendered in the watchlist list component, showing key lot information and controls to view details or remove each lot.

Workflow: Sort watchlisted lots
1. User opens the sort control (e.g., dropdown) that is populated from getAuctionSortOptions.
2. User selects a sort option (e.g., a closing-time-based or price-based option) identified by its id.
3. The sort control updates the sortOption URL parameter to the selected id (via a URL update or navigation) without altering other filter parameters.
4. The page re-calls getWatchlistItems with the new sortOption and existing filter parameters.
5. The watchlist list re-renders items in the new order.

Workflow: Filter watchlist by category, price, and closing time
1. User interacts with the filter bar:
   - Selects or clears a category filter (categoryId) derived from categories present in the current watchlist results.
   - Enters or clears a maximum price value (priceMax).
   - Selects a closing-date filter type (closingDateFilterType) and, if applicable, provides minimum/maximum days from now (closingDateDaysFromNowMin and closingDateDaysFromNowMax).
2. On filter change, the filter bar updates the corresponding URL parameters, preserving any existing sortOption where applicable.
3. The page calls getWatchlistItems with the updated parameters.
4. The watchlist list re-renders to show only items matching the applied filters.
5. The filter controls stay synchronized with the URL parameters, so a page reload or link sharing preserves the same filters.

Workflow: Remove a lot from watchlist
1. In the watchlist list, user clicks the remove control associated with a specific lot.
2. The list component calls removeLotFromWatchlist with that lot’s ID.
3. On a successful response, the list component either:
   - Removes the item from its local list state, or
   - Triggers a refresh by re-calling getWatchlistItems with the current URL parameters.
4. The total count and list display update to reflect the removed item.
5. If removing the last item results in an empty watchlist, an appropriate empty-state message is shown.

Workflow: Navigate to lot details from watchlist
1. User clicks on a watchlisted lot’s clickable area (e.g., title or row) in the watchlist list.
2. The click handler reads the lot.lot.id from the watchlist item data.
3. The browser navigates to lot_detail.html with URL parameter lotId set to that ID (lot_detail.html?lotId=<selectedLotId>).
4. The watchlist page’s current sort and filter state remain encoded in its URL for when the user navigates back.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Trigger: watchlist.html is loaded.
- Behavior: The initialization component reads all supported URL parameters and uses them to call getAuctionSortOptions and getWatchlistItems. The responses populate the sort control and the list of watchlisted lots.

Interaction: Sort change
- Trigger: User selects a different sort option in the sort control.
- Behavior: The component updates the sortOption URL parameter, then calls getWatchlistItems with the updated sortOption and current filter parameters. The list re-renders in the new order.

Interaction: Category filter change
- Trigger: User selects or clears a category filter option.
- Behavior: The component updates the categoryId URL parameter (setting it, changing it, or removing it) and calls getWatchlistItems with the new categoryId plus existing sort and other filters. The list re-renders with lots matching the selected category state.

Interaction: Price maximum filter change
- Trigger: User inputs or modifies a maximum price.
- Behavior: On apply/submit, the filter bar updates the priceMax URL parameter and calls getWatchlistItems with the updated priceMax and existing sort and other filters, then re-renders the list.

Interaction: Closing date filter change
- Trigger: User selects a closing date filter type and optionally adjusts the min/max days from now.
- Behavior: The filter bar updates closingDateFilterType, closingDateDaysFromNowMin, and closingDateDaysFromNowMax URL parameters accordingly, then calls getWatchlistItems with those values and existing sort/other filters. The list re-renders and shows only lots whose closing_datetime falls within the specified range as interpreted by the backend.

Interaction: Remove from watchlist
- Trigger: User clicks a remove control on a watchlist item.
- Behavior: The item row component calls removeLotFromWatchlist with the associated lot ID. On success, the item is removed from the displayed list (or the list is refreshed via getWatchlistItems). If the backend indicates failure, the component can display an error state without changing the list.

Interaction: Navigate to lot detail
- Trigger: User clicks on a watchlist item’s clickable area (e.g., title, image, or row).
- Behavior: The component reads the lot ID from the item data and navigates to lot_detail.html?lotId=<lot.id>. No additional watchlist-specific parameters are passed to the destination page.

Interaction: URL-driven state (back/forward navigation, external link entry)
- Trigger: User uses browser back/forward buttons or opens a saved URL to watchlist.html with query parameters.
- Behavior: The initialization component re-reads the URL parameters and calls getWatchlistItems and getAuctionSortOptions using those values, ensuring the UI (sort control, filters, list) reflects the encoded state without using localStorage.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is entirely driven by URL query parameters and API responses, without relying on localStorage or hardcoded data. On every load or URL change, the page parses the following parameters: sortOption, categoryId, priceMax, closingDateFilterType, closingDateDaysFromNowMin, closingDateDaysFromNowMax. These parameters are passed directly to getWatchlistItems with matching names. If a parameter is absent from the URL, the frontend either omits it from the API call or uses a default value consistent with backend expectations. The sort control options are always loaded via getAuctionSortOptions and matched against the current sortOption parameter to show the selected value. The filter bar reads the current URL parameters to pre-populate inputs, so reloads or shared links reproduce the same filtered/sorted state. When a user changes sort or any filter, the corresponding URL parameter is updated (either through full navigation or history API) and then getWatchlistItems is called again with the updated parameter set. Removal of an item via removeLotFromWatchlist does not change URL parameters by itself; it affects only the data, after which the displayed list is updated to remain consistent with the server’s watchlist state. Navigation to lot_detail.html passes only lotId, leaving all watchlist page state encoded in the watchlist.html URL for future visits.

components.functionality: Initializes the Watchlist page by reading URL parameters, fetching sort options and watchlist items, and distributing this data to other components.
Displays available sort options for watchlist items and applies the selected option by updating the sortOption URL parameter and reloading data.
Provides filter controls for category, maximum price, and closing date filters, synchronizes with URL parameters, and triggers watchlist reloads when filters change.

Renders the list of watchlisted lots returned by getWatchlistItems, including key lot information and action controls (view details, remove from watchlist). Handles empty state display when there are no items.

Represents a single watchlist item row or card, displaying lot-level fields (title, category_name, breed, head_count, price_unit, current_price_amount, location_city, location_state, closing_datetime, auction_format, status) and exposing actions to navigate to lot details or remove the lot from the watchlist.
Encapsulates the removeLotFromWatchlist call for a given lot ID and coordinates updating the watchlist list after a successful removal.
Detects when getWatchlistItems returns no items (totalCount indicates zero) and displays an appropriate empty-state message instead of the list.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column grid likely based on a 12-column system, with main content occupying approximately 8–9 columns and the right sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same pattern as the mockup: a full-width slim header (outside this scope), then a main two-column content area. The Watchlist lives entirely in the left main column, which holds a vertical stack: page title (e.g., 'Watchlist'), then the filter bar, then the sort control, and then the watchlist list itself. The right column remains a simple vertical sidebar for site-wide content (e.g., other auction links), visually separated by a subtle vertical divider. Within the main column, components are arranged in a straightforward top-to-bottom flow with no overlapping modules.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same pattern as the mockup: a full-width slim header (outside this scope), then a main two-column content area. The Watchlist lives entirely in the left main column, which holds a vertical stack: page title (e.g., 'Watchlist'), then the filter bar, then the sort control, and then the watchlist list itself. The right column remains a simple vertical sidebar for site-wide content (e.g., other auction links), visually separated by a subtle vertical divider. Within the main column, components are arranged in a straightforward top-to-bottom flow with no overlapping modules.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Below the header, the content area spans the full width. The left main column occupies roughly 65–70% of the width, and the right sidebar occupies the remaining 30–35%, matching the mockup's proportions. The Watchlist components (filters, sort, list, empty state, loader) all sit within the left column and extend nearly its full width, with 16–24px padding inside their containers. Vertical spacing between major bands (filters, sort, list) is approximately 24–32px, with smaller 12–16px gaps between individual rows/items.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderately low, similar to a clean WordPress listing. Each band (filters, sort, list) has clear padding on all sides, and list items use 16–20px vertical padding with a 1px light gray divider between rows. Text is allowed to breathe with comfortable line height and 8–12px gaps between lines of metadata within each row. There are no decorative panels; whitespace and simple dividers define structure.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the header down to the 'Watchlist' page title at the top of the left column, then sequentially down through the filter bar, the sort control, and into the list of watched lots. Each row reads left-to-right: lot title and key identifier on the far left, supporting metadata in the middle, and the remove/management controls aligned to the far right. After scanning the main column, the eye naturally shifts right to the sidebar due to the vertical divider and the contrast between the denser list on the left and the simpler sidebar on the right, preserving the mockup’s left-to-right, top-to-bottom flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the slim, full-width header, the page splits into two columns framed by a white background and light gray dividers. The left column, about two-thirds of the width, holds the Watchlist content in a clean vertical stack: the 'Watchlist' title sits close to the top, followed by a full-width filter bar, then a slightly more compact sort control band, and then the watchlist list filling most of the remaining vertical space. Each watched lot appears as a simple, full-width row with a subtle bottom divider, the lot title and key identifiers on the left, supporting auction details running horizontally or in short lines, and a discrete remove control on the right. The right column is a narrow sidebar aligned to the top of the content, separated from the main column by a light vertical rule, and reserved for site-wide items such as links to other auctions. Generous vertical spacing between sections, consistent left alignment, and understated borders create a functional, corporate-feeling layout that is easy to scan without visual clutter.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init data loader is not visible as a standalone block in the final layout, but when active it manifests as a lightweight overlay focused on the left main column. It covers the area from just below the 'Watchlist' title down to the bottom of the main column, matching the width of the watchlist content (approximately 65–70% of the page width). The overlay is centered horizontally within this column, with a single-line 'Loading watchlist…' label and a small spinner or progress indicator aligned to the center both vertically and horizontally. The background remains white or a very subtle translucent white so the overall two-column structure and header still read through faintly, consistent with the minimalist style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a temporary absolute-positioned overlay anchored to the main content column; it sits on top of the filter bar, sort control, and list area while loading, then disappears entirely, returning those components to standard document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control appears as a slim horizontal band directly beneath the filter bar within the left main column. It spans nearly the full width of this column, leaving only standard page margins on the far left and right (roughly 24px). Vertically, it uses about 16–20px of padding above and below its contents. The primary sort dropdown or buttons are right-aligned within the band to echo the mockup’s subtle top-bar right anchoring of search, while any label such as 'Sort by' is aligned inline to the left of the control. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space above it (separating it from the bottom of the filter bar) and 24–32px of space below it before the first list row, preserving a clear segmentation between controls and the data list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in standard document flow as a fixed-position band within the left column; on scroll, it moves up and off-screen with the rest of the content, not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters bar is the first interactive band immediately under the 'Watchlist' page title in the left main column. It spans nearly the full width of the main column (around 65–70% of the page), with a white background and a subtle 1px light gray bottom border that visually separates it from the sort control below. Inside, filter controls (e.g., dropdowns, pill toggles, or checkboxes) are arranged left-to-right in a single row where possible, with 12–16px gaps between them. The entire bar has 16–24px of internal padding on all sides and sits about 16–24px below the page title, ensuring the title feels closely connected while still distinct. There is no heavy framing—just whitespace and the light divider keep it consistent with the minimalist, utility-driven style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow horizontal band that expands vertically if filters wrap to multiple lines; always aligned flush with the left column and constrained to its width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The watchlist list occupies the bulk of the left main column below the sort control. It begins approximately 24–32px beneath the bottom edge of the sort band, reinforcing a clear break between controls and data. The list container stretches the full usable width of the main column, with 16–24px horizontal padding and no visible card-style border—only a light 1px divider at the top (optional) and between individual rows. The list flows vertically downward as a stack of uniform list-item rows, each touching the next only at the divider line, creating a continuous but clearly segmented column of lots. As the user scrolls, this list area is what primarily moves, while the right sidebar scrolls in parallel, mirroring the classic blog + sidebar behavior. On taller viewports, the list visually dominates the page height, ensuring the Watchlist content feels like the primary focus.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A vertically scrolling container that participates in normal page scrolling; list height is unconstrained, so rows extend as needed, pushing the page length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each watchlist item row is a full-width horizontal strip inside the list, following the same visual language as the lot detail text in the mockup. The row stretches from the left padding edge to the right padding edge of the list container, with 16–20px of vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. At the top-left of the row, the lot title (e.g., 'Lot 7 – 75 Head Angus Steers') appears as a bold or slightly larger line, aligned directly under the left edge of the column content. Below or immediately to the right of the title, key metadata such as auction date, seller, weight range, and location are arranged in 2–3 inline label-value clusters, separated by 12–16px of horizontal space or wrapped onto a second line if necessary. The rightmost portion of the row is reserved for actions: a subtle 'Remove from watchlist' link or icon (triggered by the remove handler) is aligned to the far right, vertically centered relative to the content. A thin light gray divider runs along the bottom edge of the row, reaching the full width of the list, providing a clean separation from the next item. The overall feel is informational and flat—no box shadows or heavy borders—optimized for quick vertical scanning.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Each row is a block-level element that participates in the vertical stack inside the list; content within the row uses a simple left-to-right flex or grid distribution, allowing the right-aligned action area to maintain its position even as the main text wraps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-lot handler is tied directly to a small control positioned in the far right segment of each watchlist item row. Visually, this appears as a compact text link (e.g., 'Remove') or a minimalist icon (e.g., an 'x' or trash can) with no heavy button chrome. It is right-aligned so that its clickable area sits flush with the right padding of the row, vertically centered against the row’s content. There is typically 8–12px of horizontal space between this control and any adjacent text. The handler itself has no visible container; instead, it is bound to this link/icon, which respects the row’s existing padding and divider. Hover and focus states may employ a subtle underline or slight color shift while retaining the overall understated, corporate look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives inside each item row, aligned within a right-anchored inline/flex element. It scrolls naturally with its row and does not alter layout dimensions beyond its own small inline footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the watchlist is empty, the list container area in the left main column is replaced by a centered empty state message. This block begins roughly where the first list row would normally appear—about 24–32px beneath the sort control—and spans the same width as the list. Inside, a short heading like 'No lots in your watchlist' is centered horizontally, with a brief explanatory line below it and an optional link or button guiding users back to browse auctions. The text block is vertically padded generously (32–48px top and bottom), creating a calm, open space that visually distinguishes it from the denser list view. The right sidebar remains present as in the normal state, reinforcing the same overall page structure even when the primary list content is missing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same position and width as the list within the standard flow; it fully replaces the list content when active and scrolls normally with the page.

name: Saved Searches
title: Saved Searches
description: Manage saved auction searches and their in-site alert settings, and quickly rerun any saved search on the Auctions page.

core_features: Fetch and display all saved searches for the current user using getSavedSearches, including name, criteria summary, sort option, alert status, and creation time.
Highlight a recently created saved search when a highlightId URL parameter is present.
Allow the user to rerun a saved search by clicking its name, navigating to auctions.html with the savedSearchId URL parameter.
Allow the user to rename a saved search via a modal form using renameSavedSearch.
Allow the user to toggle in-site alerts on or off for each saved search using updateSavedSearchAlerts.
Allow the user to delete a saved search with confirmation using deleteSavedSearch.
Handle loading, empty-state, and error states for the saved searches list without using static or hardcoded data.
user_workflows: View and review saved searches: 1) User opens saved_searches.html from the header navigation. 2) Page reads the highlightId URL parameter, if present. 3) Page calls getSavedSearches with no parameters. 4) While waiting, a loading state is shown. 5) On success, the list of SavedSearch items is rendered, each showing the name, criteria_summary, sort_option, alerts_enabled, and created_at. 6) If highlightId matches one of the returned items, that row is visually emphasized and scrolled into view.

Rerun a saved search: 1) User identifies a saved search in the list. 2) User clicks the saved search name in its row. 3) The page navigates to auctions.html with the URL parameter savedSearchId set to the selected search’s id (e.g., auctions.html?savedSearchId={id}). 4) The Auctions page reads that parameter and applies the associated filters (handled on that page).

Toggle alerts for a saved search: 1) User locates a saved search in the list. 2) User interacts with the alerts toggle control in that row (e.g., switches it on or off). 3) The page immediately calls updateSavedSearchAlerts with savedSearchId set to the item id and alertsEnabled set to the new toggle value. 4) On success, the alerts_enabled state for that item is updated in memory and the toggle remains in the new position. 5) On failure, the toggle is reverted to the previous value and an error message is displayed within the row or globally.

Rename a saved search: 1) User clicks the rename control (e.g., an icon or button) in a saved search row. 2) A modal opens with a text input prefilled with the current name. 3) User edits the name and submits the form. 4) The page calls renameSavedSearch with savedSearchId set to the item id and newName set to the input value. 5) While pending, the submit button indicates progress. 6) On success, the modal closes and the corresponding row in the list updates its displayed name. 7) On failure, the modal stays open and shows an inline error message; the user can retry or cancel.

Delete a saved search: 1) User clicks the delete control in a saved search row. 2) A confirmation dialog appears explaining that the saved search and its alert settings will be removed. 3) User confirms deletion. 4) The page calls deleteSavedSearch with savedSearchId set to the item id. 5) On success, the item is removed from the in-memory list and the visible list updates without a page reload; if no items remain, the empty state is shown. 6) On failure, the dialog closes or stays open (depending on design) and an error message is displayed; the item remains in the list.

Handle highlightId after returning from Auctions page: 1) User creates a new saved search on auctions.html. 2) That page redirects or links to saved_searches.html with highlightId set to the new saved search id (e.g., saved_searches.html?highlightId={id}). 3) On load, the Saved Searches page reads highlightId and calls getSavedSearches. 4) After data loads, the component identifies the item whose id matches highlightId, applies a transient visual highlight, and scrolls it into view. 5) Optionally, after a time delay or interaction, the highlight state is cleared in memory without modifying the URL.
interactions: Click saved search name in a row: triggers navigation to auctions.html?savedSearchId={row.id}.
Click rename control in a row: opens the rename modal prefilled with the current saved search name and associates it with that row’s id.

Submit rename form in the modal: validates non-empty input, then calls renameSavedSearch(savedSearchId, newName); on success, updates the list item’s name and closes the modal; on failure, shows an error message.
Click delete control in a row: opens a confirmation dialog bound to that specific saved search id.
Confirm deletion in dialog: calls deleteSavedSearch(savedSearchId); on success, removes that item from the list; on failure, displays an error.

Toggle alerts switch for a row: immediately updates local UI state to show pending change, calls updateSavedSearchAlerts(savedSearchId, alertsEnabled); on success, keeps new state; on failure, reverts toggle and shows an error.
Initial page load: on DOM ready, reads the highlightId URL parameter (if present), invokes getSavedSearches, and then applies highlight logic to the matching item if found.
Manual page refresh: re-runs getSavedSearches and re-applies highlight logic based on the current highlightId URL parameter.
Error retry interaction: if getSavedSearches fails, the page displays an error with a retry control; clicking retry calls getSavedSearches again without changing URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary external state is the highlightId URL parameter. On every initial page load or when the URL changes, the page reads window.location.search for highlightId. The page then calls getSavedSearches once and stores the returned items in in-memory state. If highlightId is defined and matches an item.id from the response, that item is marked as highlighted in component state and scrolled into view. No state is persisted to localStorage or other client storage. After renameSavedSearch, updateSavedSearchAlerts, or deleteSavedSearch succeed, the page updates its in-memory representation of the SavedSearch list: for rename, it updates the name of the corresponding item; for alerts, it updates the alerts_enabled property; for delete, it removes the item. The URL is not modified in these operations. The only outgoing navigation from this page is to auctions.html with the savedSearchId URL parameter; the Saved Searches page itself does not append extra parameters to that URL. If highlightId is missing or does not correspond to any returned item, no highlight is applied and the page still operates normally. All transient UI states (loading, errors, open modals, pending toggles, transient highlight) are maintained solely in memory and are reset on full page reloads.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and top-level state for the Saved Searches page. On load, parses the highlightId URL parameter, calls getSavedSearches, stores the resulting list of SavedSearch items in state, and passes the data and highlightId down to child components. Manages global loading and error flags for the fetch.

Displays the appropriate global state based on the status of getSavedSearches: a loading indicator while the request is pending, an error message with a retry control on failure, or nothing when data has successfully loaded. Invokes getSavedSearches again when the retry control is activated.

Renders a list of saved searches using the items returned from getSavedSearches. For each SavedSearch, it shows the name, criteria_summary, sort_option, alerts_enabled state, and created_at, and wires up row-level actions (run search, rename, delete, toggle alerts). If the list is empty, renders an empty-state message without static examples.

Represents a single SavedSearch entry in the list. Receives all SavedSearch fields plus a computed isHighlighted flag (derived from comparing the item id with highlightId inside the parent). Handles row-level interactions: clicking the name to navigate to auctions.html with savedSearchId, clicking rename to open the rename modal with this id, clicking delete to open the delete confirmation dialog, and using the alerts toggle to request an update via updateSavedSearchAlerts. Visually indicates highlight when isHighlighted is true.

Manages highlight state for a SavedSearch item based on the highlightId URL parameter. Receives the fetched list from getSavedSearches and the parsed highlightId. Determines which item (if any) should be highlighted, informs the list or row components via an isHighlighted property, and can clear the highlight in memory after a timeout or user interaction without changing the URL.

Provides a form to rename an existing SavedSearch. Opens when a row’s rename action is triggered, pre-populating the input with the current name. On submit, validates the input and calls renameSavedSearch with parameters savedSearchId and newName. On success, signals the parent to update the corresponding item in the SavedSearch list and closes the modal. On failure, displays an inline error message and allows resubmission or cancellation.

Asks the user to confirm deletion of a specific SavedSearch. Opens when a row’s delete action is triggered. On confirmation, calls deleteSavedSearch with savedSearchId set to the selected item’s id. On success, notifies the parent to remove the item from the in-memory list and closes. On failure, displays an error and keeps the dialog open or closes it according to the implementation.

Controls the alerts_enabled state for a single SavedSearch. Embedded inside each row, it reflects the current alerts_enabled value and allows it to be toggled. When the user toggles the control, it calls updateSavedSearchAlerts with savedSearchId and alertsEnabled (the new value). On success, the SavedSearch list state is updated to match. On error, the toggle reverts to the previous value and an error message is surfaced by the parent or row.

Implements the navigation when a saved search is run again. Binds to the click on a saved search name and constructs a URL for auctions.html that includes the savedSearchId query parameter set to the selected SavedSearch id. Triggers a full page navigation to that URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column grid likely based on a 12-column system, with main content occupying approximately 8–9 columns and the right sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same classic blog layout as the mockup: a full-width, slim global header at the very top; beneath it, a two-column content area where the left column is the main content for Saved Searches (page title, description, list of saved searches) and the right column is a narrower sidebar for small utilities (e.g., informational text, links, or secondary controls related to searches). Within the main column, everything stacks vertically in a linear top-to-bottom flow: page title at the top, a short explanatory paragraph, then the saved searches list, with each saved-search row stacked one after another.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same classic blog layout as the mockup: a full-width, slim global header at the very top; beneath it, a two-column content area where the left column is the main content for Saved Searches (page title, description, list of saved searches) and the right column is a narrower sidebar for small utilities (e.g., informational text, links, or secondary controls related to searches). Within the main column, everything stacks vertically in a linear top-to-bottom flow: page title at the top, a short explanatory paragraph, then the saved searches list, with each saved-search row stacked one after another.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header forms a shallow horizontal band across the full width, about 64px–80px tall. Below, the content container spans the full width but is constrained to a centered max-width (e.g., 1080–1200px) to mirror the WordPress-style theme. Inside this container, the left main column takes roughly 65–70% of the width and the right sidebar 30–35%, separated by a vertical gutter of about 24px–32px. Within the main column, the title and introductory text are grouped near the top with a 24px–32px gap before the list. Each saved-search row occupies the full width of the main column and is separated by subtle 1px dividers and 16px–24px vertical padding. The sidebar content forms a single vertical stack: small top block for any search/filter or helper text, then other sidebar content below with about 32px separation between blocks.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately low density consistent with the mockup: generous line-height for text, 8px–16px spacing between inline elements, and 16px–24px padding inside list rows. Elements are not crammed together; controls within each saved-search row are clearly separated but still feel part of a compact, functional block. The sidebar list or text blocks use single-line items or short paragraphs with modest spacing (8px–12px) between them, keeping the interface clean and readable without feeling empty.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a strong left-to-right, then top-to-bottom pattern. On load, the user’s attention first hits the global header (logo left, navigation center, search icon or global search right), then drops to the main content where the 'Saved Searches' page title anchors the top-left of the main column. From there, the eye moves down through the description text and then into the vertical list of saved-search rows. Each row reads left-to-right: search name and metadata on the left, then secondary actions (run search navigation link, alerts toggle) aligned to the right edge. After scanning the main column, the eye is gently pulled to the right by whitespace and the vertical separation line/gutter, where the sidebar’s top block and subsequent items are scanned top-to-bottom. Modals, when present, interrupt this flow by centering over everything with a dimmed backdrop, making them the clear focus.

layout.overall_description: The Saved Searches page adopts the same understated, content-first aesthetic as the auction lot detail mockup. A slim, full-width header sits at the very top: logo anchored to the far left, a small cluster of primary navigation links horizontally centered, and a search icon or compact search entry aligned to the far right, all on a white background with perhaps a subtle bottom divider. Below this, the main page content is centered within the viewport in a two-column layout bounded by generous left and right margins. The left column, about two-thirds of the width, begins almost immediately under the header with the 'Saved Searches' title in a simple, bold type treatment, followed by a short explanatory line describing how saved searches and alerts work. A bit lower, the saved-searches list occupies the rest of the left column, running vertically down the page. Each saved-search row spans the full width of this column, with a thin light-gray divider between rows and comfortable padding within. The search name and metadata are aligned to the left, while a 'Run search' navigation link and the alerts toggle are aligned towards the right side of each row, creating a clear functional rhythm as users scan down the list. The right sidebar, about one-third of the width, starts at the same vertical level as the main column. At its top, a compact utility block (such as a hint text or small search/filter specific to saved searches) sits with 16px–24px padding, and below it, additional sidebar content (e.g., links back to the Auctions page or explanatory text about alerts) stacks vertically with ample spacing. The whole page presents a calm, corporate feel: crisp black and gray typography on white, minimal borders, and whitespace guiding the eye from the header, through the main saved searches, over to the supporting sidebar. When loading, a lightweight loader sits within the main content area; when renaming or deleting, a centered modal or confirmation dialog dims the background, briefly taking over the otherwise linear reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell defines the global frame and two-column structure. At the very top of the viewport, it renders a slim, full-width header bar on a white background. Within this header, the site logo is left-aligned with about 24px–32px left padding from the viewport edge. In the center of the header, a horizontal row of navigation links (e.g., Auctions, Saved Searches, Account) is centered relative to the page width. On the far right of the header, aligned with about 24px–32px right padding, sits a small global search icon or compact input field. The header height is modest (around 64px–72px total), matching the mockup’s slim top band, and has a light gray bottom border to visually separate it from the content. Below the header, the shell creates a centered content area with a maximum width (e.g., 1080–1200px) and 32px–48px of top margin from the bottom of the header. Inside this content area, it applies a two-column layout: the left main column consumes roughly 65–70% of the width, starting flush at the left of the content container; the right sidebar occupies the remaining 30–35%, with a 24px–32px gutter separating them. The main column begins with the 'Saved Searches' page title aligned to the left edge, followed by a short paragraph or line of helper text, and then the saved-searches list stacked vertically. The sidebar column aligns its top to roughly the same vertical starting point as the 'Saved Searches' title, hosting smaller, secondary content blocks stacked downward. Vertical spacing between primary sections (header, main content, footer if present) follows the 32px–48px spacing rule, keeping the layout open and orderly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width container that scrolls normally with the page; header is anchored at the top of the document (not sticky) while the main content and sidebar scroll together below it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading state sits inside the main content column defined by the page shell, occupying the space where the saved-searches list would normally appear. Horizontally, it aligns with the left edge of the main column and spans nearly its full width (about 90–100% of the main column, respecting its internal padding). Vertically, it is positioned directly beneath the page title and introductory text, with about 16px–24px of top margin. The loader content (whether a spinner or skeleton rows) is centered within this block: the spinner would appear centered horizontally with roughly 48px–64px of vertical padding above and below, while skeleton rows would mimic the width and height of saved-search rows, stacked in a linear vertical column with 8px–16px gaps between them. No full-screen overlay is used; the header and sidebar remain visible, but the main column clearly indicates that content is loading via subdued gray tones matching the site’s minimal, utility-focused style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main content flow, replacing or overlaying the saved-searches list area; scrolls with the page and does not block interaction with the header or sidebar (unless a stricter blocking behavior is desired, in which case it can dim only the main column).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-searches list is positioned in the left main column, directly under the 'Saved Searches' title and any accompanying explanatory text. It begins approximately 16px–24px below the final line of introductory copy, occupying the full usable width of the main column from left to right (minus any internal padding applied by the shell, typically 16px–24px). The list is a vertical stack of saved-search rows with no card-style container; instead, it uses a simple white background, with each row separated by a thin 1px light-gray horizontal border that runs full width across the column. Top and bottom padding for the list block as a whole is modest (16px–24px at the top and bottom), so it feels anchored but not framed. The list extends downward as far as there are items; if it surpasses the viewport height, it scrolls with the page, maintaining alignment with the main column. There is no additional grid within the list itself—only a straightforward top-to-bottom sequence that echoes the blog-style post list from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block that fills the main column and grows vertically with its content; scrolls with the main page and maintains alignment with the left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each saved-search row is a horizontal strip within the saved-searches list, spanning nearly the full width of the main column. The row has a white background and is bounded above and below by thin light-gray dividers (1px) that run edge-to-edge across the column. Inside the row, padding of about 16px–20px is applied on all sides, creating a comfortable buffer between text and borders in line with the 8px base unit. Content is arranged in a two-line, left-right layout. On the first line, the saved search name appears left-aligned in a slightly heavier or larger font, occupying most of the left side, while the 'Run search' navigation link is right-aligned on the same line, hugging the right edge with a small 8px–12px inset from the row’s inner padding. On the second line, directly beneath the name, lighter metadata such as creation date, last run date, or summary of filters is left-aligned in smaller, muted gray text. On the right side of this same second line, aligned vertically with the metadata, sits the alerts toggle control with a short label (e.g., 'Alerts on' / 'Alerts off') aligned to the right edge. Horizontal spacing between left and right blocks is managed by flexible space, so the left text does not crowd the right-aligned controls. Vertically, the two lines are separated by about 4px–8px, maintaining a low-density but tidy appearance. If the row needs more content (e.g., additional tags or labels), these can appear below the metadata as a third line, aligned left and separated by another 4px–8px, while the right-aligned controls remain anchored to the top or middle of the right side, preserving the visual hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline list item that expands in height based on content but always spans the full width of the list; no special positioning, it scrolls with the list and page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlight controller affects individual saved-search rows transiently rather than occupying its own visible space. When activated (e.g., after creating or updating a search), it applies a subtle visual emphasis to the target row. This emphasis appears as either a soft, pale background tint across the entire row (e.g., a very light gray or brand-accent color) or a thin vertical accent bar (2px–4px) along the left edge of the row, running the full row height, combined with a slightly stronger row background compared to others. The highlight sits entirely within the row’s existing boundaries and does not change the row’s padding or overall dimensions, preserving the consistent vertical rhythm of the list. The effect begins immediately upon trigger, holds for a short period (e.g., 3–5 seconds), and then smoothly fades back to the standard row appearance, ensuring it draws the eye without feeling jarring.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay-style styling applied to an existing row within the list; it does not alter layout structure, only visual treatment, and moves with the row as the page scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rename modal appears as a centered dialog overlaying the entire page content, including both main column and sidebar, while leaving the global header faintly visible underneath a semi-transparent backdrop. The backdrop covers the viewport from edge to edge with a low-opacity black or dark gray layer, subtly dimming the underlying interface. The modal itself is a white rectangular panel, roughly 400–480px wide on desktop, centered both horizontally and vertically in the viewport. It has 24px–32px of internal padding on all sides, aligning with the main content components’ padding scale. At the top of the modal, a simple title such as 'Rename Saved Search' is left-aligned; directly beneath it, a single-line explanatory text may appear with 8px–12px of vertical spacing. The main content area of the modal is a full-width text input field labeled 'Search name', with 8px–12px spacing above the field label and 16px–24px below the input before the action buttons. At the bottom right of the modal panel, primary and secondary action buttons (e.g., 'Save' and 'Cancel') are aligned horizontally, with 8px–12px space between them and about 16px–20px vertical padding from the bottom edge. The modal has a subtle drop shadow and possibly a slight border radius, but remains clean and minimal, consistent with the corporate utility feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay that centers in the viewport and remains in place while the underlying page is scrolled (scrolling is typically disabled while it is open); includes a full-viewport dimming backdrop to focus attention.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delete confirmation dialog is a slightly more compact overlay than the rename modal but follows the same central positioning. When invoked, it appears in the center of the viewport with a semi-transparent backdrop covering the entire page. The dialog width is around 320–400px, with 24px padding on all sides. At the top, a brief, bold prompt such as 'Delete this saved search?' is left-aligned. Below, a short paragraph explains the consequence (e.g., 'This will remove the saved search and its alerts. This action cannot be undone.') with 8px–12px of vertical spacing after the title. The bottom portion houses action controls: a destructive primary button (e.g., 'Delete') aligned on the right, with a secondary 'Cancel' button to its left, separated by 8px–12px. Vertical spacing between the content text and the button row is around 16px–20px to maintain clarity without clutter. The dialog’s visual treatment is minimal (white background, subtle shadow) and does not use heavy color except on the destructive button, which may use the brand’s accent red, keeping it visually consistent with the rest of the site yet clearly signaling risk.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position confirmation dialog centered in the viewport with a dimming backdrop; blocks interaction with the underlying page until dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The alerts toggle is positioned within each saved-search row on the right side, typically on the second line opposite the metadata. It is right-aligned, sitting flush with the row’s inner right padding (around 16px–20px inset from the right border). Horizontally, the toggle control itself (a compact switch-style element) is closest to the right edge, with its label (e.g., 'Alerts on' / 'Alerts off') to the left of the switch with about 8px of spacing. Vertically, the toggle aligns to the baseline or vertical center of the metadata text on the left, so the row appears balanced. The control does not expand the row’s height; instead, the row’s vertical padding ensures there is enough breathing room above and below the toggle. On narrower widths, if space becomes constrained, the toggle and its label can wrap to a new line beneath the metadata, but still remain right-aligned, preserving the right-hand actions grouping. The visual style is understated: simple switch track and thumb with minimal color, using a subtle accent for the 'on' state that stands out against the otherwise grayscale interface, yet still feels utilitarian.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline control within the row layout that moves with its parent row; no fixed or sticky behavior, it simply occupies its allocated right-aligned position and scrolls with the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The run navigation element appears in each saved-search row as a text-based link or lightweight button aligned to the top-right of the row, on the same first line as the saved search name. It is right-aligned within the row, positioned just inside the row’s right padding margin (about 16px–20px from the edge). The link text (e.g., 'Run search') uses the primary accent color or a slightly stronger gray compared to body text, and is kept small and unobtrusive, matching the minimalist, functional theme. The link sits horizontally opposite the search name, with flexible space in between, and vertically centered on the first line’s text baseline. There is about 4px–8px of vertical spacing between this line and the metadata line below, ensuring the link doesn’t visually collide with the information beneath. When a row is narrower (e.g., on smaller screens), the 'Run search' link can wrap to a second line directly under the name but remains right-aligned, keeping the pattern of right-side actions intact. No icon is strictly necessary; if used, a small right-pointing arrow could sit to the right of the link text with 4px spacing. The element does not add borders or extra containers, staying visually light.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline navigation link inside each row that scrolls with the page; it has no sticky or floating behavior and simply maintains a right-aligned position within the row’s content area.

name: Profile Settings
title: Profile Settings
description: Configure your profile details, livestock interests, contact preferences, and visibility settings used across auctions and messaging.

core_features: Load the current user's profile details and selected livestock interests using getProfileSettings when the page is opened.
Load the available livestock interest options (IDs and labels) using getLivestockInterestOptions for use as selectable checkboxes.
Allow editing of basic profile information including full name, ranch/business name, and location (state and city), bound to the ProfileSettings model.
Display account type and email from the profile data (with account type used for context and not altered on this page, unless future requirements change).
Allow the user to select or modify livestock interests via a checkbox list driven entirely by getLivestockInterestOptions and the selectedLivestockInterests data.
Allow the user to choose a preferred contact method using the enumeration defined in ProfileSettings.preferred_contact_method.
Provide a toggle to control the ProfileSettings.show_buying_interests_on_profile flag, determining whether livestock interests are visible to other users.
Validate required profile fields before saving (at minimum: account_type, preferred_contact_method, show_buying_interests_on_profile, and any additional client-side rules).
Save profile and livestock interest changes by calling updateProfileSettings with the current form state.
Respond to the onboarding URL parameter to adjust page behavior for first-time setup, including tailored post-save navigation to auctions.html.
Provide a way to navigate back to auctions.html (e.g., 'Back to auctions' or 'Start browsing') after successfully saving settings, in line with outgoing_connections.
user_workflows:
Existing user updates profile settings:
1. User navigates to profile_settings.html (typically via the Profile link in the header navigation).
2. On page load, the page parses the URL for the onboarding parameter; if not present or not true, the page treats this session as a standard edit session.
3. The page triggers getProfileSettings to retrieve the current ProfileSettings and selectedLivestockInterests.
4. In parallel or immediately after, the page triggers getLivestockInterestOptions to retrieve the list of available interest options and labels.
5. Once both calls succeed, the form fields are populated: basic profile info from profile, the preferred_contact_method selector from profile.preferred_contact_method, the visibility toggle from profile.show_buying_interests_on_profile, and livestock interest checkboxes pre-checked based on selectedLivestockInterests.
6. The user edits full name, ranch/business name, and location fields directly in the form inputs.
7. The user modifies livestock interests by checking or unchecking options in the interest list, which updates in-memory selection state.
8. The user changes the preferred contact method via a select/radio control bound to profile.preferred_contact_method.
9. The user adjusts the visibility toggle controlling profile.show_buying_interests_on_profile as desired.
10. On clicking the Save button, client-side validation runs; if validation passes, the page builds a profile object (using the current field values) and a livestockInterests array (the selected interest IDs) and calls updateProfileSettings.
11. If updateProfileSettings succeeds, the page updates its in-memory profile and selection state with the response and shows a success confirmation.
12. The user may either remain on the page to continue editing or use the 'Back to auctions' link to navigate to auctions.html.

Newly registered user onboarding flow:
1. After registration on index.html, the user is redirected to profile_settings.html with the onboarding URL parameter set (e.g., profile_settings.html?onboarding=true).
2. On page load, the onboarding flag is read from the URL and the page enters onboarding mode.
3. The page calls getProfileSettings to load the initial ProfileSettings (typically containing registration defaults) and selectedLivestockInterests, and getLivestockInterestOptions for the interest options.
4. Once data is loaded, the profile form fields and interests are displayed; onboarding-specific messaging or emphasis (non-marketing) can be shown to indicate that completion helps tailor auction results.
5. The user fills in or adjusts core profile fields (full name, ranch/business name, location), selects livestock interests, and sets a preferred contact method and visibility toggle.
6. The user clicks the Save & Start Browsing (or equivalent) primary action; validation runs, and if valid, updateProfileSettings is called with current profile and livestockInterests state.
7. On success, the page updates local state to match the response and displays a confirmation.
8. In onboarding mode, a follow-up action is presented to navigate to auctions.html (for example, the primary button now triggers navigation after successful save). The navigation occurs only after a successful updateProfileSettings response.
9. If the user attempts to navigate away (e.g., via browser back) with unsaved changes detected, the page can optionally prompt the user to confirm leaving without saving (handled purely client-side without additional interfaces).

Handling errors and validation during profile update:
1. User completes edits on the Profile Settings form and clicks Save.
2. The client-side logic checks required fields including the required ProfileSettings enums and booleans and any additional required fields defined by the product team.
3. If any required field is missing or invalid, the form prevents the updateProfileSettings call and displays inline validation error messages via a feedback component.
4. If client-side validation passes, the page calls updateProfileSettings with the constructed profile object and livestockInterests array.
5. If updateProfileSettings returns an error (success is false or the call fails), the page shows an error message describing that the save was unsuccessful, using the returned message when available.
6. The user corrects the indicated issues or tries again later; upon a subsequent successful call, error messages are cleared and replaced by a success confirmation.
7. The navigation link or button to auctions.html remains enabled only once the latest save attempt has succeeded (depending on product decision), particularly in onboarding mode.
interactions: Page load interaction: When profile_settings.html loads, an onPageLoad handler parses the onboarding URL parameter and triggers getProfileSettings and getLivestockInterestOptions. While these are in flight, the page may show a loading state and disable form interactions until data is ready.

Profile form input interaction: When the user types into the full name, ranch/business name, location state, or location city inputs, an onChange/onInput handler updates the in-memory ProfileSettings representation and marks the form as 'dirty' (unsaved changes).

Livestock interest checkbox interaction: When the user toggles any livestock interest checkbox, an onOptionToggle handler updates an in-memory collection of selected interest IDs, ensuring it stays in sync with selectedLivestockInterests and that this collection is used when calling updateProfileSettings.

Preferred contact method selection interaction: When the user selects a preferred contact method from the available enumeration values, an onChange handler updates the profile.preferred_contact_method in the in-memory ProfileSettings state and marks the form dirty.

Visibility toggle interaction: When the user uses the 'show buying interests' toggle, an onToggle handler flips the profile.show_buying_interests_on_profile value in the in-memory state and marks the form dirty.

Save button interaction: When the user clicks the Save (or Save & Start Browsing) button, an onClickSave/onSubmit handler runs validation on the current form data. If valid, it constructs the update payload and calls updateProfileSettings. The button enters a loading/disabled state until the response is received.

Save success interaction: If updateProfileSettings returns success, an onSaveSuccess handler updates all bound components with the new profile and selectedLivestockInterests, clears the dirty state flag, displays a success message, and, if onboarding is true, enables or highlights the navigation action to auctions.html.

Save error interaction: If updateProfileSettings fails, an onSaveError handler displays an error message and re-enables the Save button without clearing the dirty state, allowing the user to retry after making changes.

Navigation to auctions interaction: After a successful save, when the user clicks the 'Back to auctions' or 'Start browsing' link/button, an onNavigateToAuctions handler navigates the user to auctions.html in accordance with outgoing_connections. In onboarding mode, navigation may be blocked or discouraged until a successful save has occurred.

Unsaved changes guard interaction (optional): If the user attempts to close the tab, refresh the page, or navigate away (including using browser back) while the form is dirty, an onBeforeUnload or route-change guard prompts the user to confirm leaving without saving, implemented purely on the client without additional API calls.

Feedback message interaction: When a success or error message is shown, the user may dismiss it using an onDismissMessage handler, which removes the message from the visible state without affecting stored ProfileSettings.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state driver for this page is the onboarding URL parameter. On each page load, the script reads the 'onboarding' parameter value from window.location. If the parameter is present and evaluates to a truthy representation (e.g., 'true'), the page enters onboarding mode; otherwise it uses standard edit mode. Onboarding mode affects: (1) the primary action label (for example, labeling it as an initial setup action), (2) whether navigation to auctions.html is emphasized immediately after a successful save, and optionally (3) whether navigation to auctions.html is blocked until at least one successful updateProfileSettings call has completed in this session. Aside from the onboarding param, the rest of the page state is managed in memory based on interface responses and user interactions: 'loading' vs 'ready' vs 'error' state from getProfileSettings and getLivestockInterestOptions; a 'dirty' flag tracked whenever form fields diverge from the last successfully saved ProfileSettings and selectedLivestockInterests; and transient UI states such as 'saving' during the updateProfileSettings call, and current feedback messages (success or error). No state is persisted via URL parameters beyond the initial onboarding flag, and no state is persisted via localStorage; all persistence of user configuration is handled exclusively by updateProfileSettings. Navigation to auctions.html does not add additional URL parameters, following the specified outgoing_connections.

components.functionality: On page load, parses the onboarding URL parameter, triggers getProfileSettings and getLivestockInterestOptions, and initializes the shared in-memory state used by all child components (profile data, selected livestock interests, interest options, loading/error flags, onboarding mode).

Presents editable fields for basic profile information (full name, ranch/business name, location_state, location_city) and displays non-editable fields such as account_type and email if required. It keeps these fields synchronized with the in-memory ProfileSettings state and marks the form as dirty when changes are made.

Displays the list of livestock interest options using the data returned from getLivestockInterestOptions, and pre-selects those whose IDs are present in selectedLivestockInterests. It updates the in-memory selected interests collection whenever a checkbox is toggled so that updateProfileSettings can be called with an accurate livestockInterests array.

Allows the user to view and modify the preferred_contact_method field of ProfileSettings using controls that map directly to the enumeration values defined for that field. Changing the selection updates in-memory ProfileSettings and marks the form as dirty.

Provides a single toggle bound to the show_buying_interests_on_profile boolean in ProfileSettings, allowing the user to control whether their livestock interests are visible on their public-facing profile. Updates the in-memory ProfileSettings state and dirty flag on change.

Renders the primary Save (and, in onboarding mode, Save & Start Browsing) actions, orchestrates client-side validation across all bound sections, constructs the payload for updateProfileSettings, calls the interface, and handles success/error results. On success, it updates in-memory ProfileSettings and selected interests, resets the dirty flag, and manages whether navigation to auctions.html is enabled.

Displays validation errors, loading or saving errors, and success confirmations triggered by other components. Messages may be derived from updateProfileSettings.message or from client-side validation logic. Allows users to dismiss individual messages without altering underlying ProfileSettings data.

Monitors whether the form is in a dirty state (ProfileSettings or livestock interests differ from the last saved version) and, if so, intercepts browser unload or internal navigation attempts to prompt the user before leaving without saving. Does not call any interfaces; it only interacts with in-memory state and the browser navigation lifecycle.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column grid likely based on a 12-column system, with main content occupying approximately 8–9 columns and the right sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same classic blog-style layout as the mockup: a slim full-width header at the top, and below it a two-column content area. The left main column holds the linear, text-first profile settings form (basic profile, livestock interests, contact preferences), stacked in a top-to-bottom sequence. The right sidebar is reserved for secondary controls, with the visibility toggle placed near the top of the sidebar similar to the search box in the mockup. System-level overlays (loader, unsaved changes guard) sit above this structure and do not disturb the underlying two-column flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same classic blog-style layout as the mockup: a slim full-width header at the top, and below it a two-column content area. The left main column holds the linear, text-first profile settings form (basic profile, livestock interests, contact preferences), stacked in a top-to-bottom sequence. The right sidebar is reserved for secondary controls, with the visibility toggle placed near the top of the sidebar similar to the search box in the mockup. System-level overlays (loader, unsaved changes guard) sit above this structure and do not disturb the underlying two-column flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain the 12-column feel: the main content column occupies roughly 8–9/12 of the page width (about 65–70%), positioned left-aligned under the header. The sidebar sits to its right, about 3–4/12 wide (30–35%), separated by a light vertical divider or clear gutter. The header remains a shallow band full-width at the top. The save bar aligns with the main column and hugs its bottom edge, without spanning into the sidebar. Overlays (loader and navigation guard) cover the entire viewport content area below the header.
layout_strategies.content_density: Follow a moderately low density: each form section uses a single-column layout with clear typography, 16–24px internal padding, and 16–24px vertical spacing between fields. Between major sections (basic profile, interests, contact preferences) use 32–40px vertical separation. Sidebar elements have slightly tighter padding (12–16px) but still breathe. Alerts and the save bar are visually compact but separated by at least 16–24px from adjacent content to avoid crowding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the left-to-right, then top-to-bottom flow of the mockup. After the header, the eye lands on the page title and any top feedback messages in the left column, then travels downward through the profile sections in order. The gutter and vertical separation then pull attention to the right sidebar, where the visibility toggle sits near the top as a quick secondary control. The save bar anchors the bottom of the main content column, reinforcing completion. Overlays like the loader and unsaved-changes dialog temporarily interrupt this flow by centering a focused state over the entire page.

layout.overall_description: Below a shallow, full-width header bar, the page splits into a clean two-column layout on a white background. The left side—around two-thirds of the width—is the main profile settings column, starting close under the header with the “Profile Settings” title, any feedback messages, and then a tall, single-column stack of form sections: your basic profile information at the top, followed by livestock interests, then contact preferences. Each section reads like a simple article: labels and inputs flow top-to-bottom, with comfortable gaps between groups so the content feels ordered but not sparse. To the right, a narrower sidebar runs from near the same top baseline, separated from the main column by whitespace or a subtle vertical divider; at its top is the visibility toggle, sitting in a compact block much like the search field in the original mockup’s sidebar. As the user scrolls, the main column dominates the reading path, while the sidebar remains a quiet secondary strip. At the bottom edge of the main column, a low-profile save bar aligns precisely with the left column’s width, offering actions without intruding into the sidebar.
