When data is loading or the user attempts to navigate away with unsaved changes, the main two-column view is dimmed and a centered overlay or spinner appears, but once dismissed, the user returns to the straightforward, content-first layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The initial data loader appears as a semi-transparent overlay that covers the entire viewport content area below the main header. It sits above both the left main column and the right sidebar, starting directly under the header’s bottom edge and extending to the bottom of the visible window. At the center of this overlay, a small, unobtrusive loading indicator (e.g., spinner with a short status label) is horizontally and vertically centered. The underlying two-column layout is slightly dimmed and non-interactive while the loader is visible. Once loading completes, the overlay fades out to reveal the main profile settings layout without shifting any underlying content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay anchored to the viewport beneath the header, spanning 100% width and height of the content area. It floats above all other components (highest z-layer) and does not affect the positioning or size of the underlying layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The basic profile form sits in the upper portion of the left main column, directly below the page title and any feedback messages. It spans the full width of this column, which takes roughly 65–70% of the page width, left-aligned with the site’s main content margin. A top margin of about 24–32px separates it from feedback messages, maintaining a clear but connected flow. Inside this form, fields are arranged in a single vertical stack: each label appears above its input, with about 12–16px vertical spacing between fields, and 16–24px padding from the form’s outer edges. The bottom edge of this form section ends with a slightly larger gap (around 24–32px) before the next section (livestock interests), making it clear that this is a distinct block within the overall settings.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the left main column, participating in the normal top-to-bottom document flow. It resizes fluidly with the column width on responsive breakpoints while preserving its single-column internal layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The livestock interests selector is positioned immediately below the basic profile form within the left main column, aligned flush with the same left and right edges. A vertical gap of about 32px separates it from the form above, signaling a new section while maintaining linear continuity. The component occupies the full width of the main column and presents its checkboxes in a simple stacked layout: a section heading at the top, followed by checkbox options arranged in one or two columns depending on viewport width, with 8–16px vertical spacing between rows. The padding inside this section matches or slightly exceeds that of the basic profile form (around 16–24px), ensuring the area feels comfortably readable and consistent. Below this selector, another 32px gap delineates the transition into the contact preferences section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the left main column, fully participating in the vertical flow. It grows vertically as more interest options are present, pushing subsequent content downward while keeping the sidebar unaffected.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact preferences section follows the livestock interests selector in the left main column, separated by roughly 32px of vertical whitespace. It spans the full width of the main column, sharing the same left alignment and right edge as the sections above. Inside, the layout continues the single-column, label-above-input pattern, with each preference (e.g., email notifications, SMS) arranged as its own row and 12–16px vertical spacing between rows. If grouped into subcategories (e.g., Auctions, Messages), each group is separated by a slightly larger internal gap (16–24px) and subtle subheadings. Padding around the content stays within 16–24px, matching the design DNA. A generous 32–40px space is left below the last contact preference group before the area where the save bar visually anchors the end of the form content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the main left column, maintaining the sequential vertical flow. It expands vertically as needed without overlapping the save bar, which appears below it in the same column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visibility toggle is located in the right sidebar, acting as the primary control within that secondary column. It is aligned near the top of the sidebar so that its top edge roughly matches the vertical starting point of the basic profile form in the main column. A modest top margin (around 24–32px) under the header keeps it visually separate from the header but clearly part of the page’s content area. The toggle sits inside a compact block spanning the full width of the sidebar (approximately 30–35% of the overall page width), with 12–16px padding on all sides and a subtle border or light gray background to distinguish it, similar in visual weight to the search field block in the mockup’s auction sidebar. Any label (e.g., “Profile visibility”) appears left-aligned within the block, with the toggle control on the same line to the right or directly below, and 8–12px spacing around it. Below this block, the rest of the sidebar remains open or reserved for additional secondary content, maintaining a clear, uncluttered vertical strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the right sidebar column, following the sidebar’s vertical flow. It stays fixed relative to other sidebar content and scrolls naturally with the page, independent of the save bar’s behavior in the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile save bar anchors the bottom of the main content column, providing clear actions after users review their settings. In the normal flow, it appears directly below the contact preferences section, separated by about 32px of vertical whitespace. Horizontally, it aligns exactly with the main left column: the bar’s left edge lines up with the column’s left margin, and its right edge stops where the sidebar gutter begins, so it does not extend beneath the sidebar. The bar itself is a low-height horizontal strip with 12–16px vertical padding, displaying actions such as “Save Changes” and “Cancel” right-aligned within the bar, with a subtle border on the top edge. On taller viewports or when the user scrolls past the end of the form content, the bar becomes sticky within the main column—gently pinning to the bottom of the viewport while maintaining the same width and horizontal alignment—so that it is always visible while the form is in view. The surrounding page background remains visible above and to the right, retaining the minimalist feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal block element at the bottom of the main column, but with a sticky-on-scroll behavior that fixes it to the bottom of the viewport within the main column’s width once the user has scrolled far enough. It does not overlap the sidebar and releases back into the normal flow when scrolled back up.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Feedback messages appear near the top of the left main column, immediately below the page title region and above the basic profile form. They span the full width of the main column, maintaining the same left and right alignment as the form sections. When present, each alert occupies its own horizontal block with 12–16px internal padding and subtle styling (e.g., light background tint and border) consistent with the minimalist theme. Multiple messages, if needed, are stacked vertically with 8–12px between them, forming a tight but readable cluster. A vertical gap of around 24px separates the bottommost feedback message from the start of the basic profile form, ensuring that the messages feel connected to the subsequent content but not merged into it. The right sidebar begins at a similar vertical level, so the messages visually lead the user’s eye down the main column before the sidebar attracts attention.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline block(s) within the left main column, appearing and disappearing without changing the layout structure beyond pushing the main form content downward when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The unsaved changes guard manifests as a full-page modal overlay when the user attempts to navigate away with pending edits. It dims the entire viewport, including header, main column, and sidebar, with a semi-transparent dark or gray layer. Centered within the viewport is a modest dialog panel—about 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop—aligned horizontally and vertically in the middle. The dialog’s content area uses 24–32px padding on all sides, with a concise title at the top, explanatory text beneath, and action buttons (e.g., “Discard Changes”, “Cancel”, “Save and leave”) arranged horizontally or in a vertical stack at the bottom, separated by 16–24px. The underlying two-column layout remains visible but clearly subdued and non-interactive. When the guard is dismissed, the overlay and dialog fade away, returning focus to the original scroll position in the main layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, full-viewport modal overlay on top of all content (including the loader if triggered later), preventing interaction with underlying elements. It does not alter the scroll position or layout of the underlying page and is centered regardless of scroll offset.

name: Messages
title: Messages
description: Inbox-style page for viewing, managing, and replying to in-site message threads related to auction lots.

core_features: Load and display the user's message threads list using getMessageThreads, including unread counts and last message previews.
Allow filtering the threads list by all messages vs unread-only via the unreadOnly parameter to getMessageThreads.
Allow selecting a message thread (via click or URL threadId parameter) to load its full details and message history using getMessageThreadDetails.
Display thread metadata (subject, counterpart name, related lot title) and link from the lot title to the associated lot detail page.
Render the ordered message history for the selected thread, distinguishing outgoing vs incoming messages.
Allow composing and sending a reply within the selected thread using sendMessageReply, and immediately reflecting the new message in the history and list preview.
Handle empty states (no threads, no thread selected), loading states, and error states for all interface calls.
user_workflows: Open Messages from header: 1) User clicks Messages/Inbox link or icon in header_user_menu. 2) Browser navigates to messages.html (optionally with URL parameters such as unreadOnly or threadId). 3) On page load, Messages page reads URL parameters. 4) Page calls getMessageThreads with unreadOnly derived from URL (or default). 5) Threads list is populated; totalUnreadCount is used to show an overall unread indicator. 6) If a threadId is present in the URL, the page calls getMessageThreadDetails(threadId) and shows that thread as selected.

Open Messages from a lot detail page: 1) On lot_detail.html the user triggers an action that links to messages.html with a threadId parameter for an existing conversation. 2) Browser navigates to messages.html?threadId={threadId}. 3) Messages page loads, calls getMessageThreads with current unreadOnly setting. 4) Messages page then calls getMessageThreadDetails for the threadId from the URL. 5) The specified thread is opened by default, with its history displayed.

Filter threads to unread only: 1) User toggles an 'Unread only' filter control on the Messages page. 2) Page updates the unreadOnly query parameter in the URL accordingly (e.g., unreadOnly=true or unreadOnly=false). 3) Page calls getMessageThreads with the updated unreadOnly value. 4) Threads list refreshes to show only unread threads (or all threads), and totalUnreadCount is updated. 5) If the currently selected thread is filtered out, the detail pane shows an appropriate empty state until the user selects another thread.

Read a conversation: 1) User clicks a thread row in the threads list. 2) Page updates the threadId parameter in the URL to the selected thread's id. 3) Page calls getMessageThreadDetails with the selected thread id. 4) Page displays thread metadata (subject, counterpart_name, lot_title) and ordered message history. 5) User can scroll through messages; backend is assumed to handle marking messages as read when details are fetched. 6) Threads list is updated client-side to reflect reduced unread_count for that thread if applicable.

Reply within a thread: 1) With a thread selected, user types a message into the reply input area. 2) User submits the reply (button click or form submit). 3) Page validates that body is non-empty and then calls sendMessageReply with threadId (from current selection/URL) and body (from the input). 4) If sendMessageReply returns success, page appends sentMessage from the response to the message history, clears the input, and updates the corresponding thread's last_message_preview and last_message_at in the threads list. 5) If sendMessageReply indicates failure, page shows an error notification and leaves the unsent text in the input.

Navigate to related lot details: 1) In the opened thread, the user clicks the lot title in the thread header. 2) Page navigates to lot_detail.html with a lotId URL parameter set from the selected thread's lot_id field. 3) User views the lot details and can later return to messages.html (e.g., via browser back or Messages link) with the prior threadId still in the URL, reopening the same conversation.

Handle absence of threads or selection: 1) If getMessageThreads returns an empty threads array, page shows a 'no messages yet' functional empty state instead of a list. 2) If threads exist but no threadId is in the URL and the user has not yet selected one, page either shows a 'select a conversation' message in the detail area or automatically selects the first thread (and updates threadId in the URL) based on the chosen behavior.
interactions: Toggle unread-only filter: User interacts with a checkbox or toggle control; on change, the page updates the unreadOnly URL parameter, calls getMessageThreads(unreadOnly), and refreshes the threads list and unread count display.

Select thread from list: User clicks on a thread row; the page sets that row as active, updates threadId in the URL, calls getMessageThreadDetails(threadId), and displays the conversation in the detail pane.

Navigate via URL threadId: When the URL already has a threadId parameter (e.g., from lot_detail.html), the page on load uses that value to automatically call getMessageThreadDetails and select the corresponding thread in the list.

Scroll through message history: User scrolls within the message history container; the page only handles client-side scroll, since all messages for the thread are already provided by getMessageThreadDetails.
Compose reply: User types into a multi-line text input bound to the reply body; the page tracks the current input value as pending reply text.

Send reply: User clicks a send button or submits the form; the page validates input, calls sendMessageReply(threadId, body), shows a transient loading state on the button, and then either appends the sent message to history and clears the input on success or displays validation/error feedback on failure.

Open lot details from thread: User clicks the lot title (if present) in the thread header; the page navigates to lot_detail.html?lotId={thread.lot_id}, enabling the user to view the associated AuctionLot.

Refresh threads list: User may trigger a manual refresh control (if present); the page reuses the current unreadOnly state from the URL and calls getMessageThreads(unreadOnly) again, updating list and totalUnreadCount.

Handle API errors: If getMessageThreads, getMessageThreadDetails, or sendMessageReply return errors or unsuccessful results, the page displays an error notification and provides a retry option where applicable.

Handle loading states: While waiting for getMessageThreads or getMessageThreadDetails, the page shows loading indicators in the relevant areas (threads list or detail pane) and disables interactions that depend on the pending data.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Messages page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for view state, without relying on localStorage. On page load, the component reads the current URL for supported parameters: 1) threadId (string, optional): If present, the page treats this as the currently selected thread. After getMessageThreads returns, the threads list highlights the thread whose id matches threadId if it exists. Immediately after or in parallel with the threads call, getMessageThreadDetails(threadId) is invoked to populate the detail pane. If threadId does not match any returned thread, the detail pane shows an error or empty state. When the user selects a different thread from the list, the page updates the threadId parameter in the URL via history.pushState or history.replaceState, keeping the rest of the query parameters (e.g., unreadOnly) unchanged, and then calls getMessageThreadDetails for the new thread id. 2) unreadOnly (boolean-like string, optional): Acceptable URL values can be interpreted as 'true' or 'false'. On load, the page parses this parameter; if absent, the page uses a default (e.g., false) and may reflect that in the URL for consistency. The current unreadOnly value is passed directly into getMessageThreads(unreadOnly) and is also bound to the unread filter toggle in the UI. When the user changes the filter toggle, the page updates the unreadOnly parameter in the URL and then calls getMessageThreads with the new value. If a threadId is present and the selected thread becomes filtered out, the page can either clear threadId from the URL or leave it while showing an appropriate message that the thread is not visible in the current filter; this behavior must be consistent and deterministic. Navigation into the page: 1) From header_user_menu, the link can be messages.html or messages.html with a preset unreadOnly value; on arrival, the page uses whatever parameters are provided. 2) From lot_detail.html, links can include a threadId parameter (e.g., messages.html?threadId={threadId}); on arrival, the page uses that threadId to open the specific conversation. Navigation out of the page: 1) Clicking the lot title in the thread header navigates to lot_detail.html?lotId={thread.lot_id}; this does not change any Messages state itself, but if the user returns via browser back, the previous URL (with its threadId and unreadOnly values) will restore the prior selection and filter state. All stateful behavior—selected thread, filter mode—is either derived from, or persisted back into, the URL parameters so that reloading or sharing the URL preserves the same view.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial load of the Messages page by reading URL parameters, calling getMessageThreads with the correct unreadOnly value, and optionally calling getMessageThreadDetails when a threadId is present. It also manages updating URL parameters when the selected thread or unread filter changes.

Provides controls to filter the message threads list, primarily an 'Unread only' toggle bound to the unreadOnly state. When toggled, it triggers refetching the threads list through getMessageThreads with the corresponding unreadOnly parameter and updates the URL.

Displays the list of message threads returned from getMessageThreads, including subject, counterpart_name, related lot_title (if provided), last_message_preview, last_message_at, and unread_count. It allows selecting a thread to open, and visually indicates unread threads using unread_count.

Represents a single thread row within the threads list, binding to one MessageThread object. It shows key properties such as counterpart_name, subject, lot_title (if available), last_message_preview, last_message_at, and unread_count. It triggers selection of the thread when clicked.

Shows summary information above the threads list, including a count of total threads (derived from the length of the threads array) and totalUnreadCount from getMessageThreads. It reflects changes when the unreadOnly filter is applied or when the threads list is refreshed.

Holds the selected thread's metadata and message history. When a threadId is selected, it triggers getMessageThreadDetails(threadId), manages loading and error states for the detail area, and coordinates updates to child components such as the header and history list.

Displays metadata for the selected thread returned from getMessageThreadDetails.thread, including subject, counterpart_name, created_at, lot_title, and lot_id. Provides a clickable lot_title that navigates to lot_detail.html with lotId set to lot_id for this thread.

Displays the ordered list of Message objects for the selected thread based on getMessageThreadDetails.messages. Messages are rendered with different alignment or styling depending on their direction (outgoing vs incoming). The component supports vertical scrolling and updates as new messages are added (e.g., after a reply is sent).
Represents a single message within the history, showing body and sent_at and styling according to direction. It may also visually indicate read status if is_read is provided and relevant.

Allows the user to compose and send a reply within the currently selected thread. It binds to the current threadId and a text input for body. On submit, it validates input and calls sendMessageReply(threadId, body). On success, it appends the returned sentMessage to the message-history component and updates the corresponding thread's preview fields in the thread-list.

Displays informative messaging when no thread is selected or when there are no messages for a selected thread. It reacts to changes in threadId URL parameter and threads list contents, hiding itself when a valid thread with messages is loaded.
Displays an empty state when getMessageThreads returns an empty threads array. It may include contextual guidance and an optional manual refresh control to retry getMessageThreads.
Shows a loading state over the threads list area while getMessageThreads is in progress, and hides itself when data or an error is returned.
Shows a loading state over the thread detail area while getMessageThreadDetails is in progress for the selected thread, and hides itself when data or an error is returned.

Displays error messages for failed interface calls (getMessageThreads, getMessageThreadDetails, sendMessageReply). It can present inline notifications in the relevant section or a global banner, and provide retry actions tied to the failed operation.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column grid likely based on a 12-column system, with main content occupying approximately 8–9 columns and the right sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Replicate the mockup’s two-column article + sidebar pattern: a slim, full-width header band at the top (handled outside these components), followed by a main content area where the left column is the primary reading surface (thread details and conversation) and the right column is a functional sidebar (thread filters and thread list). The left column hosts the selected message thread: header at the top, history in the middle, reply form at the bottom. The right column starts with a compact filter/search bar, then a label-like summary header, then the scrollable list of threads. Empty states and loading states occupy their respective column regions without changing the overall structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Replicate the mockup’s two-column article + sidebar pattern: a slim, full-width header band at the top (handled outside these components), followed by a main content area where the left column is the primary reading surface (thread details and conversation) and the right column is a functional sidebar (thread filters and thread list). The left column hosts the selected message thread: header at the top, history in the middle, reply form at the bottom. The right column starts with a compact filter/search bar, then a label-like summary header, then the scrollable list of threads. Empty states and loading states occupy their respective column regions without changing the overall structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use a 12-column mental grid. The main thread detail column spans approximately 8–9 columns (about 65–70% of the width), anchored to the left. The thread list sidebar spans about 3–4 columns (30–35%) on the right, separated by a subtle vertical divider or a clear gutter of 24–32px. Vertical spacing between major stacked sections (header vs content area, thread header vs history vs reply form, filters vs list) follows the 32–48px section margin. Internal padding within containers stays around 16–24px on the left column and 12–16px in the right sidebar widgets, consistent with the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately low content density: generous line-height and 8–16px spacing between related text lines, 16–24px gaps between distinct blocks (e.g., between thread list items, between messages). In the sidebar, thread items are single-line or two-line snippets with modest vertical separation to avoid clutter while allowing quick scanning. In the thread detail, messages are clearly separated by whitespace and subtle dividers, prioritizing readability over fitting maximum content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the mockup’s left-first reading pattern. After the global header, the user’s eye lands on the left column’s thread-detail-header (subject/participants), then moves down through the message-history and finally to the reply-form at the bottom. The vertical divider and whitespace then guide the eye to the right sidebar, starting at the thread-filter-bar at the top, then down to the threads-summary-header label and further down the thread-list. At the page level, the flow forms a soft Z from header logo/navigation, down to the left main content, then across to the right sidebar.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: Below the site’s slim global header, the messages page presents a clean two-column layout centered in the viewport with ample white space on the sides. The left two-thirds of the screen is a simple, card-like panel for the currently selected conversation: a narrow thread header strip at the top, a tall vertical stack of messages below it, and a full-width reply form anchored near the bottom, all separated by subtle dividers and consistent padding. To the right, taking up roughly one-third of the width, a narrow sidebar starts with a compact filter/search bar, followed by a labeled ‘Threads’ style summary heading and a long, scrollable list of thread rows that echo the mockup’s auction lot list. A light vertical gutter between the columns and understated gray borders keep the sections visually distinct without adding decoration. When there are no threads or no thread is selected, centered empty-state messages appear within their respective column surfaces, preserving the overall structure. Loading spinners and error notifications appear inline in their appropriate lanes, never disrupting the core two-column hierarchy. The result feels like a straightforward, corporate-style inbox that borrows the same proportions and rhythm as the auction lot page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The messages-page-init component defines the overall page canvas beneath the global header. It introduces a central content container that runs full width on small screens and is constrained to a comfortable max-width (e.g., 1120–1200px) on larger screens, centered horizontally with equal left and right margins. Directly below the header (with about 32px of vertical space), this container splits into two columns: the primary thread detail column on the left occupying roughly 65–70% of the available width, and the sidebar thread list column on the right occupying 30–35%. A consistent horizontal gutter of about 24–32px separates the two columns, optionally with a subtle 1px light gray vertical divider aligned with the right edge of the main column, echoing the mockup’s content vs sidebar separation. On narrow screens (e.g., below tablet breakpoint), the layout stacks vertically: the main thread detail section appears first, spanning 100% width, and the thread list sidebar content appears below it with a clear 32px gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static structural container that establishes the two-column layout; participates in normal page scrolling with no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The thread-filter-bar sits at the very top of the right sidebar column, flush with the top edge of the sidebar’s content area, aligned to the right of the page. It occupies the full width of the sidebar column, inset by 12–16px internal padding on all sides. Visually, it resembles the slim search box from the mockup: a single horizontal bar with a text input (for search or filters) spanning about 80–100% of the sidebar width, optionally accompanied by minimal filter controls aligned to the right within the same row. There is approximately 16px of vertical space above any following content in the sidebar (such as the threads-summary-header), ensuring the filter bar reads as a compact, standalone control strip at the top of the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; scrolls with the page and does not stick, maintaining the simple, linear flow of the design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The thread-list component occupies the main vertical area of the right sidebar column, directly beneath the threads-summary-header. It spans the full usable width of the sidebar (minus standard 12–16px horizontal padding), forming a clean vertical stack of thread-list-items that extend from near the top of the sidebar down towards the bottom of the viewport. Each item is separated by a light gray horizontal divider or 8–12px of vertical whitespace, echoing the mockup’s auction lot list style. On long lists, the entire right column scrolls with the page; the list naturally extends below the fold without internal scroll containers, maintaining a simple, single-scroll experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fills the remaining vertical space in the right column below its header; scrolls as part of the page without its own fixed height or internal scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each thread-list-item is a horizontally full-width row within the sidebar, with 12–16px padding on the left and right and about 12px padding top and bottom. The item height is driven by content but typically sits in the 48–64px range, keeping to a moderately low density. Text content (thread subject, sender name, snippet, or unread indicator) is left-aligned, stacking from top to bottom with 4–8px spacing between lines. The primary line (e.g., subject) appears in a slightly bolder weight, with secondary metadata in a lighter gray tone beneath. A subtle 1px light gray divider line appears along the bottom edge of each item, or a consistent 8px vertical gap separates items, mirroring the clean list style from the auction lots. When an item is selected, it may have a light gray background strip or a thin left border, but still remains visually understated.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard list row within the sidebar; expands in height based on content and pushes adjacent items up or down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The threads-summary-header sits within the right sidebar column, directly below the thread-filter-bar, separated from it by about 16–24px of vertical space. It functions like the 'Auction Lots' heading in the mockup: a simple, single-line text label such as 'Threads' or 'Inbox', left-aligned and occupying the full width of the sidebar’s content area. There is a small 8–12px space between this heading and the first thread-list-item, creating a clear grouping. Optionally, a thin light gray underline can run along the bottom of the header text, subtly framing the start of the list without adding decorative weight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text element within the right column; participates in normal flow, pushing the thread-list downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The thread-detail-container occupies the entire left main column, starting roughly 32px below the global header (or below any page-level error notification if present). It spans about 65–70% of the main content width, with 16–24px internal padding on all sides. Within this container, content is arranged in a strict top-to-bottom order: the thread-detail-header at the top, followed by the message-history occupying most of the vertical space, and the reply-form at the bottom. The container itself does not have heavy borders—just a subtle 1px light gray outline or a bottom divider that stops at the column gutter, keeping with the minimalist corporate feel. On taller viewports, this column extends downward and scrolls as a whole; the reply form remains near the bottom of the content stack rather than being fixed to the viewport edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Main content column; scrolls with the page and adjusts height based on the length of the message history.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The thread-detail-header sits at the very top inside the thread-detail-container, flush with its top padding. It spans the full width of the left column’s inner content and has its own vertical padding of about 16–24px. The primary thread title or subject is left-aligned at the top of this block, with participants, related auction lot info, or timestamps arranged beneath as label-value lines with 4–8px spacing, mimicking the auction lot detail text stacking from the mockup. A thin light gray divider line runs along the bottom edge of this header, separating it from the message-history below. There is minimal vertical gap (around 8–12px) between this divider and the first message in the history, keeping the header visually connected to the conversation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the left column; does not stick when scrolling, letting the conversation scroll naturally underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The message-history block sits directly below the thread-detail-header within the left column, separated by a small 8–12px vertical gap or simply the header’s bottom divider. It occupies the majority of the vertical space in the thread-detail-container, extending downward as each message-item is added. Messages are stacked in chronological order from top to bottom, each separated by 16–24px of vertical space or by a subtle horizontal rule, achieving the low-to-moderate density reading experience seen in the mockup’s paragraphs. The message-history spans the full inner width of the column (minus padding), keeping text aligned to the same left edge as the header content. The bottom of this block sits about 24–32px above the reply-form, giving a clear visual break before the input area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands in height based on the number of messages; scrolls with the rest of the left column content without internal scrollbars.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each message-item is a text block within the message-history, aligned to the left and occupying the full width of the content area. It has 8–16px of padding above and below its main content, with the sender name and timestamp on the top line or top two lines in a slightly smaller or lighter gray font, followed by the message body text below with 4–8px of line spacing. The body text wraps in a single column and can span multiple lines, respecting generous line height for legibility. Messages from different participants can be distinguished subtly (e.g., by a slightly bolder sender name), but the visual style remains minimalist with no heavy chat bubbles—just text blocks separated by whitespace or a very faint divider. Each message-item sits about 16–24px away from the next, maintaining a steady reading rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows vertically within the message-history; height is content-driven, pushing subsequent messages downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reply-form is positioned at the bottom of the thread-detail-container, directly beneath the message-history with about 24–32px of vertical separation. It spans the full width of the left column’s content area and is enclosed by a subtle top border line in light gray, echoing the structural dividers seen elsewhere. Inside, there is 16–24px of padding. A multi-line text area occupies most of the width, left-aligned and extending horizontally almost edge-to-edge, with about 8–12px of space above it for any label or placeholder text. Below and/or to the right of the textarea, the primary action button (e.g., 'Send') is aligned to the right edge, with any secondary actions to its left. The overall height of the reply-form is moderate, avoiding dominance over the message-history while still offering comfortable input space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard form element at the bottom of the left column’s content stack; scrolls with the page and does not remain fixed at the bottom of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The detail-empty-state occupies the same left column area as the thread-detail-container’s usual content whenever no thread is selected or when there are no messages for the selected thread. It is centered within the left column both horizontally and, as much as reasonable, vertically within the visible portion of the column. The empty-state content (icon, heading, and short explanatory text) is wrapped in a simple block with 16–24px internal padding, but no heavy border. There is ample whitespace around it, particularly above and below (32–48px), so it feels like a neutral placeholder rather than a dense message. The text is center-aligned within this block, matching the understated, informative tone of the rest of the interface.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in place of the standard thread-detail-header, message-history, and reply-form when applicable; otherwise hidden. It does not alter the two-column layout or column proportions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The threads-empty-state lives within the right sidebar column, occupying the same area typically used by the threads-summary-header and thread-list when no threads are available. It appears below the thread-filter-bar, with about 24–32px of vertical space above it. The message is centered horizontally within the sidebar column, with text lines stacked and spaced by 8–12px, and surrounded by generous whitespace on all sides to keep it airy. There is no need for additional framing beyond perhaps a soft icon above the text, staying within the minimalist language. The filter bar remains visible above, reinforcing that this area belongs to the thread listing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the threads-summary-header and thread-list when active but stays within the right column, respecting its width and padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator-threads is positioned inside the right sidebar column in the area where the thread-list would normally appear. It is centered horizontally and placed near the upper-middle of the sidebar’s visible area, just below the thread-filter-bar (and optionally the threads-summary-header if still shown). The indicator itself is small and unobtrusive—such as a spinner icon with a short text label below—surrounded by at least 24–32px of clear space in all directions so it does not appear cramped. Underlying list content is either hidden or visually dimmed while loading, keeping focus on the indicator.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Only visible while thread data is loading; occupies inline space within the right column and scrolls normally if the page is scrolled.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator-details appears within the left thread-detail-container area whenever the selected thread’s messages are being fetched. It is centered horizontally and placed roughly in the upper-middle portion of the left column’s visible area, replacing the message-history and reply-form content during loading. There is ample whitespace around the indicator, mirroring the thread-detail empty state. If a thread-detail-header is already known (e.g., subject and participants), it can remain visible above, with about 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the loading indicator, preserving the page’s structural hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Visible only while the detailed thread content is loading; sits within the normal flow of the left column and scrolls with the page if necessary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-notification appears as a slim horizontal bar spanning the full width of the main content container, immediately below the global site header and above the two-column messages content. It has 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, with error text left-aligned and possibly a dismiss icon on the far right. The background is a light, desaturated error color but kept subtle to match the utilitarian style, with a simple 1px border along the bottom edge. There is about 16–24px of vertical space between this notification bar and the start of the messages columns, so it feels distinct yet connected to the page. If multiple errors appear, the latest one replaces the previous, preserving the clean layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits directly under the header in the normal document flow; pushes the columns downward when present and disappears without leaving gaps when dismissed, without fixed or sticky behavior.

name: About
title: About
description: Read-only informational page that loads and displays dynamic About content for the livestock auction marketplace using a single content API.

core_features: Load About page content (title, sections, lastUpdated) from the getAboutPageContent interface on initial page load.
Display the About page title and optionally synchronize the document title with the API-provided title.
Render all About sections returned by getAboutPageContent in order, each with a heading and body.
Provide in-page navigation generated dynamically from the loaded sections, allowing users to jump directly to a specific section.
Support deep-linking to a specific About section via a "section" URL parameter (e.g., about.html?section=derived-section-slug) and automatically scroll to that section after content is loaded.
Reflect in-page navigation selection in the URL by updating the "section" parameter without reloading the page, enabling sharable links.
Display the last updated information from getAboutPageContent so users know how current the About information is.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Basic viewing of About information
1. User clicks the "About" link in the footer navigation (which navigates to about.html without any URL parameters).
2. The page initializes and triggers the about-page-init controller component.
3. The controller calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters.
4. Once the promise resolves, the controller stores the response (title, sections, lastUpdated) in page state.
5. The about-header component binds to the title and lastUpdated fields and renders them at the top of the page.
6. The about-section-list component binds to the sections array and renders each section (heading + body) in sequence.
7. The about-inpage-nav component binds to the same sections array, derives navigation items from the headings, and renders an in-page navigation list.
8. The user scrolls through the content; the page is read-only and does not modify any server data.

Workflow 2: Deep-link to a specific section via URL parameter
1. From another page (e.g., a help or FAQ page), the user follows a link to about.html with a "section" URL parameter (for example, about.html?section=<section-slug-derived-elsewhere>).
2. The page initializes and the about-page-init controller parses the current URL for the "section" parameter value (desiredSectionSlug).
3. The controller calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters.
4. After the response arrives, the controller normalizes each section heading into a slug (e.g., lowercased, spaces replaced with dashes, non-URL-safe characters removed) and associates that slug with the section in client-side state.
5. The about-section-list component renders all sections.
6. The controller compares desiredSectionSlug to the computed slugs of each section. If a match is found, the page programmatically scrolls to the corresponding section element.
7. The about-inpage-nav component highlights the matched section as active, based on the same slug comparison.
8. If no matching section slug is found, the page remains at the top of the About content without error, and no section is highlighted.

Workflow 3: Navigating between sections using in-page navigation
1. The user is on about.html (with or without an existing "section" URL parameter) and sees the in-page navigation list generated from section headings.
2. The user clicks a navigation item corresponding to a section.
3. The about-inpage-nav component intercepts the click event.
4. The component determines the slug for the target section based on the bound section heading.
5. The component updates the browser URL using the History API to set or replace the "section" query parameter with the selected section's slug, without triggering a full page reload.
6. The component requests the about-page-scroll-manager (or similar logic within the controller) to scroll to the target section element in the DOM.
7. The in-page navigation updates its active state to reflect the newly selected section.
8. The user can copy the URL (now containing the updated "section" parameter) and share it; when another user opens it, Workflow 2 executes.

Workflow 4: Refreshing the page while viewing a specific section
1. The user is on about.html with a "section" query parameter in the URL and is currently viewing a specific section.
2. The user refreshes the browser or reopens the same URL in a new tab, preserving the URL (including the "section" parameter).
3. The page reloads, triggers about-page-init, and re-parses the "section" parameter from the URL.
4. The controller calls getAboutPageContent and receives the title, sections, and lastUpdated.
5. Section slugs are recomputed from the headings as in Workflow 2.
6. When the section matching logic finds a slug equal to the "section" parameter, the page scrolls directly to that section and highlights it in the in-page navigation.
7. From the user's perspective, the page state (which section is in focus) is preserved across the refresh by using URL parameters and dynamic content rather than local storage.
interactions: Footer Navigation Click: When the user clicks the global footer "About" link, the browser navigates to about.html (optionally with a "section" query parameter); no client-side handler on the originating page is required beyond standard navigation.

Initial Page Load: On DOM ready or equivalent lifecycle event, the about-page-init component calls getAboutPageContent, then propagates the returned data to about-header, about-section-list, and about-inpage-nav components via shared page state.

In-Page Navigation Item Click: When the user clicks an item in the in-page navigation list, an onClick handler prevents default behavior if necessary, derives the slug of the target section, updates the "section" query parameter in the URL using the History API, scrolls to the corresponding section, and updates the navigation's active state.

URL Parameter Change (Manual): If the user manually edits the URL's "section" parameter and presses Enter, the page reloads; on reload, about-page-init re-parses the parameter and performs the same section focus logic as in a normal deep link.

Scroll-based Active State (Optional extension): As the user scrolls through the About content, a scroll event handler can observe which section is currently in view and update the active navigation item to match, without changing the URL unless desired.

Last Updated Display: The about-header component reads the lastUpdated field from the getAboutPageContent response and displays it in a non-editable field or text area; this is purely informational and does not support interaction aside from being read.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The About page is read-only and does not maintain any persistent user-specific state. All dynamic behavior is derived from the getAboutPageContent interface response and the current URL. No localStorage, sessionStorage, or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used.

URL Parameters:
- section (optional, string): Used to indicate which About section should be emphasized or scrolled into view. The value is a slug derived from a section's heading text.

State Behavior:
1. On every page load, the controller parses the current URL for a "section" query parameter. If present, its value is stored temporarily in page state as desiredSectionSlug.
2. The controller calls getAboutPageContent and receives an object with title, sections, and lastUpdated.
3. The controller computes a slug for each section based on its heading (for example, by lowercasing the heading, trimming whitespace, replacing spaces with hyphens, and removing or encoding characters that are not URL-safe). These computed slugs are not hardcoded and are computed for every response.
4. After content and slugs are available, the controller compares desiredSectionSlug (from the URL) to the computed slugs:
   - If a match is found, the controller scrolls to that section and sets that section as the active one in the in-page navigation.
   - If no match is found or if no "section" parameter is present, the page remains scrolled to the top of the About content, and no section is pre-selected as active.
5. When the user clicks an item in the in-page navigation, the click handler derives the slug for the target section from its heading and updates the "section" query parameter in the URL using history.pushState or history.replaceState. This ensures the URL always reflects the user's current section selection, without requiring a full reload.
6. If the URL changes due to navigation (e.g., the user clicks Back/Forward in the browser and the "section" parameter changes), the controller listens for the popstate event, re-reads the "section" parameter, and scrolls to the appropriate section using the already-loaded content.
7. The document title may optionally be synchronized with the API's title field. For example, the page can set the browser tab title to the value returned by getAboutPageContent. This is a one-way binding and does not require or allow user modification.
8. No state from the About page is propagated to or stored in any server-side models (AuctionLot, ProfileSettings, etc.), and no unrelated data models are accessed from this page.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial loading of About content, parsing of URL parameters, computation of section slugs, and orchestration of scrolling and active-section highlighting. It is responsible for calling getAboutPageContent when the page loads or when a full navigation occurs.
Displays the About page title and last updated information using the data returned by getAboutPageContent. Optionally updates the document title based on the bound title field.

Renders all sections from the getAboutPageContent response in order. Each section is rendered with its heading and body. The component exposes identifiers or DOM references for each section so that the controller or navigation component can scroll to them.

Generates an in-page navigation menu from the About sections. It derives display labels from section headings and section identifiers from computed slugs. Clicking an item updates the "section" query parameter in the URL and requests scrolling to the corresponding section.

Provides logic for scrolling to specific sections based on their computed slug or index. It is invoked by the controller on initial load (if a "section" parameter is present) and by the in-page navigation on user clicks.

Manages synchronization between the current section selection and the URL's "section" query parameter. It reads the parameter on load and on popstate, and writes the parameter when navigation items are clicked.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column grid likely based on a 12-column system, with main content occupying approximately 8–9 columns and the right sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global site header (logo, nav, search icon), the About page uses the same classic two-column layout as the auction lot mockup. A main content column sits on the left and holds the About page header followed by a vertically stacked list of text sections (pulled from the single content API). On the right, a slimmer sidebar column holds an in-page navigation list that mirrors the sections on the left. Invisible controller/behavior components sit behind these visible elements and do not occupy any visual space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global site header (logo, nav, search icon), the About page uses the same classic two-column layout as the auction lot mockup. A main content column sits on the left and holds the About page header followed by a vertically stacked list of text sections (pulled from the single content API). On the right, a slimmer sidebar column holds an in-page navigation list that mirrors the sections on the left. Invisible controller/behavior components sit behind these visible elements and do not occupy any visual space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header remains a slim full-width band at the very top. The primary content area below is centered in the viewport with a max-width container and a consistent horizontal gutter. Within this container, the left main column occupies roughly 65–70% of the content width, while the right sidebar (in-page nav) takes 30–35%. The About header sits close to the top of the left column with a margin of about 32–40px below the global header. The in-page nav aligns vertically with the About header in the right column and uses slightly tighter internal padding than the main content, reflecting the original sidebar widget styling.
layout_strategies.content_density: Text is moderately spaced: comfortable line height, clear paragraph breaks, and 24–32px vertical separation between major sections in the About content. The in-page nav links are single-line items with modest vertical spacing (8–12px) between links, creating a tidy but not sparse list. Overall density mirrors the functional, content-first feeling of the mockup: everything is easy to scan without appearing decorative or overly airy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the global header’s left-to-right scan (logo → nav → search icon), the user’s eye is drawn to the About page header at the top-left of the main content column. From there, the reading flow is top-to-bottom through the stacked About sections. A vertical gutter (and optionally a subtle light-gray divider) between the main column and the sidebar then guides the eye to the right-side in-page navigation, which is scanned top-to-bottom. Interaction-wise, the flow is: choose a section from the right nav, scroll comfortably to that section on the left, with the behavior and state components keeping the nav and URL in sync without adding visual noise.

layout.overall_description: The About page sits beneath the same slim, full-width global header used on the auction listing pages. Below this header, a centered content container spans most of the viewport width, divided into a left main column and a right sidebar in a 70/30 proportion. The left main column begins with a simple, bold 'About' page header set near the top, followed directly by vertically stacked text sections describing the marketplace, its mission, processes, and any other informational content. Each section reads like a clean blog article block, separated by consistent vertical spacing so the page feels readable and organized rather than dense. To the right, separated by a clear gutter and possibly a subtle vertical divider line, the sidebar hosts an in-page navigation box that lists each About section as a simple text link, aligned from top to bottom. The nav starts level with the About header and can remain visible as the user scrolls, echoing the mockup’s practical, utility-first sidebar. Behind the scenes, non-visual components manage data loading, smooth scrolling, and URL hash synchronization, but visually the page remains very minimal: white background, light-gray dividers, and straightforward black/gray typography in a classic two-column, content-first layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component does not render any visible UI and therefore does not occupy explicit screen space. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the About page’s hierarchy, initializing data fetches from the single content API before the visible components (about-header, about-section-list, about-inpage-nav) are displayed. In layout terms, it is positioned logically above the main content container that houses the two-column layout, but it does not affect spacing, alignment, or the visual arrangement of any elements on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, participates only in application logic; no direct spatial behavior in the rendered layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The About header sits at the top-left of the main content area, directly beneath the global site header. Inside a centered page container, it is aligned to the left edge of the main (left) column, occupying the full width of that column. Horizontally, there is a shared gutter of approximately 24–32px between the end of this column and the right sidebar to its right. Vertically, the top of the about-header is positioned about 32–40px below the bottom edge of the global header, echoing the slim separation seen in the mockup. The header itself (e.g., 'About' plus any short subtitle) has internal padding around 16–24px if it is rendered as a block, but visually it should feel minimal: a bold title text on a white background, possibly with a light-gray bottom border or simple spacing underneath. Immediately below this header, the about-section-list begins, maintaining a continuous, linear flow in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in normal document flow, anchoring the top of the left content column. It scrolls away with the page content and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-list occupies the full width of the left main column directly beneath the about-header. It follows a pure top-to-bottom flow: each section (e.g., 'Our Story', 'How the Marketplace Works', 'For Sellers', 'For Buyers') appears as a vertically stacked block of text. Each block spans roughly 65–70% of the overall page width (matching the main column size) with generous left and right padding or margins that keep text lines comfortably narrow for reading. Vertical spacing between sections is approximately 24–32px, aligning with the spacing system and mirroring the paragraph rhythm from the auction lot details in the mockup. There is no complex card framing—just white background, light-gray divider lines between some sections if needed, and clear typographic hierarchy (section titles followed by paragraph text). This list extends downward and defines the overall scroll length of the page. On larger screens, the right edge of these sections lines up vertically with the left edge of the sidebar gutter, maintaining a consistent, invisible alignment grid between main content and in-page nav.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the main left column in normal flow, expanding vertically with its content. On narrower viewports (e.g., mobile), this list naturally becomes full-width, with the right sidebar stacking above or below it according to the responsive rules, but without any change in its top-to-bottom content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The in-page navigation sits in the right sidebar column, visually mirroring the auction lots list from the mockup but tailored to the About sections. It is aligned to the top of the sidebar so that its top edge roughly matches the top edge of the about-header across the gutter. Horizontally, the sidebar occupies about 30–35% of the page’s content width, starting after a clear gutter of approximately 24–32px to the right of the main column. Optionally, a subtle vertical light-gray divider line can run between the main content and this sidebar, reinforcing the separation while retaining the minimalist look. Inside the sidebar, the nav content is wrapped in a simple box with 12–16px internal padding and no heavy borders—just a small heading (e.g., 'On this page') at the top and a single-column list of links below it. Each link corresponds to a section in about-section-list, set as a single text line with about 8–12px vertical spacing between links. The list extends downwards but typically remains shorter than the main content height, creating a compact, scan-friendly block of navigation items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned in the right column of a two-column layout, with potential sticky behavior: on larger screens, it can become sticky relative to the viewport—after the initial scroll, it stays pinned near the top (e.g., 80–96px from the top to clear the global header) while the left-side content scrolls. On small screens, it may drop into normal flow (stacking above or below the main content) and lose the sticky behavior to preserve vertical reading comfort.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This behavior helper does not render any visible UI and therefore has no direct spatial footprint. It conceptually sits alongside the about-section-list and about-inpage-nav, orchestrating how the viewport moves between them. When a user clicks a link in the about-inpage-nav on the right, this component ensures that the corresponding section in the left column scrolls into view with smooth motion and appropriate offset beneath the global header and about-header, without introducing any extra visible elements or changing the layout of existing content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and layered logically above the content in terms of behavior. It influences scroll positioning relative to the fixed global header and any sticky behavior of the in-page nav but does not affect the structural arrangement, sizing, or spacing of elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-sync component is completely invisible within the page and does not consume layout space. It conceptually wraps around the about-section-list and about-inpage-nav to keep the browser’s URL (e.g., hash or query parameter) aligned with the currently active section. As users scroll through the About sections on the left or click items in the navigation on the right, this component updates the URL state in the background so the page can be shared or reloaded at a particular section, while the visual layout remains unchanged and minimal.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavior-only layer; it does not alter the physical layout, alignment, or spacing of UI components. Its spatial influence is indirect, affecting only which section is considered 'active' for purposes like highlighting the corresponding nav link.

name: Help & How It Works
title: Help & How It Works
description: Provides dynamically loaded guidance, FAQs, and troubleshooting content for using the livestock and cattle auction site, with deep links to specific help sections via URL parameters.

core_features: Load structured help content (sections, FAQs, last-updated metadata) via the getHelpPageContent interface.
Display a list of help sections that users can expand and navigate via a table of contents, with each section identified by a stable section id.
Support deep-linking to specific help sections using the sectionId URL parameter and automatically scroll to the matching section after content loads.
Render a FAQ area using the faqs collection from getHelpPageContent, with expandable/collapsible answers.
Provide contextual navigation to contact.html for support, pre-filling the topic URL parameter for support-related queries.
Offer client-side filtering of help sections and FAQs based on a user-entered search term, without additional interfaces.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Open Help page from footer
1. User clicks the global footer link to help.html.
2. The page initializes and invokes getHelpPageContent with no parameters.
3. Once the response arrives, sections and FAQs are stored in page state and bound to section and FAQ components.
4. The table of contents displays all section titles; the main content area renders all sections and FAQs.
5. If there is no sectionId URL parameter, the page remains at the top of the content.

Workflow: Arrive with a specific help topic (sectionId) from another page
1. User clicks a contextual help link (for example, from auctions.html) that navigates to help.html with a sectionId query parameter.
2. Help page initializes, reads the sectionId value from the URL, and calls getHelpPageContent.
3. After content is loaded, the page finds the section whose id matches sectionId.
4. If a match is found, the page scrolls to that section and marks it as the active/selected section in page state.
5. If no matching id is found, the page stays at the top and keeps no section selected.

Workflow: Navigate between help sections using the table of contents
1. User clicks a section entry in the table of contents.
2. The click handler updates the sectionId parameter in the URL (e.g., help.html?sectionId={clickedId}) using navigation or history APIs.
3. Page state updates the currently selected section id and scrolls to the associated section element.
4. The active section is visually distinguished by the application’s presentation layer (outside the scope of this spec).

Workflow: Use help search to find guidance
1. User types a query into the help search input and submits.
2. The search component reads the query and updates internal state, optionally also updating a query URL parameter to allow bookmarking.
3. The sections list component filters sections based on the query (e.g., matching the text contained in the title and/or body fields from getHelpPageContent).
4. The FAQ component performs similar client-side filtering of faqs.
5. User can clear the query to restore the full, unfiltered help and FAQ lists.

Workflow: Contact support from a help article
1. Within any help section or FAQ answer, user clicks a support link.
2. The click handler navigates to contact.html with the topic URL parameter set to support (e.g., contact.html?topic=support), optionally including the current sectionId in the URL if desired by the broader application.
3. The user is taken to the contact page, where they can submit a support request.
interactions: On page load, the help-content-loader component triggers getHelpPageContent once, manages loading/error state in memory, and passes the resulting sections, faqs, and lastUpdated data to child components.

When the URL contains a sectionId parameter, the section-anchor-handler component observes it and, after content is loaded, scrolls to the section element whose id matches that value and sets it as active.
Clicking a section entry in the table of contents updates the URL’s sectionId parameter, sets the clicked section as active in state, and scrolls to its content block.
Submitting the help search form updates an internal searchQuery state (and optionally a query URL parameter), and triggers sections and FAQ lists to re-render in a filtered mode.
Clicking a FAQ question row toggles expansion/collapse of the corresponding answer in the faq-accordion component, with open/closed state stored in memory keyed by FAQ index or a derived identifier.
Clicking a 'Contact support' action within any section or FAQ invokes a navigation handler that routes to contact.html with topic=support in the URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is derived from and synchronized with URL parameters and API responses. On initial load, the page reads the sectionId query parameter from the URL, if present. It then calls getHelpPageContent and stores the returned sections, faqs, and lastUpdated in memory. Once content is available, if sectionId is defined and matches a section.id from the API response, the page scrolls to that section and sets it as active; otherwise, no section is auto-selected. When a user clicks a section link in the table of contents, the page updates the URL's sectionId parameter (without requiring a full page reload) and updates the active section in state to keep the URL and UI in sync. If the application chooses to also expose the current help search term in the URL, an additional query parameter (for example, query) can be read on load and written on search submission; when present, it is used to filter sections and FAQs client-side. All state related to content (sections, faqs, lastUpdated) originates exclusively from the getHelpPageContent interface; no hardcoded help content or FAQs are embedded in the page.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getHelpPageContent, stores the returned sections, faqs, and lastUpdated values in page-level state, and exposes them to descendant components. Manages loading and error states for the help content.
event_handlers: onLoad: Triggered when help.html is loaded; invokes getHelpPageContent and initializes state.
onContentLoaded: Fired when getHelpPageContent resolves; notifies section-anchor-handler and other components that content is ready.
onError: Handles failures from getHelpPageContent by updating an error state that other components can render through an application-level messaging/notification system.

components.functionality: Synchronizes the sectionId URL parameter with the currently active section in the UI. After content loads, scrolls to and highlights the section whose id matches sectionId, if any.
event_handlers: onUrlChange: Observes changes to the URL query string; when sectionId changes, updates activeSectionId in page state and scrolls to the matching section element, if present.
onContentLoaded: After sections are rendered, checks the current sectionId parameter and performs the initial scroll/highlight if it matches a section.id.
onActiveSectionChange: When user-driven navigation changes the active section, updates the URL’s sectionId parameter to keep it in sync.

components.functionality: Displays a navigable list of help sections as a table of contents. Each entry corresponds to a section from getHelpPageContent.sections and allows the user to jump directly to that section. Indicates which section is currently active based on page state.
event_handlers: onClickSectionLink: When a section entry is clicked, sets pageState.activeSectionId to the corresponding sections[i].id, updates URLSearchParams.sectionId accordingly, and triggers a scroll to the associated section in the content area.

components.functionality: Renders all help sections returned by getHelpPageContent in the main content area. Each section is rendered with an element id that matches the section.id value so that it can be targeted for scrolling and highlighting. Supports optional filtering based on a search query.
event_handlers: onSectionInView: When a section enters the viewport (for example, from user scrolling), optionally updates pageState.activeSectionId to that section’s id, which can in turn update the URL’s sectionId if the application uses scroll-based syncing.
onSearchQueryChange: Reacts to updates in pageState.searchQuery by filtering which sections are rendered or by emphasizing matching text within section titles/bodies.

components.functionality: Provides a text input and submit/reset interactions to filter help sections and FAQs. The search is executed client-side against the in-memory sections and faqs returned from getHelpPageContent.
event_handlers: onSubmit: Reads the entered search text, updates pageState.searchQuery, and optionally updates a query URL parameter so the filtered view can be shared or bookmarked.
onChange: Updates a live searchQuery state as the user types, enabling instant filtering if desired by the application.
onClear: Resets pageState.searchQuery (and any associated query URL parameter), restoring the full list of sections and FAQs.

components.functionality: Displays frequently asked questions and their answers from getHelpPageContent.faqs in an accordion-like structure. Each FAQ can be expanded or collapsed. Supports optional filtering based on the help search query.
event_handlers: onClickFaqHeader: Toggles the expanded/collapsed state for the clicked FAQ by updating pageState.openFaqIds (for example, adding or removing an index or derived identifier).
onSearchQueryChange: Filters visible FAQs according to pageState.searchQuery, using a matching strategy defined by application logic (e.g., matching on question and/or answer text).

components.functionality: Displays the lastUpdated value returned by getHelpPageContent, so users know when the help content was last refreshed. The raw string is rendered or formatted according to application-level localization rules.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: When getHelpPageContent resolves, reads lastUpdated and displays or formats it according to the application’s date/time formatting utilities.

components.functionality: Provides a reusable control within help sections and FAQs that navigates the user to the support contact page, passing the required topic support parameter and optionally including the current help context via URL parameters.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigates to contact.html with topic=support in the URL (e.g., contact.html?topic=support). The handler may also append contextual parameters such as the current sectionId or other identifiers as needed by the wider application, but it must always include topic=support as specified.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column grid likely based on a 12-column system, with main content occupying approximately 8–9 columns and the right sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Replicate the mockup’s classic blog + sidebar pattern: a full-width, slim header on top, then a two-column content area. The Help & How It Works content (title, metadata, sections, FAQ) forms a vertically stacked main column on the left. The right sidebar holds utility elements related to navigation within help content (Help search, table of contents, contact link), stacked from top to bottom. Non-visual controllers (loader, anchor handler) sit logically around the main content but do not occupy visible space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Replicate the mockup’s classic blog + sidebar pattern: a full-width, slim header on top, then a two-column content area. The Help & How It Works content (title, metadata, sections, FAQ) forms a vertically stacked main column on the left. The right sidebar holds utility elements related to navigation within help content (Help search, table of contents, contact link), stacked from top to bottom. Non-visual controllers (loader, anchor handler) sit logically around the main content but do not occupy visible space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Above the fold, the header occupies a shallow horizontal strip. Below, the main area is split into an 8–9/12 width left column and a 3–4/12 width right sidebar. Within the left column, the page title and last-updated metadata sit near the top with modest padding (16–24px), followed by a tall vertical stack of help sections and the FAQ accordion. In the right sidebar, the help-specific search form is a compact block at the top, with the Help TOC taking most of the remaining sidebar height, and the contact-support link anchored visually near the bottom. Vertical spacing between major blocks follows the 32–48px rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately low density: generous line height for text, clear 16–24px padding inside content blocks, and 24–32px gaps between major sections. Lists in the TOC and FAQ accordion are single-line or short multi-line items with modest 8–16px separation, mirroring the sidebar link list in the mockup—functional and readable, never cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first hits the left-side page title, then drops slightly to the last-updated metadata and continues down through the help sections and FAQ, following a straightforward vertical reading flow. Once the primary content is scanned, the vertical divider/whitespace naturally shifts attention rightwards to the help search and TOC, which are then scanned top-to-bottom. The header still provides a subtle initial left-to-right scan (logo, nav, global search icon), forming a soft Z-pattern at the very top before the focus settles into the left column.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a white canvas with a very clean, WordPress-like two-column layout. At the very top, a slim full-width header band holds the site logo at the far left, a row of navigation links centered, and a global search icon at the far right. Below this, a light gray horizontal divider separates the header from the main Help content. The main area is centered in the viewport with comfortable horizontal padding. On the left, taking roughly two-thirds of the width, the page title “Help & How It Works” appears in a bold but not oversized type, flush left with 24px padding above and below. Just beneath it, a small gray “Last updated” line sits close to the title, indicating data freshness without overwhelming the page. Under that, the main help sections are stacked vertically: each section heading and its explanatory text fill the left column in a linear flow, with 32–48px gaps between sections. Below these sections, the FAQ accordion appears as another clean text block, still aligned to the left column, each question row separated by light dividers and generous line height. On the right, the sidebar starts near the same vertical line as the title: at the top is a compact Help-specific search form with a single-line input spanning most of the sidebar’s width and a small search button or icon aligned to the right edge. A subtle divider or extra whitespace separates this from the “Help Topics” TOC list, which is a vertical list of section links mirroring the structure of the left column. Each TOC item is a single line, left-aligned, with small 8–12px vertical gaps. Near the bottom of the sidebar, slightly separated from the TOC, a simple “Contact Support” link is placed as a final escalation path, aligned with the other sidebar content. The entire layout is minimalist, with a strong top-to-bottom reading experience in the left column and a supporting, secondary navigation role in the right column.
