component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-content-loader is a non-visual backbone component that conceptually sits just inside the main content area, above the visible help-section-list and faq-accordion in the document hierarchy. It spans the full width of the left main column (approximately 65–70% of the page width) but does not render any visible UI elements. Functionally, it controls what content appears in the help-section-list and faq-accordion based on URL parameters or other state. It should be treated as occupying zero visual space: the left column’s visible layout starts with the page title, last-updated indicator, then the loaded sections below, all laid out as if the loader is not there.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, occupies no layout space; logically bound to the left main column and scrolls with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-anchor-handler is also non-visual and exists to synchronize the URL hash or query parameters with the scroll position of the help-section-list and faq-accordion. It can be conceptually placed near or around those components in the DOM, but it should not introduce any padding, margins, or visible elements. The left column’s spatial arrangement—title at the top, followed by metadata, then sections and FAQ—remains uninterrupted; the handler only listens to and updates anchor and scroll behavior behind the scenes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; occupies no visible space and simply tracks/controls scroll and anchor behavior for left-column sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-toc (table of contents) is positioned in the right sidebar, beginning just below the help-search-form with a clear vertical gap of about 24–32px. It is left-aligned within the sidebar and spans roughly 90–100% of the sidebar width, leaving small horizontal padding (16–24px) from the outer page edge. At the top of this block, a simple heading like “Help Topics” appears in medium-weight text, followed by a vertical list of clickable section titles. Each TOC item is a single line (or wraps to two lines at most) with 8–12px of vertical spacing between items, and no bullets, matching the clean auction lot link list from the mockup. The TOC occupies most of the vertical space in the sidebar, running parallel to the central portion of the main content on the left. When the page scrolls, the TOC scrolls naturally with the sidebar, maintaining its proportional relationship to the left column without sticking or floating.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar; scrolls with the page, occupying a block from below the help-search-form down toward the contact-support-link.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page for viewing support contact details and submitting a support request about auctions, listings, or account issues.

core_features: Retrieve and display current support contact details (email, phone, hours, mailing address) using getContactSupportInfo.
Provide a contact form that allows users to submit a support request with name, email, topic, subject, and message via submitContactSupportRequest.
Pre-fill the contact form topic field using the optional URL parameter `topic` when coming from help.html or other pages.
Display submission feedback (success or error) including a reference ticket ID returned by submitContactSupportRequest.
Prevent duplicate submissions and indicate in-progress state while a support request is being submitted.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Viewing support contact details
1. User navigates to contact.html (e.g., via the footer link).
2. On page initialization, the page calls getContactSupportInfo with no parameters.
3. The response values (supportEmail, supportPhone, supportHours, mailingAddress) are bound to the contact-support-info-panel component.
4. The user can use the displayed email or phone (e.g., via mailto/tel links) as an alternative to the form.
5. If getContactSupportInfo fails, the panel shows a non-specific error state and hides any incomplete data (no hardcoded fallback values).

Workflow: Contacting support from a help topic with preselected topic
1. User clicks a "Contact support" link on help.html that navigates to contact.html with a URL such as contact.html?topic=<topic-value>.
2. On initialization, the contact-support-form component reads the `topic` URL parameter.
3. If a `topic` value is present, the form’s topic field initial value is set to that parameter value.
4. The form scrolls into view and the first editable field (e.g., name) is focused to encourage immediate input.
5. The user may keep or change the topic value before submitting.

Workflow: Submitting a new support request
1. User fills in the contact-support-form fields: name, email, topic (pre-filled from URL or entered manually), subject, and message.
2. On clicking the submit button, the form runs client-side validation (e.g., required fields, basic email format) without using localStorage.
3. If validation passes, the form enters a "submitting" state: the submit button is disabled and a pending indicator is shown.
4. The page calls submitContactSupportRequest with parameters: name, email, topic, subject, message, taken directly from the current form state.
5. When the response returns:
   - If `success` is true, the contact-form-status-banner component displays the success message and ticketId. The form may optionally clear its fields while retaining the topic value, or keep all values based on product decision.
   - If `success` is false, the status banner displays the returned message as an error, and the form leaves all field values unchanged for correction.
6. The "submitting" state ends and the submit button becomes clickable again.

Workflow: Correcting and resubmitting after an error
1. If submitContactSupportRequest returns `success: false`, the contact-form-status-banner shows an error message.
2. The user reviews the message content and edits the relevant form fields (for example, clarifying the message or adjusting email format).
3. The user clicks submit again; the form repeats validation and, if valid, resends the request using submitContactSupportRequest.
4. On success, the status banner updates with the new success message and ticketId.

Workflow: Navigating to Contact and back to help or other pages
1. User navigates to contact.html from the footer without a `topic` parameter; the form loads with an empty topic field.
2. After viewing details or submitting a request, the user uses the browser back button or global navigation to return to help.html or other pages.
3. Because no state is stored in localStorage, any future visit to contact.html will again be initialized only from URL parameters and fresh API calls.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Trigger: contact.html is loaded.
- Behavior: The page-level controller initializes components.
  - Calls getContactSupportInfo once and passes the response to contact-support-info-panel.
  - Reads the `topic` URL parameter and passes this initial value to contact-support-form.
  - Sets initial internal flags such as `isSubmitting = false` and `lastSubmissionResult = null` in the form component state.

Interaction: Topic prefill from URL
- Trigger: contact-support-form onInit.
- Behavior: The component reads the `topic` URL parameter (if present).
  - If present and non-empty, sets the form's topic field initial value to this parameter.
  - If missing or empty, leaves the topic field blank.
  - The user can override this value at any time via the form input controls.

Interaction: Editing form fields
- Trigger: User types into name, email, topic, subject, or message fields or changes any control related to those values.
- Behavior: The form's internal state updates on each change (onChange handlers).
  - The current values are kept in component state only; they are not written to localStorage or query parameters.
  - Any previously shown validation messages may be cleared or updated in real time.

Interaction: Submitting the form
- Trigger: User clicks the submit button or activates the form submission via keyboard (e.g., Enter).
- Behavior:
  - Run synchronous validation for required fields and format checks (e.g., email pattern) using only the in-memory form state.
  - If validation fails, show inline error messages and prevent calling submitContactSupportRequest.
  - If validation passes, set `isSubmitting = true`, disable the submit button, and hide prior status messages while keeping form values.
  - Call submitContactSupportRequest with the current form field values.

Interaction: Handling submitContactSupportRequest response
- Trigger: Promise resolved or callback returned from submitContactSupportRequest.
- Behavior:
  - Set `isSubmitting = false`.
  - If `success` is true:
    - Store the response object (success, message, ticketId) in the form component state.
    - Pass that state to contact-form-status-banner to display the success message and ticketId.
    - Optionally reset some or all form fields (e.g., clear subject and message while keeping topic), according to product rules.
  - If `success` is false:
    - Store the response object as an error state.
    - Pass that state to the status banner to display the error message.
    - Keep all form field values unchanged for user correction.

Interaction: Clicking email or phone in support info
- Trigger: User clicks the email address or phone number displayed in contact-support-info-panel.
- Behavior:
  - For email: triggers a standard mailto link using the runtime value of supportEmail.
  - For phone: triggers a standard tel link using the runtime value of supportPhone.
  - No additional API calls are made, and no local state is updated besides standard browser behavior.

Interaction: Dismissing the status banner
- Trigger: User clicks a close/dismiss control on contact-form-status-banner.
- Behavior:
  - The status banner hides itself.
  - Any stored `lastSubmissionResult` remains in component state (for potential analytics or logging), but is not persisted across reloads and is not written to URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameter usage and page state

- URL Parameter: topic (string)
  - Source: May be appended by help.html or other pages when linking to contact.html, e.g., contact.html?topic=<topic-value>.
  - Read Timing: Parsed on initial page load before initializing contact-support-form.
  - Behavior:
    - If `topic` is present and non-empty, it is used as the initial value of the form's topic field.
    - If `topic` is missing or empty, the topic field starts empty.
    - After the page is loaded, user changes to the topic field do not modify the URL; they are only maintained in in-memory state.
  - No automatic redirects or additional API calls are triggered solely by the presence of the `topic` parameter.

- Form State (in-memory only)
  - The form component maintains current values for name, email, topic, subject, message, `isSubmitting`, and `lastSubmissionResult` (including any ticketId) in its internal state.
  - This state is reset when the page is reloaded or navigated to again; it is not saved to localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies.

- API Response State
  - getContactSupportInfo response is stored in memory and bound to the contact-support-info-panel. If the user reloads the page, the data is fetched again.
  - submitContactSupportRequest response is stored in memory within the form component and exposed to the contact-form-status-banner. It is cleared when the component resets or the page is reloaded.

- Navigation
  - Access via footer navigation: navigating from any page to contact.html always triggers fresh state initialization and a new call to getContactSupportInfo.
  - Back/forward navigation is entirely handled by the browser; this design does not modify the history state beyond the initial URL with its `topic` parameter.

components.functionality: Fetches and displays current support contact information including email, phone, support hours, and mailing address using getContactSupportInfo.

Collects user input (name, email, topic, subject, message), optionally pre-filling the topic from the URL parameter, validates the data, and submits a support request through submitContactSupportRequest.
Displays the result of the most recent support request submission, including success or error state and any ticketId returned by submitContactSupportRequest.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column grid likely based on a 12-column system, with main content occupying approximately 8–9 columns and the right sidebar 3–4 columns.

layout.overall_description: The Contact page sits within a minimalist, corporate-feeling layout: a slim full-width header with a logo on the left, simple text navigation across the top center, and a lone search icon on the right, all on a white background with a subtle bottom divider line. Below this, the page splits into two columns. On the left, occupying roughly two-thirds of the width, the Contact heading appears near the top with minimal top margin. Directly beneath it, a slim status banner stretches across the width of this main column when there is a message to show, forming a neat bar of slightly tinted color or bordered emphasis. Below that, a clean support info panel presents phone, email, and support hours in a straightforward block with subtle gray borders and comfortable padding. Further down, the primary support form fills the remainder of the column, its fields stacked vertically with clear labels, full-width inputs, and generous vertical spacing, making the flow from one field to the next feel linear and deliberate. A consistent left alignment for all text and components reinforces the content-first feel. On the right, separated by a vertical gutter or light divider, the sidebar occupies the remaining third of the width, hosting auxiliary site content in a low-contrast manner so it doesn’t compete with the contact actions. On smaller screens, everything collapses into a single column: header at the top, then title, status banner, info panel, form, and finally any sidebar content, all following the same measured spacing system for a calm and utilitarian interface.

It spans nearly the full width of the left column (about 70% of the page on desktop), aligned flush with the left edge of the column and inset with standard page side margins. The panel itself is rendered as a simple rectangular block with a white background and either a subtle light gray border or a faint bottom divider, using 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. Inside, support contact details (e.g., label-value lines for phone, email, and support hours) are left-aligned and stacked vertically with modest 8–12px gaps. Labels may be slightly bolder or darker gray, while values remain regular weight, ensuring the block looks structured but not heavy. The panel’s height is determined by its content, generally occupying a moderate vertical slice—enough to clearly communicate all key contact methods without pushing the form too far below the fold. On smaller screens, this panel still appears above the form and spans 100% of the available width with the same internal padding and spacing, preserving its relative hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The info panel participates in the normal document flow: it scrolls with the page, does not stick or float, and always maintains its vertical position between the status banner (or title) above and the contact form below. It respects the column grid, maintaining consistent left and right margins in line with other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support form is the dominant component in the left main column and is placed directly below the contact-support-info-panel, separated by a clear 32–40px vertical gap to visually distinguish it as the primary interaction area. It extends across the full width of the left column (about 70% of the page on desktop), aligning perfectly with the left and right edges of the info panel above. Internally, the form uses 16–24px padding, mirroring other main content blocks. Each form field (e.g., subject, auction/lot reference, message, contact details) is arranged in a single vertical stack: label on top, then a full-width input or textarea beneath, with 12–16px vertical spacing between fields. Labels are left-aligned, small to medium in size, and use regular or slightly bold black/gray text; helper text, if present, appears just below labels or inputs in a lighter gray and smaller size. The submit button is positioned at the bottom-left of the form, aligned with the field labels, with 16–24px of top margin above it so it doesn’t appear crowded against the last input. The overall form block typically occupies most of the vertical space in the left column content area, extending well below the info panel, reinforcing its role as the primary task on the page. On smaller screens, the form simply fills 100% width, with the same vertical field order and spacing, preserving readability and touch-friendly tap targets.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The form is in standard flow, scrolling naturally with the rest of the page. It is not fixed or sticky and adapts to the height of its content. On responsive breakpoints, it reflows to occupy the full available width while maintaining consistent padding and vertical spacing between its fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status banner appears in the left main column as a slim, horizontally stretched bar directly beneath the Contact page title and above the contact-support-info-panel. When visible, it spans the full width of the left column, aligning exactly with the left and right edges of the info panel and form components below. Vertically, it is relatively compact, with about 12–16px of padding above and below the status text, making it noticeable but not bulky. The banner background uses a very light color tint or soft gray fill distinct from the white page background, with optional thin border or subtle drop shadow, ensuring it stands out as feedback without breaking the minimalist aesthetic. The status message text is left-aligned, using regular body size, with maybe a slightly stronger weight or darker color for key words (e.g., “Success,” “Error,” “Sent”). There is approximately 16–24px of space between the bottom of the banner and the top of the info panel, keeping it visually separated as a transient feedback element. If the banner is not present, the info panel moves up to sit directly under the page title, preserving the vertical rhythm. On small screens, the banner retains its position just under the title and above the info panel, widening to 100% of the viewport width within the content column margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The status banner appears and disappears within the normal document flow, pushing the info panel down when present but not overlaying or floating above other elements. It scrolls with the page and does not remain fixed at the top of the viewport. Its height is content-dependent but remains visually slim to preserve the minimalist feel.

name: Terms of Use
title: Terms of Use
description: Displays the current Terms of Use for the livestock auction website by loading legal content from the server, including version, last-updated date, and structured sections with in-page navigation.

core_features: Load the Terms of Use metadata and section content from the server using getTermsOfUseContent when the page is initialized.
Display the Terms of Use version and last updated date provided by getTermsOfUseContent.
Render all Terms of Use sections in order, using the returned headings and bodies, without hardcoded text.
Provide an automatically generated table of contents based on section headings, allowing users to jump to a specific section.
Support deep-linking to a specific Terms of Use section using URL parameters, and automatically scroll to and highlight that section.
Update the URL parameters when the user clicks a section link so the current section can be bookmarked or shared.
Handle page reloads or direct navigation with URL parameters by restoring the same section focus and scroll position based on the parameters.
user_workflows: Viewing the Terms of Use from the footer link: 1) User clicks the 'Terms of Use' link in the global footer (navigates to terms.html with no additional URL parameters). 2) On page load, the terms-content-loader component calls getTermsOfUseContent. 3) After the data is returned, the terms-header component displays the version and last updated date from the response. 4) The terms-toc component builds a list of section links using the returned headings. 5) The terms-section-list component renders all sections in order using the returned headings and bodies. 6) User scrolls the page to read the Terms of Use.

Navigating directly to a specific section via URL: 1) User opens a link such as terms.html?sectionIndex={n} or terms.html?sectionHeading={encodedHeading}. 2) On page load, the url-state-sync component parses the URL parameters and sets the selected section state. 3) The terms-content-loader component calls getTermsOfUseContent and stores the response. 4) After content is loaded, the terms-section-list component uses the selected section state to scroll into view the matching section (by index or by matching the decoded heading to a returned heading) and visually indicate it as active. 5) The terms-toc component highlights the same section entry to reflect which section is currently focused.

Using the table of contents for in-page navigation: 1) User is viewing terms.html with content loaded. 2) User clicks a section link in the terms-toc component. 3) The terms-toc component triggers an onClickSectionLink handler that updates the URL parameter (for example, sectionIndex or sectionHeading) via the browser history API without reloading the page. 4) The url-state-sync component detects the URL change, updates the selected section state, and instructs terms-section-list to scroll to the corresponding section. 5) The clicked section is highlighted in both the table of contents and the main section list.

Sharing or bookmarking a specific Terms of Use section: 1) User navigates to a section using the table of contents (which sets section-related URL parameters). 2) The browser address bar now contains terms.html with sectionIndex or sectionHeading indicating the current section. 3) User copies the URL or bookmarks the page. 4) Another user (or the same user later) opens this URL. 5) On load, url-state-sync reads the parameters and focuses the same section after getTermsOfUseContent has loaded.

Returning to the top of the Terms of Use: 1) User has scrolled down or navigated to a deep section. 2) User clicks a 'Back to top' link rendered by the back-to-top-link component. 3) The component scrolls the page to the top and optionally removes section-related URL parameters via the history API. 4) The url-state-sync component updates the selected section state to indicate that no specific section is selected.
interactions: On page initialization, the terms-content-loader component automatically calls getTermsOfUseContent with no parameters and waits for the response before rendering version information and sections.

Clicking any section link in the terms-toc component triggers an event handler that updates the URL (adding or updating a sectionIndex or sectionHeading parameter) and scrolls the associated section into view.

When the browser URL changes (for example, due to back/forward navigation or internal link clicks), the url-state-sync component detects the change and updates the selected section state without reloading the page.

If the user reloads the page while a section parameter is present in the URL, the same parameter is read again on load, and after getTermsOfUseContent completes, the terms-section-list component scrolls to the corresponding section.
Clicking a 'Back to top' control triggers a scroll-to-top behavior and may remove section-related URL parameters to reset focus to the top of the Terms of Use.

If the structure of the sections changes in the data returned by getTermsOfUseContent (for example, section order or headings), the table of contents and section list automatically reflect those changes because they are generated entirely from the current response.
page_functionality.state_logic: Page state is driven by the URL and the response from getTermsOfUseContent. On load, url-state-sync parses query parameters from window.location.search. Supported parameters include a numeric sectionIndex and/or a text sectionHeading. The sectionIndex parameter represents the zero-based position of a section in the sections array returned by getTermsOfUseContent; if present and within bounds, it is preferred for determining the selected section. The sectionHeading parameter represents an encoded heading string; if sectionIndex is not present or invalid, url-state-sync searches the sections array for a heading whose encoded value matches sectionHeading and uses its index. These parameters are not sent to getTermsOfUseContent, because the interface accepts no parameters; instead, they are used solely to control client-side focus, highlighting, and scrolling after the full Terms of Use content has been loaded. When a user clicks a section in the table of contents, the sectionIndex and/or sectionHeading in the URL is updated via the History API (pushState or replaceState), and the selected section state is updated accordingly. When the user uses the browser back/forward controls, the popstate event causes url-state-sync to re-parse the current URL and adjust the selected section so the view stays in sync with the URL. If no section-related parameters are present, the default state is that no specific section is selected and the page shows the top of the Terms of Use. All text content, headings, and metadata displayed are taken directly from the latest getTermsOfUseContent response, with no hardcoded or static content.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by calling getTermsOfUseContent to retrieve the Terms of Use version, lastUpdated value, and sections array, then exposes this data to other components as shared state.
Displays the static page title 'Terms of Use' along with the dynamic version and last updated information coming from the getTermsOfUseContent response.
Generates a table of contents from the TermsOfUseContent.sections array, listing each section heading as a clickable link that updates URL parameters and scrolls to the corresponding section.

Renders each Terms of Use section in sequence, using the heading and body values from the sections array, and applies highlighting or focus to the section indicated by the current URL-driven selectedSectionIndex.

Synchronizes the selected section state with the browser URL by parsing sectionIndex and sectionHeading query parameters on load and during navigation events, and by updating these parameters when section navigation occurs inside the page.
Provides a control to return to the top of the Terms of Use page, optionally clearing section-related URL parameters to reset the focus state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column grid likely based on a 12-column system, with main content occupying approximately 8–9 columns and the right sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the global slim header bar, the page immediately splits into a two-column layout: a wider main content column on the left for the Terms of Use title and legal text, and a narrower sidebar on the right for in-page navigation via a table of contents. Within the left column, content flows linearly from the terms header (title, version, last updated) directly into the stacked terms sections. The right column maintains a simple vertical list of section links, visually mirroring the auction-lot list behavior from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the global slim header bar, the page immediately splits into a two-column layout: a wider main content column on the left for the Terms of Use title and legal text, and a narrower sidebar on the right for in-page navigation via a table of contents. Within the left column, content flows linearly from the terms header (title, version, last updated) directly into the stacked terms sections. The right column maintains a simple vertical list of section links, visually mirroring the auction-lot list behavior from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column occupies roughly 65–70% of the usable page width, aligned to the left, while the right sidebar occupies the remaining 30–35%, separated by a subtle vertical divider or sufficient whitespace. The terms header sits close to the top of the main content area with about 32–40px space below the global header. The table of contents in the sidebar begins near the top alignment of the terms header but with slightly more top padding to keep it visually secondary. Vertical gaps of 32–48px separate major blocks (header vs sections, sections vs footer/back-to-top). Internal padding within blocks is about 16–24px for the main column and 12–16px for sidebar elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: Typography is set for comfortable reading: body text with generous line height and clear paragraph spacing, leading to moderately low density despite long-form legal content. Sections within the terms are clearly separated with 24–32px gaps between them and headings that stand out via weight/size rather than decorative styling. The table of contents links are single-line items with about 8–12px vertical spacing, forming a clean, easy-to-scan list without feeling sparse. Overall, the density feels functional and text-first, consistent with a minimalist corporate theme.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye lands first on the 'Terms of Use' header at the top-left of the content area, then moves down through the version and last-updated metadata into the first terms section. After establishing the main content, the eye is gently drawn to the right by the vertical divider/whitespace into the sidebar table of contents, where sections are scanned top-to-bottom. As users click TOC links, the main content scrolls, reinforcing a left-to-right, then top-to-bottom reading pattern. At the bottom of the terms, the back-to-top link appears as a small, right-aligned utility element, offering a clear end to the reading flow.

layout.overall_description: Under the full-width, slim global header, the Terms of Use page presents a clean two-column layout on a white background. The left column, taking roughly two-thirds of the width, starts with a compact yet prominent 'Terms of Use' header section that includes the page title, legal version, and last-updated date, all aligned to the left with comfortable padding. Directly beneath that, the main legal text flows vertically in clearly separated sections, each with a bold heading and well-spaced paragraphs, forming a long, scrollable column of content. To the right, separated by a light gray vertical rule or a clear gutter, a narrower sidebar column contains a simple table of contents: a short heading and a vertical list of plain-text section links spaced for readability, visually echoing the auction-lot list from the mockup. Invisible data and URL sync components operate behind the scenes, without disrupting the layout. At the very end of the main content column, a small, right-aligned 'Back to top' text link provides a quiet navigational affordance. The overall effect is that of a restrained, corporate-style legal page: text-first, minimalist, and easy to scan, with a clear primary focus on the left-hand terms content and a supportive, navigational sidebar on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-content-loader component operates entirely in the background; it does not render any visible UI or occupy layout space. Conceptually, it is attached to the main content area that spans both columns, initializing before the terms header and terms sections are drawn. It can be thought of as running at the top of the content stack beneath the global header, but it neither shifts nor reserves any pixels in the layout—its activity only affects what text appears inside the terms-header and terms-section-list components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual background process; occupies no physical space and does not affect box dimensions. It simply feeds data into visual components that sit in the left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-header is positioned at the very top of the main content column on the left, directly below the global site header. It is left-aligned with the main column’s grid, spanning approximately 65–70% of the total page width and using the full width of that column. There is about 32–40px of vertical space between the bottom edge of the global header bar and the top of the terms-header, matching the mockup’s modest but clear section separation. Inside the terms-header, the 'Terms of Use' title appears at the upper left with strong typographic emphasis, followed by the version label and last-updated date stacked beneath or alongside in a smaller, lighter style, maintaining a compact vertical grouping. The block has internal padding of approximately 16–24px on all sides, and it ends with a clear bottom margin of 24–32px before the first terms section begins, ensuring the header feels visually distinct but not overly isolated.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, scrolling away naturally as the user moves down the page. It anchors the start of the main content flow but does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-toc component sits in the right-hand sidebar column, aligned to the top of the main content region but slightly offset downward so it feels subordinate to the primary terms-header. Horizontally, it begins flush with the right column’s left edge and spans nearly the full width of the sidebar (about 30–35% of the page width). The left and right edges of the sidebar are visually separated from the main column via either a 1px light gray vertical divider at the boundary or a gutter of around 24px, mirroring the structural separation from the mockup. At the top of the terms-toc, a small heading such as 'On this page' appears, followed by a vertically stacked list of section links. Each link occupies a single line and is separated from the next by about 8–12px of vertical space, creating a clean, legible list reminiscent of the auction-lot list in the original design. The overall block uses around 12–16px of internal padding and has ample whitespace above the heading—approximately 24–32px from the top edge of the sidebar—to avoid feeling crowded. As the user scrolls, the sidebar simply moves with the page; the table of contents remains a static, right-aligned navigation column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar element within the right column. It scrolls with the page content and does not fix or stick to the viewport, preserving the straightforward blog-style layout from the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-section-list occupies the main content column directly beneath the terms-header, aligned to the same left margin and sharing its 65–70% page-width footprint. It forms a long, vertical stack of individual terms sections that extends downward and will typically require scrolling. Each section begins with a section heading (e.g., '1. Acceptance of Terms') set slightly larger or bolder than the body text, followed by one or more paragraphs of legal text. Section headings have a top margin of roughly 24–32px from the previous section’s content, creating clear visual separation, while the paragraphs within a section use comfortable line spacing and 8–12px spacing between paragraphs to maintain readability. Left and right padding of 16–24px match the terms-header, creating a unified column block. The right edge of this content sits a short distance (around 24px) from the vertical divider/gutter that separates it from the sidebar, reinforcing the two-column structure and guiding the reader’s eye down the main column before glancing right to the table of contents.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrollable content within the left column. It defines the primary vertical reading flow; sections can be navigated via in-page anchors linked from the table of contents and URL state, but the component itself scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component is invisible and layered logically over the entire scrollable content area rather than occupying a specific visible position. Conceptually, it is associated with both the terms-section-list (for tracking which section is in view) and the terms-toc (for highlighting the active link and updating the URL hash). It does not render any box, line, or text and has zero width and height, so it does not alter the spatial relationships between the left content column and the right sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual state listener bound to scroll position and link interactions. It does not affect layout flow; it only updates URL state and triggers visual states (such as active TOC highlighting) within existing components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-top-link appears as a small, unobtrusive text link located at the very bottom of the main content column, immediately after the final terms section. It is right-aligned within the left column, sitting a few pixels (about 8–12px) above the bottom margin of the content block, and indented from the column’s right padding edge by the same 16–24px used elsewhere, so it visually hugs the lower-right corner of the text area. There is a clear vertical gap of around 24–32px between the last paragraph of legal text and this link, signaling that the main content has ended and this is a utility control. The link uses the same minimalist typographic style as inline links in the text (e.g., an underlined or slightly colored word) without any button chrome, in keeping with the restrained, corporate aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element positioned at the end of the left column’s content flow. It scrolls into view only when the user reaches the bottom of the terms-section-list and does not remain fixed to the viewport.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Displays the current privacy policy loaded from the backend, with section-based navigation and deep linking using URL parameters.

core_features: Load the latest privacy policy content (version, last updated timestamp, sections) using the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface on page load.
Render all privacy policy sections returned by the interface, showing each section’s heading and body in a readable, scrollable layout.
Generate a dynamic table of contents (TOC) from the returned section headings to allow quick navigation to any section.
Support deep linking to a specific section via a URL parameter so users can open the page directly at a given section.
Support a print-friendly mode controlled by URL parameters (e.g., expanding all sections and optionally hiding navigation elements) to make printing easier.
user_workflows:
View the entire privacy policy:
1. User navigates to privacy.html (for example, via the footer link).
2. The page controller calls getPrivacyPolicyContent with no parameters.
3. The interface returns an object containing version, lastUpdated, and an array of sections.
4. The header component displays the page title along with the returned version and lastUpdated values.
5. The TOC component generates a list of links from the returned sections’ headings.
6. The section list component renders each section heading and body in order.
7. User scrolls through the page to read the full policy.

Navigate within the policy using the table of contents:
1. After the policy content is loaded, the TOC is populated with links for each section.
2. User clicks a section link in the TOC.
3. The click handler updates the URL’s section parameter to the identifier derived from the clicked section heading.
4. The page controller updates its internal state to mark that section as active.
5. The section list component scrolls to the corresponding section and optionally highlights it as active.
6. If the user uses the browser back/forward buttons, the controller re-reads the section parameter and re-activates the corresponding section.

Open the page directly at a specific section (deep link):
1. User receives or enters a URL such as privacy.html?section={sectionIdentifier}.
2. When the page loads, the controller parses the section parameter from the URL.
3. The controller calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
4. Once sections are loaded, the controller looks for a section whose derived identifier matches the URL parameter value.
5. The matching section is scrolled into view and optionally highlighted as active.
6. The TOC reflects the active section state so the user can see where they are in the policy.

Use print-friendly mode:
1. User navigates to privacy.html and chooses to print (for example by clicking a print control on the page, or by using a URL parameter that enables print mode).
2. If the user clicks a print control, the page updates the view parameter in the URL to a value representing print mode and updates the internal view state.
3. In print mode, the controller ensures all sections are expanded and non-essential navigation (such as the TOC) can be hidden from print if desired.
4. The component responsible for printing may trigger the browser’s print dialog or instruct the user to use their browser’s print function.
5. After printing, the user can return to normal mode by changing the URL (e.g., removing the view parameter) or using a control that resets it.
interactions: On page load, the controller parses URL parameters (such as section and view) and calls getPrivacyPolicyContent to retrieve the privacy policy data.
Clicking a TOC entry updates the URL section parameter, updates the active section state in the controller, and scrolls the corresponding section into view.

Clicking on a section heading (if collapsible behavior is implemented) toggles the expanded/collapsed state for that section, and may update a URL parameter (for example, expanded) to reflect whether all sections should be expanded.
Clicking a print control (if provided) updates the view parameter in the URL to indicate print mode and may trigger the browser’s print dialog.

Using browser back/forward buttons triggers a re-evaluation of the URL parameters; the controller reacts by updating the active section and view mode without re-calling getPrivacyPolicyContent unless the page is reloaded.

External navigation (such as clicking the footer link to the privacy page from another page) loads privacy.html without parameters or with externally defined parameters; the page then behaves according to those parameters when initializing state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for view state, in addition to the policy content retrieved from getPrivacyPolicyContent.

Handled URL parameters (no localStorage is used):
1) section: Optional. Represents the identifier of the section that should be active on load. The identifier is derived from each section heading returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent (for example, by applying a deterministic transformation such as lowercasing, trimming, replacing whitespace with hyphens, and removing or normalizing non-alphanumeric characters). On initial load, after getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves, the controller searches the sections array for a section whose derived identifier matches the section parameter value:
   - If a match is found, that section is scrolled into view and marked as the active section in component state.
   - If no match is found or the parameter is absent, the page shows the default view (e.g., the top of the document) without any special highlighting.
   - When a user clicks a TOC item, the section parameter in the URL is updated to the clicked section’s identifier using the same derivation logic, and the active section state is updated accordingly.

2) view: Optional. Controls the overall rendering mode of the policy page:
   - If view has a value representing a print-focused layout, the controller puts the page into print mode (e.g., all sections expanded, optional hiding of non-essential interactive elements from print output). The same policy content returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent is used; only layout behavior changes.
   - If view is absent or has a different value, the page renders in normal mode.
   - When a user clicks a print control, the click handler updates the view parameter to indicate print mode so that reloading or sharing the URL preserves the print-friendly layout.

3) expanded: Optional. Controls collapsed/expanded state of sections as a whole (if collapsible functionality is in use):
   - If expanded is set to a value representing all sections expanded, the section list component renders all sections in an expanded state regardless of previous interaction.
   - If expanded is set to a value representing all sections collapsed, the section list component initializes with all sections collapsed.
   - If expanded is absent, section expansion state follows the page’s default behavior (for example, all expanded in normal mode) and individual user interactions (e.g., clicking headings) are kept in in-memory state and are reset upon full page reload.

URL parameters are read on every full page load and whenever the browser navigation changes the query string (if that is observed). No policy content is stored or synchronized via localStorage; all content comes from getPrivacyPolicyContent and current state comes from URL parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading of privacy policy data and manages page-level state based on URL parameters and user interactions. On initialization, it parses URL parameters (such as section, view, and expanded), calls getPrivacyPolicyContent to fetch the policy object, and then passes the resulting data and derived state (active section identifier, current view mode, initial expansion behavior) to child components. It also listens for changes triggered by user actions (e.g., TOC clicks, print mode toggles) and updates the URL parameters to keep the state in sync.

Displays the page title and key metadata about the privacy policy, including the version and lastUpdated values returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent. It does not fetch data itself; it receives the policy metadata from the page controller and renders it so users can see which version of the policy they are viewing and when it was last updated.

Renders a dynamic table of contents derived from the sections array returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent. For each section, it creates an item using the section heading and a derived identifier. When a user clicks an item, it notifies the page controller to update the section URL parameter and to set the corresponding section as active, and it may trigger a scroll to that section in the section list component.

Iterates over the sections array returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent and renders a sequence of section items. It receives the current ActiveSectionIdentifier, ViewMode, and SectionExpansionState from the controller to determine which section, if any, should be highlighted as active, and whether sections should be rendered expanded or collapsed. It also exposes hooks for scrolling a given section into view when notified by the controller (e.g., in response to TOC clicks or URL parameter changes).

Represents a single section of the privacy policy. It receives a section object (with heading and body), its derived identifier, the current ActiveSectionIdentifier, the current ViewMode, and a flag or configuration indicating whether it should start expanded. It renders the section heading and body, applies active styling if its identifier matches ActiveSectionIdentifier, and optionally allows the user to toggle the section’s expanded/collapsed state. When the heading is clicked (for toggling), it notifies the controller so that SectionExpansionState can be updated and reflected in the URL if necessary.

Provides user controls related to printing or switching the view mode. It receives the current ViewMode from the controller and can render controls such as a button to enter or exit print-friendly mode. When activated, it signals the controller to update the view parameter in the URL and to adjust the ViewMode state. The component can also trigger the browser’s print dialog according to the application’s behavior, but it does not modify or store any policy content itself.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column grid likely based on a 12-column system, with main content occupying approximately 8–9 columns and the right sidebar 3–4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same classic blog-style layout as the mockup: a slim global header (outside this scope), then a two-column content area controlled by the page-controller. The privacy policy title and the policy body (sections) occupy the main left column, while the table of contents sits in a right-hand sidebar column. The flow is strictly top-to-bottom in each column: in the left column, policy header at the top, then print controls just beneath or aligned with it, followed by the stacked list of policy sections. In the right column, the table of contents block sits near the top and lists section links vertically. All components read as simple, stacked text modules with minimal ornamentation.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same classic blog-style layout as the mockup: a slim global header (outside this scope), then a two-column content area controlled by the page-controller. The privacy policy title and the policy body (sections) occupy the main left column, while the table of contents sits in a right-hand sidebar column. The flow is strictly top-to-bottom in each column: in the left column, policy header at the top, then print controls just beneath or aligned with it, followed by the stacked list of policy sections. In the right column, the table of contents block sits near the top and lists section links vertically. All components read as simple, stacked text modules with minimal ornamentation.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page-controller establishes a two-column grid where the main content column occupies about 65–70% of the available width and the right sidebar about 30–35%, matching the auction layout. The policy header and print controls sit at the top of the main column. Below that, the section list fills the rest of the left column vertically. The TOC navigation in the right sidebar starts slightly lower than the top of the left column, with a comfortable top margin, then extends downward as a vertical list. Vertical gaps between major blocks (header, print controls, sections, TOC) follow the 32–48px spacing for clear separation.
layout_strategies.content_density: Text uses a moderately low density: generous line-height and 16–24px vertical spacing between paragraphs and list items. Section headings stand out with slightly larger font size and 16–24px spacing above and below. The TOC items are single-line (or two-line at most) entries with about 8–12px vertical separation, echoing the auction lot list. Padding within each major block (main content panel, sidebar TOC) is around 16–24px, keeping the page readable and uncluttered while still feeling information-focused and corporate.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s pattern. After the global site header, the eye first lands on the privacy policy title in the top-left of the content area. From there it moves downward through the print controls and then the successive policy sections. Once the user scans down the left column, the vertical divider and whitespace draw attention to the right sidebar, where the table of contents is scanned top-to-bottom. Deep linking reinforces this: clicking a TOC item moves the viewport to the corresponding section in the left column, then the user resumes a top-to-bottom reading pattern. Overall flow is left-to-right, then top-to-bottom, with no alternating blocks or complex layouts.

layout.overall_description: The Privacy Policy page sits within the same minimalist, two-column shell as the auction listing pages. Below the site’s global header, the content area is divided into a wide left column and a narrower right sidebar. In the left column, the Privacy Policy title appears at the top-left, with a clean, content-first presentation: bold text on a white background, minimal lines, and generous padding. Just beneath and right-aligned to this title row sits a slim action bar for print-related controls. Below that, the policy itself unfolds as a vertical stack of clearly separated sections, each with a heading and body text, spanning the full width of the main column. To the right, separated by a subtle vertical divider or clear white gutter, the sidebar begins slightly lower, with the table of contents presented as a simple vertical text list under a small heading. Each TOC entry aligns neatly left, echoing the look of auction lot links. The whole page feels like a corporate document rendered in a blog-style layout: structured, easy to scan, with text as the hero and just enough spacing and alignment to keep everything legible and orderly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller occupies the full browser width beneath the global site header and establishes the two-column grid. It centers the main content within a max-width container (e.g., around 1040–1200px), with equal horizontal margins on very wide screens. Inside this container, it divides the space into a main content column on the left (approximately 65–70% of the width) and a sidebar column on the right (approximately 30–35%), separated by a 24–32px gutter. The page-controller also defines vertical rhythm: about 32–48px of space between the site header and the top of the policy content, between the policy header and section list, and between main content and footer. The background is solid white with no boxes around the columns; instead, any separation is achieved with a light vertical rule or simply the white gutter. All other components (policy header, print controls, section list in the left column, and TOC in the right column) are positioned relative to this grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a static layout container that defines the two-column responsive grid; on smaller screens it can collapse to a single column with TOC stacking below the main content while maintaining the same vertical spacing rules.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy header is placed at the top of the left column, directly under the vertical offset from the global header established by the page-controller. It spans the full width of the left column, with 16–24px internal padding on the left and right edges to align the text nicely with the body paragraphs below. The title text (“Privacy Policy”) is left-aligned and visually dominant, sitting about 16–24px above the print controls/action bar. There may be a small, secondary line under the main title (e.g., last updated date) rendered in smaller, lighter text, with about 8–12px spacing beneath the title. The header block itself keeps a low profile—no colored background, just text on white with perhaps a thin light-gray divider line below to separate it from the body. Vertically, the block consumes a modest band at the top, aligning conceptually with how ‘Auction Lot 7’ sits at the top of the auction layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored to the top of the main content column; it scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The table of contents occupies the top portion of the right sidebar column, starting roughly aligned with the vertical midpoint of the policy header in the left column, creating a slight visual offset downward and an airy feel. It sits inside the sidebar with 12–16px padding on all sides, leaving a clean margin from the sidebar’s left boundary and the page edge. At the top of this TOC block is a small heading (e.g., “On this page” or “Sections”), left-aligned and separated from the first TOC item by about 8–12px. Below, the individual TOC entries are listed as a simple vertical list of text links, each left-aligned, occupying nearly the full width of the sidebar (minus padding). Each item has about 8–12px vertical spacing from the next, similar to the auction lot links in the mockup, ensuring the list is readable but not sparse. The block extends downward as needed to accommodate all section links. A subtle light-gray top border or a bit of extra top margin can visually separate this TOC from any sidebar content above (if present) while otherwise remaining visually quiet.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the sidebar; on taller viewports it scrolls with the page, though it could optionally become sticky within the sidebar once scrolled into view, keeping its position relative to the viewport while staying within the sidebar’s width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section list sits directly beneath the policy header (and directly adjacent to the print controls), occupying the full width of the left column from left padding to right padding. The top of the section list begins roughly 24–32px below the bottom of the header block, creating a clear but modest separation. Within this container, individual section items are stacked vertically in a single column, each separated by about 24–32px of vertical space to echo the paragraph spacing of the mockup and to keep sections visually distinct. The list itself does not appear as a card; it’s simply a flow of text sections against the white page background, following the blog-style article feel. Any deep-linking anchors are positioned so that when a user clicks a TOC link, the corresponding section’s heading appears near the top of the viewport with a bit of breathing room above (around 16–24px of visible whitespace).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that scrolls normally, carrying all section-items. It defines the vertical stacking order but does not impose additional borders or backgrounds beyond basic spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each section item is a content block within the main column’s text flow. It spans the full width of the left column’s content area, aligning its heading and body text with the policy header’s left edge. At the top of each section, the section title is set in a slightly larger, bold font, left-aligned, with about 8–12px spacing below it before the body text begins. The body text is comprised of paragraphs and possibly bullet lists, each paragraph separated by about 8–16px to maintain readability. The entire section item has an implicit top margin of around 24–32px from the previous section, creating a clear visual break without any lines or boxes. There is no special background; the separation is created purely through spacing and typography, mirroring the way different descriptive blocks are separated in the auction lot text. If there are subheadings within a section, they follow the same pattern at a slightly smaller hierarchy level, with consistent vertical spacing above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully static text block that flows with the page scroll; its position in the document is the anchor target for deep links from the TOC.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The print controls appear as a slim action bar directly beneath or aligned with the bottom edge of the policy header in the left column. Horizontally, the controls are right-aligned within the same width as the policy header, creating a subtle visual tension between the left-aligned title and right-aligned actions. The action bar’s vertical height is modest—around 32–40px—with 8–12px of padding above and below the buttons or links. Typical actions might be text buttons such as “Print” or “Download PDF,” spaced 8–16px apart horizontally. There is no heavy background; at most, a faint light-gray underline or thin dividing line separates this action row from the content below. A vertical gap of about 16–24px follows the action bar before the first section item begins, ensuring the actions feel connected to the title but distinct from the main body text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: By default, static within the main column, scrolling away as the user reads. Optionally, on long policies, this bar can become a lightweight sticky element within the left column—locking to the top edge of the main column’s viewport area while the user scrolls through sections, without extending beyond the column’s width.
